title: A Single Rewrite Suffices: Empirical Lessons from Production Skill Description Optimization
summary: Enterprise AI agents route user queries to specialized skills by matching queries against natural language skill descriptions. When two skills share overlapping descriptions, the routing LLM misroutes queries, a failure we term skill collision. As agents scale to dozens of skills, manually tuning descriptions to maintain routing accuracy becomes a significant engineering bottleneck. We deploy an automated description optimization pipeline on a production enterprise group chat agent (9 skills, 372 regression cases). The pipeline produces descriptions averaging 79.2% F1, matching manually tuned descriptions at 79.4% F1 (average per-skill difference -0.20%, within the 0.78% multi-seed noise floor), while reducing per-skill engineering effort from 120 minutes to 3.8 minutes (32 times speedup). We then examine which pipeline components actually drive this match. Systematic ablation on both the production system and ToolBench (16k tools) reveals that a single LLM rewrite using any available false-positive and false-negative cases captures most of the available improvement. Other design choices we tested (iteration budget, feedback signal composition, dual editing of confused pairs, and training set size) each affect final F1 by less than 0.5%. Description optimization addresses skill collisions caused by overlapping descriptions but cannot resolve cases where two skills intended scopes genuinely overlap. We identify a diagnostic (a large train-validation F1 gap) that flags the latter cases for architectural rather than text-level intervention.

AI.tldr: They propose and evaluate an automatic way to rewrite natural language descriptions of AI agent skills so that an LLM router chooses the right skill more reliably, matching human-tuned performance while greatly reducing engineering time.
AI.motivation: Enterprise AI agents rely on natural language skill descriptions so an LLM can route user queries to the correct skill. When descriptions overlap, the LLM gets confused, causing skill collisions and misrouting. As the number of skills grows, manually tweaking descriptions to avoid collisions is labor-intensive and does not scale. There is a need for an automated, systematic way to optimize these descriptions while preserving or improving routing accuracy.
AI.method: They build an automated description optimization pipeline for an LLM-based skill router. The pipeline uses regression cases (labeled routing examples) and a large language model to rewrite each skill’s description, using information from false-positive and false-negative cases to better separate skills. They deploy and test this pipeline on a real enterprise group chat agent with 9 skills and 372 regression cases, and also run systematic ablations both on this production setting and on the ToolBench benchmark with 16k tools. The ablations vary components such as the number of optimization iterations, the kind of feedback signals used, whether to edit confused skill pairs jointly, and the size of the labeled training set, to see which parts meaningfully affect performance.
AI.result: On the production agent, the automated pipeline generates descriptions with an average F1 of 79.2%, essentially matching manually tuned descriptions at 79.4% F1; the per-skill average difference is -0.20%, which is within a 0.78% multi-seed noise floor. The pipeline cuts engineering effort from about 120 minutes of manual tuning per skill to 3.8 minutes, a 32× speedup. Ablation studies show that a single LLM-based rewrite informed by both false-positive and false-negative examples captures most of the achievable improvement, while other design choices (iteration budget, feedback composition, dual editing of confused pairs, and training set size) each change final F1 by less than 0.5%.
AI.conclusion: Automatic LLM-based rewriting of skill descriptions can effectively resolve skill collisions caused by overlapping natural language descriptions, achieving human-level routing performance while drastically reducing engineering time. The key contributor is a single informed rewrite using misrouted examples; additional pipeline complexity offers only marginal gains. However, when two skills’ intended scopes fundamentally overlap, description optimization alone cannot fix routing errors. In those cases, a large gap between training and validation F1 serves as a useful diagnostic, indicating that the problem requires architectural changes (e.g., redefining or restructuring skills) rather than further text-level description tuning.

title: Indi-RomCoM: Code-Mixed Benchmark for Evaluating LLMs on Romanized Indic-English Instructions
summary: Romanized Code Mixing (RCM), where bilingual speakers fluidly blend local languages with English in Roman script, has emerged as the dominant form of communication across multilingual communities. While Large Language Models (LLMs) perform strongly on monolingual and native-script benchmarks, their ability to follow instructions and reason over RCM-based content remains largely unexplored. To this end, we introduce the Indi-RomCoM benchmark for facilitating systematic evaluation on Indic Romanized Code-Mixed instructions. Our benchmark spans seven instruction-following tasks, four widely spoken Indic languages, and three controlled code-mixing intensity levels. We extensively evaluate a suite of LLMs covering proprietary, open-weight, and Indic-focused models under zero- and few-shot settings. LLMs consistently underperform on RCM instructions, with performance degrading as code-mixing density increases. Furthermore, reasoning tasks suffer less degradation than detection tasks (e.g., Toxicity) because the generated explanations offer necessary context. We believe Indi-RomCoM helps the community in developing inclusive multilingual systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Indi-RomCoM, a benchmark to evaluate how well LLMs follow instructions and reason when faced with Indic Romanized code-mixed text, and finds that current models struggle, especially as code-mixing increases.
AI.motivation: Romanized Code Mixing (RCM) is now a dominant real-world communication style in many multilingual communities, particularly where local Indic languages are mixed with English but written using the Roman script. Despite strong performance on standard monolingual and native-script benchmarks, it is unclear how well LLMs handle such mixed, Romanized inputs. There is a need for a systematic way to evaluate LLMs on RCM to understand their limitations and guide improvements toward more inclusive multilingual systems.
AI.method: The authors construct Indi-RomCoM, a benchmark consisting of Indic Romanized code-mixed instructions. It covers seven instruction-following task types, four widely spoken Indic languages, and three controlled levels of code-mixing intensity. They then evaluate a diverse set of LLMs, including proprietary, open-weight, and Indic-specialized models, under both zero-shot and few-shot prompting conditions, comparing performance across tasks, languages, and code-mixing densities.
AI.result: Across evaluated models, performance on RCM instructions is consistently worse than on standard inputs, and it degrades further as the level of code-mixing increases. Among task types, reasoning-oriented tasks show smaller performance drops, whereas detection tasks such as toxicity identification degrade more, indicating particular difficulty in shallow classification without explicit explanatory context. Few-shot examples and explanation generation can partially mitigate these issues but do not close the gap.
AI.conclusion: Indi-RomCoM reveals that current LLMs are not robust to Indic Romanized code-mixed instructions, especially at higher mixing densities and for detection tasks. The benchmark provides a structured way to quantify these weaknesses, and the authors argue it can drive the development and evaluation of more inclusive, robust multilingual and code-mixed language technologies.

title: Using AI Agents to Automate Black-Box Audits of Personalization Algorithms at Scale
summary: Personalization algorithms determine what content users encounter on online platforms. Auditing these systems is difficult because independent auditors have only black-box access to the algorithms, while personalization depends on users' attributes, behavior, and evolving interaction histories. Existing auditing methods face a tradeoff: studies with real users capture realistic behavior but are costly and hard to control, whereas sock-puppet audits scale more easily but often rely on scripted behavior that limits realism. Beyond this, both approaches struggle to decouple user attributes from user behavior, limiting our ability to causally understand personalization. To address this gap, we introduce a framework for black-box audits of personalization algorithms using generative AI agents as behavioral engines for synthetic accounts. Each agent is instantiated with a fixed persona, grounded in demographic and political survey data, and interacts with a platform's content by reasoning about it and choosing actions. Because behavior is fixed within each persona while platform-visible signals such as age, gender, or location can be experimentally perturbed, our design enables counterfactual auditing of how platforms respond to user attributes. As a case study, we deploy 1,120 agents on X shortly after the 2024 U.S. election, spanning 14 personas and three counterfactual conditions, collecting over 200,000 content exposures. We find that X's algorithmic feed amplifies toxic, polarizing, political, and right-leaning content relative to the chronological feed, with amplification varying sharply by user ideology. Counterfactual analyses show that demographic signals affect content delivery in persona-dependent ways: pooled effects are largely null, while subgroup-level effects vary in direction and magnitude. Our work establishes GenAI-based agents as a new tool for algorithmic auditing.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes using generative AI agents with fixed personas as behavioral engines for synthetic accounts to audit black-box personalization algorithms on online platforms, enabling counterfactual analysis of how user attributes affect content delivery.
AI.motivation: Auditing personalization algorithms is challenging because external auditors only have black-box access and personalization depends on complex, evolving user behavior and attributes. Existing methods either use real users (high realism but costly and hard to control) or sock-puppet accounts (scalable but behavior is scripted and unrealistic). Both struggle to disentangle the effects of user attributes from behavior, limiting causal understanding of personalization. The paper aims to close this methodological gap.
AI.method: The authors introduce a framework that uses generative AI agents to drive the behavior of synthetic accounts on online platforms. Each AI agent is instantiated with a fixed persona based on demographic and political survey data and interacts with platform content by reasoning and choosing actions (e.g., clicks, likes, follows). While the behavioral policy is held fixed for each persona, visible demographic attributes such as age, gender, and location are experimentally manipulated across otherwise identical agents. This allows counterfactual experiments on how the platform responds to user attributes. As a case study, they deploy 1,120 such agents on X (formerly Twitter) after the 2024 U.S. election, covering 14 personas and three counterfactual demographic conditions, and log over 200,000 content exposures from both algorithmic and chronological feeds.
AI.result: In their case study on X, the authors find that the algorithmic feed amplifies toxic, polarizing, political, and right-leaning content compared with the chronological feed, and that the degree of amplification differs substantially by users' ideological persona. Counterfactual experiments show that demographic signals influence content delivery in ways that depend on the persona: when effects are averaged across all agents the impact of demographics appears negligible, but at the subgroup level there are heterogeneous effects in both direction and magnitude.
AI.conclusion: Generative AI–driven agents with fixed personas provide a new, scalable, and controllable tool for black-box auditing of personalization algorithms. By decoupling persistent behavioral policies from experimentally varied demographic attributes, the framework enables counterfactual and causal analysis of how platforms personalize content. The case study on X demonstrates that algorithmic feeds can differentially amplify certain kinds of content and that demographic attributes shape content delivery heterogeneously across user types, underscoring the value of this auditing approach.

title: When Calibration Rankings Reverse: Accuracy-Controlled Evaluation for Fair Comparison of LLMs
summary: Calibration evaluates whether a model confidence aligns with its empirical accuracy. Existing studies often compare the calibration of different large language models using global calibration metrics such as Expected Calibration Error and Brier Score. We begin by showing, both theoretically and empirically, that such comparisons are confounded by differences in model accuracy. For fairer cross-model comparison, we then propose ACE, an accuracy-controlled evaluation framework with three complementary views: Instance-Aligned, Distribution-Aligned, and Candidate-Aligned calibration. Across multiple benchmarks, model families, and confidence elicitation methods, we use ACE to study two practically important comparison axes, small versus large models and thinking versus non-thinking models. We find that many previously reported calibration advantages under raw global metrics weaken substantially after accuracy control. We also find that ranking reversal is frequent: models favored by raw metrics often cease to be favored once accuracy is controlled. Our results show that raw global calibration metrics are not robust for cross-model comparison, and that fair calibration comparison requires accuracy-aware evaluation.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that common global calibration metrics (like ECE and Brier Score) are misleading for comparing different language models because they are entangled with accuracy. It proposes ACE, an accuracy-controlled evaluation framework that offers three complementary calibration views, and shows that many claimed calibration gains disappear or even reverse once accuracy is controlled.
AI.motivation: Researchers and practitioners routinely compare the calibration of large language models using global metrics such as Expected Calibration Error and Brier Score. However, these metrics mix two aspects: how accurate a model is and how well its confidence matches that accuracy. As models differ substantially in accuracy (e.g., small vs large, or with vs without chain-of-thought), naive use of these metrics can unfairly favor more accurate models, leading to misleading conclusions about which models are actually better calibrated. The paper aims to disentangle calibration from accuracy so that cross-model comparisons focus on calibration quality rather than raw performance.
AI.method: The authors first provide theoretical analysis and empirical demonstrations that global calibration metrics are systematically confounded by differences in accuracy across models. They then introduce ACE (Accuracy-Controlled Evaluation), a framework that holds accuracy constant while comparing calibration. ACE offers three complementary perspectives: (1) Instance-Aligned calibration, which compares models on the same instances with matched accuracy levels; (2) Distribution-Aligned calibration, which aligns the accuracy distributions of models for fair comparison; and (3) Candidate-Aligned calibration, which focuses on calibration over candidate outputs or predictions. They apply ACE across various benchmarks, model families, and confidence elicitation schemes (e.g., direct probability outputs, post-hoc calibrations) and compare small vs large models and models with vs without explicit reasoning traces (“thinking” models).
AI.result: Using ACE, the authors find that many previously reported calibration advantages of certain models, based on raw global metrics, weaken greatly after controlling for accuracy. In several scenarios, the ranking of models by calibration reverses: models that appeared better calibrated with global metrics no longer look superior when accuracy is matched. This occurs across multiple datasets, model sizes, and reasoning settings, indicating that the confounding effect is pervasive rather than anecdotal.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that raw global calibration metrics such as ECE and Brier Score are unreliable for fair cross-model calibration comparisons because they are entangled with accuracy differences. Accuracy-controlled evaluation is necessary to make meaningful claims about which model is better calibrated. The proposed ACE framework—with its instance-, distribution-, and candidate-aligned views—provides a more robust basis for such comparisons and reveals that many prior calibration claims for large or “thinking” models may be overstated or even incorrect once accuracy is taken into account.

title: What Drives Interactive Improvement from Feedback?
comment: 9 pages, 7 figures, accepted to the RLxF Workshop at ICML 2026
summary: We study when natural-language feedback produces improvement beyond the gains obtainable from repeated attempts alone. In multi-turn language agent setting, higher final accuracy can reflect useful feedback, but it can also arise from resampling, format correction, or additional test-time computation. To separate these effects, we introduce a controlled student-teacher protocol across Omni-MATH, Codeforces, BBEH Linguini, and ARC-AGI1, evaluating thirteen open-weight models in both student and teacher roles. We compare external feedback, self-feedback, and unguided self-refinement, while varying interaction history, task difficulty, and teacher access to privileged task information. Across settings, we find that multi-turn improvement is often not evidence of feedback use: self-generated feedback adds little beyond unguided self-refinement, whereas the strongest external teachers produce substantially larger feedback-specific gains, suggesting that useful feedback must provide guidance beyond generic retry. Dense student-teacher interaction matrices further show that interactive gains are driven more by the student's ability to use feedback than by the teacher's identity, although teacher choice remains important for a fixed student. These results suggest that feedback-based agents should be evaluated against repeated-attempt baselines, and that ability to act on feedback, not merely feedback availability, is a central bottleneck for interactive improvement. We release our controlled student-teacher evaluation framework at https://j-lojek.github.io/feedback-generation-is-a-bottleneck/.

AI.tldr: The paper disentangles genuine benefits of natural-language feedback from simple gains due to multiple attempts, showing that much of multi-turn improvement does not actually stem from using feedback unless the teacher is strong and the student can leverage it.
AI.motivation: Multi-turn language agents often show improved performance after feedback, but it is unclear how much of this improvement comes from actual use of feedback versus trivial factors like resampling, formatting fixes, or more computation. The authors want a rigorous way to measure feedback-specific gains and to understand when and why feedback helps.
AI.method: They design a controlled student–teacher protocol applied across several benchmarks (Omni-MATH, Codeforces, BBEH Linguini, ARC-AGI). Thirteen open-weight models are evaluated both as students and as teachers. They compare three conditions—external feedback from another model, self-feedback, and unguided self-refinement without explicit feedback—while varying interaction history length, problem difficulty, and whether the teacher has privileged task information. They analyze the resulting matrix of student–teacher interactions to attribute improvements specifically to feedback versus mere retries.
AI.result: Self-generated feedback provides minimal benefit compared with unguided self-refinement, indicating that many observed multi-turn improvements do not rely on feedback content. In contrast, strong external teachers yield significantly larger gains that can be attributed to feedback itself. The interaction matrices reveal that the student’s capability to use feedback is a stronger determinant of improvement than which teacher is used, though teacher choice still matters for any fixed student model.
AI.conclusion: Multi-turn performance gains should not be taken as evidence that an agent is effectively using feedback; evaluations must compare against repeated-attempt baselines. The main bottleneck for interactive improvement is the student model’s ability to act on feedback, not just the presence of feedback. The authors provide an open framework for controlled student–teacher evaluation to better study and benchmark feedback-based agents.

title: When transformers learn "impossible" languages, what do they learn?
summary: Recent work suggests that transformer language models show a bias towards human languages over unnatural ("impossible") languages argued to be unacquirable by humans. However, this literature has largely based these claims on differences in sample efficiency and test-set perplexity, rather than on direct evaluations of the linguistic capacities that could plausibly explain non-attestation in human languages. We evaluate two theoretically motivated linking hypotheses: impossibility arising from deficiencies in grammatical sensitivity or generative production. Using GPT-2 style models trained on perturbed "impossible" variants of English, we measure sensitivity to grammaticality using BLiMP minimal pairs, finding that model performance exhibits only gradual degradation, mediated by the language's information locality. In contrast, these models exhibited pronounced failures in generation, producing substantially fewer high-quality sentences at longer lengths. Together, these results suggest generative deficiency and transmission failures as a plausible linking hypothesis between language model behaviour and non-attestation of impossible languages.

AI.tldr: The paper tests why transformer language models treat some artificially impossible language variants differently, finding that they remain fairly grammatically sensitive but struggle to generate long, high‑quality sentences, pointing to generative and transmission issues rather than basic grammatical incapacity.
AI.motivation: Prior work claims that transformer language models prefer human languages to theoretically impossible ones, often measured only via sample efficiency or perplexity. However, these metrics do not directly probe the specific linguistic capacities that could make a language unlearnable or unattested in humans. The authors want to connect model behavior to explicit linguistic hypotheses about why certain languages are impossible, focusing on grammatical sensitivity and generative ability as potential explanatory links.
AI.method: The authors train GPT‑2–style transformer models on perturbed, "impossible" variants of English that violate proposed constraints on human language. They then evaluate (1) grammatical sensitivity using BLiMP minimal pair judgments to see whether the models can still distinguish grammatical from ungrammatical sentences, and (2) generative capacities by analyzing the quality and length of sentences the models produce. They also examine how information locality of the artificial languages modulates these effects.
AI.result: Models trained on impossible language variants show only gradual decreases in performance on BLiMP grammaticality judgments, indicating largely preserved grammatical sensitivity. This degradation correlates with the information locality of the constructed language. By contrast, the same models show strong deficits in generation: when asked to produce text, they generate far fewer high‑quality sentences as sequence length increases, compared to models trained on natural language.
AI.conclusion: The findings suggest that transformer models can still learn many grammatical distinctions even in impossible language settings, so grammatical sensitivity alone does not explain the models’ bias toward natural languages. Instead, the main failures arise in generative production and maintaining quality over long sequences. The authors propose that such generative deficiencies and resulting transmission failures offer a plausible explanation for why impossible languages are not attested, providing a more precise linking hypothesis between language model behavior and human language typology.

title: Contrastive Reflection for Iterative Prompt Optimization
comment: 6 pages, 1 figure. To appear at Agent4IR @ KDD 2026 (KDD 2026 Workshop on AI Agents for Information Retrieval)
summary:
LLM agents are becoming central to information retrieval: they issue retrieval queries, synthesize answers, and increasingly serve as judges for IR evaluation. Improving the prompts that control these agents is an optimization problem, but in applied IR settings it often looks less like blind search and more like debugging. Engineers need to know which behavior failed, which nearby behavior still worked, what distinguishes the two, and whether a prompt edit improves held-out quality without introducing regressions.
  We present Contrastive Reflection, an iterative prompt-optimization framework for agentic IR workflows. The framework starts from a task-centric quality definition: QA agents expose retrieval or reasoning traces, and grading agents expose dimension-level scores and rationales. These structured traces are used to identify error-anchored behavioral slices, add nearby successful examples from the same region, and ask a Teacher LLM to propose a targeted prompt edit. Candidate edits are accepted only when validation performance improves, optionally subject to regression checks. We instantiate the framework with a tree-based slice selector, but the contribution is the contrastive reflection loop rather than the tree itself.
  On a public HotpotQA retrieval-augmented QA setup, one tree-selected contrastive repair improves held-out exact-match accuracy from 51.4% to 60.4%. Failure-only and random-evidence variants improve less and break more previously correct examples. A light instruction-only comparison places the method near modern prompt optimizers: MIPROv2 reaches 59.4% and GEPA 57.0%. The result is an interpretable optimization loop for IR agents, aimed at making prompt repair more inspectable and validation-driven.

AI.tldr: Introduces Contrastive Reflection, a framework to iteratively debug and optimize prompts for LLM-based IR agents using contrastive analysis of failures vs nearby successes.
AI.motivation: In IR workflows, LLM agents are used not only for answering queries but also for judging results. Tuning their prompts is critical but current optimization resembles opaque search, whereas practitioners actually need debugging-style insight: what failed, what nearby behavior worked, and how edits affect performance without regressions.
AI.method: Define task-centric quality with QA agents that expose retrieval/reasoning traces and grading agents that provide dimension-level scores and rationales. Use these structured traces to form error-anchored behavioral slices, augment them with similar successful examples, and feed these contrastive sets to a Teacher LLM that proposes targeted prompt edits. Employ a tree-based slice selector to find these regions, and accept edits only if they improve validation metrics, optionally with regression checks, iterating this loop.
AI.result: On HotpotQA with retrieval-augmented QA, a single contrastive repair selected via the tree mechanism raises held-out exact-match from 51.4% to 60.4%. Variants that only look at failures or sample evidence randomly yield smaller gains and introduce more regressions. Compared to prompt optimizers, the method performs competitively, approaching MIPROv2 (59.4%) and surpassing GEPA (57.0%).
AI.conclusion: Contrastive Reflection provides an interpretable, validation-driven loop for prompt repair in IR agents, leveraging contrastive slices of failures versus nearby successes to generate effective prompt edits. It improves QA accuracy while limiting regressions and offers a more inspectable alternative to black-box prompt optimization techniques.

title: Test-Time Verification for Text-to-SQL via Outcome Reward Models
comment: Accepted to the SURGeLLM Workshop at ACL 2026, San Diego, US
summary: Improving the reliability of large language models (LLMs) at inference time is a central challenge in structured reasoning tasks such as Text-to-SQL. Common test-time inference strategies, including Best-of-N sampling and Majority Voting, rely on heuristic signals such as execution success or output frequency, which provide limited semantic discrimination across candidate outputs. In this work, we study Outcome Reward Models (ORMs) as learned semantic scoring functions for test-time verification in Text-to-SQL. While ORMs have been previously explored for test-time scaling and alignment, their application to structured query generation remains underexplored. We introduce GradeSQL, a scalable framework for training task-specific ORMs via automated candidate generation and execution-based labeling, enabling verifier training without manual annotation. We integrate ORMs into a verification-driven Best-of-N pipeline and evaluate our approach on the BIRD and Spider benchmarks across multiple open-source LLM families. ORM-based selection consistently outperforms execution-based Best-of-N and Majority Voting, with gains of up to +4.33% on BIRD and +2.10% on Spider. We further show that ORMs scale effectively with larger candidate sets and yield stronger improvements on complex queries. Overall, our results demonstrate that ORM-based verification provides a simple, effective, and scalable alternative to heuristic test-time selection strategies for Text-to-SQL. Code datasets and models are publicly available.

AI.tldr: Proposes GradeSQL, a framework using Outcome Reward Models (ORMs) to semantically score and verify Text-to-SQL outputs at inference time, outperforming common heuristic selection methods.
AI.motivation: Inference-time reliability of LLMs in structured reasoning tasks like Text-to-SQL is limited by heuristic selection strategies (e.g., execution success, majority vote) that cannot finely discriminate between semantically better or worse SQL candidates. There is a need for principled, learned verification mechanisms that can improve accuracy without requiring manual labels.
AI.method: Introduce Outcome Reward Models (ORMs) trained as task-specific verifiers for Text-to-SQL. They create GradeSQL, which automatically generates multiple SQL candidates, labels them via execution-based signals, and trains ORMs to score the semantic quality of each candidate. At inference, they plug the ORM into a verification-driven Best-of-N pipeline to select the best SQL among multiple LLM outputs. They evaluate across multiple open-source LLM families on BIRD and Spider benchmarks, vary candidate set sizes, and analyze performance by query complexity.
AI.result: ORM-based selection consistently surpasses execution-based Best-of-N and Majority Voting on Text-to-SQL. Reported gains reach up to +4.33% accuracy on BIRD and +2.10% on Spider. Performance improves further as the candidate pool grows and for more complex SQL queries, indicating good scaling properties of ORMs as verifiers.
AI.conclusion: Learned Outcome Reward Models provide a simple, scalable, and more effective alternative to heuristic test-time selection for Text-to-SQL. GradeSQL shows that ORMs can be trained without manual annotation and integrated into standard Best-of-N pipelines to substantially boost reliability and accuracy of LLM-generated SQL, especially on harder queries.

title: How Can AI Find My Model? A Model-Finding Experimental Study Considering Data Formats, Embeddings, and Retrieval Strategies
comment: Accepted for publication in Proceedings of the 2026 Winter Simulation Conference (WSC 2026). The final published version will appear in IEEE Xplore
summary: Discovering simulation models for reuse remains a fundamental challenge in Modeling and Simulation (M&S). When many models coexist, identifying those that align with a given modeling intent remains difficult. Recent advances in Artificial Intelligence (AI), particularly retrieval-based approaches, offer a promising pathway to operate at this semantic layer. In this paper, we present an experimental study investigating the impact of data representation, transformer-based embedding models, and retrieval strategies on the discovery of simulation models using natural language queries. We evaluated performance across multiple query types using standard information retrieval metrics, including recall@5 and nDCG@5. Results show that data representation matters, open-source embedding models can achieve high performance, and reranking methods are important, especially as query complexity increases. This work provides a baseline for AI-driven model discovery and discusses its role in advancing toward AI-driven composability and interoperability.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how different text representations, transformer-based embeddings, and retrieval/reranking strategies affect finding relevant simulation models with natural-language queries, showing that representation and reranking significantly influence retrieval quality.
AI.motivation: In Modeling and Simulation, many simulation models exist but are hard to find and reuse because current discovery mechanisms do not operate well at the semantic level of users’ modeling intents. With the rise of AI and neural retrieval, there is an opportunity to improve how simulation models are discovered using natural language, but it is unclear which representations, embedding models, and retrieval strategies work best for this domain.
AI.method: The authors conduct an experimental evaluation of AI-based retrieval for simulation model discovery. They compare different ways of representing model-related data, test various transformer-based embedding models (including open-source ones), and examine different retrieval pipelines, including reranking steps. They use natural language queries of varying complexity and assess performance with standard IR metrics such as recall@5 and nDCG@5.
AI.result: The experiments show that the choice of data representation strongly affects retrieval performance, open-source transformer-based embedding models can perform very well for simulation model retrieval, and reranking methods significantly improve results, particularly for more complex queries. Quantitatively, this is reflected in improved recall@5 and nDCG@5 across configurations that use better representations and reranking.
AI.conclusion: The study establishes a baseline for AI-driven discovery of simulation models using natural language queries. It concludes that careful design of data representation, selection of suitable open-source embedding models, and inclusion of reranking are key to high-quality retrieval, especially as query complexity increases. The authors argue that such AI-driven discovery is an important step toward broader goals of AI-driven composability and interoperability in Modeling and Simulation.

title: Multilingual Polarization Detection Using Transformer-Based Models with Class Weighting and Threshold Tuning
summary: This paper describes our submission to SemEval-2026 Task 9 on detecting multilingual, multicultural, and multievent online polarization. We address all three subtasks: binary polarization detection, polarization type classification, and manifestation identification for English and Swahili. Our approach leverages transformer-based models (RoBERTa-base for English, AfroXLMR-base for Swahili) with class-weighted loss functions to address severe label imbalance and per-label threshold tuning to optimize multi-label classification. On the test set, we achieve F1 macro scores of 0.7901 (English) and 0.7910 (Swahili) for Subtask 1, 0.4615 (English) and 0.4808 (Swahili) for Subtask 2 and 0.4791 (English) and 0.5830 (Swahili) for Subtask 3, which give competitive performance on the leaderboard, demonstrating the effectiveness of our methods for handling imbalanced multi-label polarization detection. Our error analysis reveals that models struggle with dehumanization detection and lack of empathy.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a SemEval-2026 shared-task system for detecting online polarization in English and Swahili using transformer models with imbalance-aware training and threshold tuning, achieving competitive F1 scores but struggling with nuanced categories like dehumanization and lack of empathy.
AI.motivation: Online discourse increasingly exhibits polarized content across languages and cultural/event contexts, and shared tasks like SemEval-2026 Task 9 require robust, multilingual systems that can handle label imbalance and multi-label settings. Existing approaches often underperform on low-resource languages and severely imbalanced, fine-grained polarization labels, motivating a focused architecture and training strategy for English and Swahili polarization detection across multiple subtasks.
AI.method: The authors build transformer-based classifiers: RoBERTa-base for English and AfroXLMR-base for Swahili. They treat the tasks as binary or multi-label classification, address severe label imbalance by using class-weighted loss functions, and further improve multi-label performance through per-label decision threshold tuning instead of relying on a default 0.5 cutoff. They evaluate the systems on all three SemEval subtasks: binary polarization detection, polarization type classification, and manifestation identification.
AI.result: On the official test sets, the system attains macro-F1 scores of 0.7901 (English) and 0.7910 (Swahili) for the binary polarization detection task (Subtask 1), 0.4615 (English) and 0.4808 (Swahili) for polarization type classification (Subtask 2), and 0.4791 (English) and 0.5830 (Swahili) for manifestation identification (Subtask 3). These results are competitive on the shared-task leaderboard, particularly given the class imbalance and multilingual setting.
AI.conclusion: The combination of strong transformer backbones, class-weighted losses for imbalance, and per-label threshold tuning yields competitive performance for multilingual, multi-label polarization detection in English and Swahili. However, error analysis shows that the models still struggle with subtle, high-level phenomena such as dehumanization and lack of empathy, highlighting the need for richer representations or additional signal (e.g., context or external knowledge) to capture these nuanced aspects of polarization.

title: BayesBench: Evaluating LLM Belief Trajectories Under Multi-Turn Evidence Accumulation
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are typically deployed in multi-turn conversations, where each turn provides new evidence that should reduce epistemic uncertainty about their environment. Acting rationally then requires inferring the unobserved quantities that govern it and updating beliefs about them as evidence accumulates. Yet most evaluations only score the model's final-turn answer in a single-turn format, leaving this process unexamined. We ask how closely LLMs' belief updates match those of a rational Bayesian reasoner in multi-turn settings, and introduce BayesBench, a suite of simulation environments that probe this across three progressively complex tasks: (i) Bayesian estimation, where the model infers an unknown parameter from sequential evidence; (ii) Bayesian prediction, where the model turns inferred beliefs about a latent variable into outcome forecasts; and (iii) latent-framed Bayesian prediction, where observations are filtered through a user-persona framing, requiring joint inference over the latent state and the persona. Across seven LLMs (3B--70B), scaling improves latent inference and evidence accumulation, with updates occasionally matching the Bayesian posterior. However, these gains do not reliably carry over to downstream prediction, exposing a gap between inferring latent structure and using it to rationally update beliefs about the target outcome.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces BayesBench, a benchmark to evaluate how closely large language models (LLMs) perform Bayesian-style belief updating in multi-turn settings, showing that while larger models get better at inferring latent variables from sequential evidence, they still struggle to translate these inferences into rational predictions about future outcomes.
AI.motivation: LLMs are often used in multi-turn conversations where each turn gives new information that should reduce uncertainty. Ideally, the model should update its beliefs like a rational Bayesian agent as new evidence appears. However, current evaluations usually look only at one-shot, final answers and ignore the belief-updating process itself. The authors want to understand and measure how well LLMs perform this kind of sequential, Bayesian-like reasoning.
AI.method: The authors build BayesBench, a collection of simulation environments designed to probe Bayesian belief updating by LLMs. They define three tasks with increasing difficulty: (i) Bayesian estimation, where the model must infer an unknown parameter from sequentially revealed evidence; (ii) Bayesian prediction, where it must turn its inferred beliefs about a hidden variable into forecasts of observable outcomes; and (iii) latent-framed Bayesian prediction, where observations are described through a user persona or framing, requiring the model to jointly infer both the hidden state and the persona. They test seven LLMs of varying sizes (3B–70B parameters) in multi-turn interactions within these environments and compare the models’ belief updates at each step to the normative Bayesian posterior.
AI.result: Empirically, larger models show better performance at latent variable inference and more consistent evidence accumulation, and in some cases their belief updates align closely with the exact Bayesian posterior. However, this improvement in internal inference does not consistently translate into better downstream predictions; models often fail to turn their inferred latent beliefs into properly updated forecasts about the eventual outcomes. This exposes a misalignment between correct Bayesian-style belief updating over hidden variables and the practical use of those beliefs for prediction.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can approximate Bayesian updating over latent variables, and scale helps them track evidence and infer hidden structure more accurately. Yet there remains a systematic gap between correctly inferring the latent state and using that information to make rational, Bayesian-consistent predictions. The paper’s BayesBench benchmark provides a way to quantify this gap and highlights the need for methods that better align models’ internal belief updates with their observable predictive behavior in multi-turn settings.

title: Training Therapeutic Judges and Multi-Agent Systems for Human-Aligned Mental Health Support
summary: Large language models show promise for mental health support, yet therapeutic quality improves only when evaluation functions as an actionable control signal rather than a passive metric. We introduce a framework that formulates therapeutic response generation as a decision-refinement problem driven by multi-dimensional, human-aligned evaluation. In Stage I, we introduce TheraJudge, an open-source therapeutic evaluator trained via preference-based optimization on human-annotated data to produce reliable judgments across 7 psychological dimensions. In Stage II, we introduce TheraAgent, which operationalizes TheraJudge's evaluations through a coordinated refinement process with specialized Critic, Coach, and Therapist roles that translate evaluative signals into targeted response revisions. Empirically, TheraJudge achieves strong agreement with clinician ratings, with intraclass correlation coefficients (ICC = 0.87-0.95), surpassing supervised baselines and strong closed-source judges, particularly on critical dimensions such as Safety, Relevance, and Empathy. Acting on these evaluations, TheraAgent yields a +0.43 improvement in human-rated therapeutic quality (on a 5-point scale) under blind evaluation, with 96\% clinician inter-rater reliability. Low-quality responses ($\leq 3$) improve by +2.45 points with a 94\% recovery rate, demonstrating targeted correction of unsafe outputs. Overall, our results indicate that effective alignment of mental-health LLMs stems from acting on human-aligned evaluation, rather than relying solely on stronger generation. We release code at https://github.com/vis-nlp/TheraAlign.

AI.tldr: The paper presents TheraAlign, a two-stage framework where a human-aligned therapeutic evaluator (TheraJudge) guides an LLM-based agent (TheraAgent) to iteratively refine mental-health responses, significantly boosting therapeutic quality, safety, and empathy.
AI.motivation: Existing mental-health LLMs show promise but often fall short on therapeutic quality, safety, and empathy because evaluation is used only as an offline metric rather than as a control signal during generation. There is a need for reliable, multi-dimensional, human-aligned evaluators and mechanisms that can operationalize their feedback to systematically improve therapeutic responses, especially correcting unsafe or low-quality outputs.
AI.method: The authors design a two-stage framework. Stage I: TheraJudge, an open-source evaluator model, is trained with preference-based optimization on human-annotated data to score responses along seven psychological dimensions (e.g., Safety, Relevance, Empathy). Stage II: TheraAgent uses TheraJudge’s multi-dimensional scores to refine responses via a coordinated process involving three specialized roles—Critic, Coach, and Therapist—that interpret evaluation signals and propose targeted edits, iterating until quality goals are met.
AI.result: TheraJudge attains high agreement with clinician ratings, with intraclass correlation coefficients between 0.87 and 0.95, outperforming supervised baselines and strong closed-source evaluators, particularly on Safety, Relevance, and Empathy. When TheraJudge’s evaluations are used to drive refinement, TheraAgent improves human-rated therapeutic quality by +0.43 on a 5-point scale under blind evaluation, with 96% clinician inter-rater reliability. For initially low-quality responses (score ≤ 3), TheraAgent increases scores by +2.45 on average and recovers 94% of such cases, notably reducing unsafe outputs.
AI.conclusion: Using human-aligned, multi-dimensional evaluation as an active control signal—rather than merely as a passive metric—enables more effective alignment of mental-health LLMs. The TheraAlign framework, comprising TheraJudge and TheraAgent, shows that evaluation-driven refinement can substantially enhance therapeutic quality, safety, and empathy in generated responses. The authors release code to facilitate further research and practical deployment.

title: When Does Learning to Stop Help? A Cost-Aware Study of Early Exits in Reasoning Models
summary: Reasoning models spend different amounts of useful computation across instances, but it remains unclear when a learned stopping rule improves over simple confidence or convergence thresholds. We study this question with LearnStop, a hidden-state-free checkpoint stopper for reasoning language models. At fixed budget checkpoints, LearnStop probes a short answer from the current reasoning prefix and predicts prefix correctness from online features such as answer confidence, entropy, prefix vote share, answer stability, and backtracking-marker density. Across 18 task-model settings spanning GSM8K, MATH-500, MMLU-Pro, AIME-90, GPQA, Qwen3, and DeepSeek-R1 distillations, the answer is task-dependent. On free-form math, learned multi-feature stopping improves the fixed-budget frontier and often beats scalar exits: on GSM8K with Qwen3-32B, the empirical frontier reaches a post-hoc peak adapt gain of +0.157, validation-selected operating points preserve positive gains, and the paired gain over the strongest scalar baseline is +0.028. On multiple-choice and very hard settings, scalar confidence, entropy, or stability rules are competitive or stronger. We therefore frame learned stopping not as a universal replacement for scalar exits, but as a tool whose value depends on trajectory structure. We further provide validation-selected operating points, paired bootstrap tests, finite-grid lost-correct risk calibration, cost accounting under KV-fork, prefix-cache, and black-box regimes, H100 serving profiles, checkpoint-schedule sweeps, transfer analyses, and robustness checks. The main practical finding is that learned stopping is useful when many questions become correct before full budget but do not exhibit a single reliable scalar stopping signal; its benefits largely disappear when confidence or answer convergence already solves the stopping problem.

AI.tldr: The paper studies when learned stopping rules for reasoning models help compared to simple scalar signals like confidence or entropy, introducing LearnStop, a multi-feature checkpoint stopper, and finds its benefits are task-dependent and strongest on free-form math where no single scalar signal is reliably predictive.
AI.motivation: Reasoning language models consume varying computation per instance, and we want to stop generation once a correct answer is reached to save compute without hurting accuracy. Existing stopping commonly uses simple scalar thresholds (confidence, entropy, convergence), but it is unclear when a more complex, learned multi-feature rule can do better. Clarifying this is important for serving efficiency and for understanding trajectory dynamics across tasks and models.
AI.method: They introduce LearnStop, a hidden-state-free checkpoint stopping mechanism that, at pre-defined compute checkpoints, probes a short answer from the current reasoning prefix and predicts whether that prefix already yields the correct final answer. LearnStop uses online, easily computed features such as answer confidence, entropy, vote share among prefixes, answer stability, and backtracking-marker density, and is trained as a classifier over checkpoints. They evaluate it across 18 combinations of tasks and models (including GSM8K, MATH-500, MMLU-Pro, AIME-90, GPQA, and Qwen3/DeepSeek-R1 distillations), compare against scalar-based exits (confidence, entropy, stability), and perform cost/accuracy tradeoff analyses, bootstrap tests, risk calibration, and various robustness and transfer studies under different serving cost models (KV-fork, prefix-cache, black-box, and H100 deployment).
AI.result: On free-form math tasks like GSM8K, LearnStop’s multi-feature stopping substantially improves the accuracy–compute frontier relative to fixed-budget generation and outperforms scalar-based stopping. For example, on GSM8K with Qwen3-32B, the achievable adaptivity gain peaks at +0.157, with validation-selected operating points still giving positive gains and a +0.028 accuracy gain over the strongest scalar baseline at comparable compute. However, on multiple-choice tasks and very hard benchmarks, simple scalar rules based on confidence, entropy, or answer stability are competitive with or superior to LearnStop, yielding little or no benefit from the more complex learned stopper.
AI.conclusion: Learned multi-feature stopping is not universally better than scalar exits. It is most useful on tasks where many questions become correct before the maximum budget and no single scalar signal reliably indicates correctness. In such settings, leveraging richer trajectory features can yield better compute–accuracy tradeoffs. When confidence or convergence already provide a clean stopping cue, the extra complexity of a learned stopper brings limited benefit. The paper also offers practical guidance: validated operating points, risk-calibrated stopping thresholds, and serving cost analyses to inform when and how to deploy learned stopping in real systems.

title: Beyond Clean Text: Evaluating Encoder and Decoder Robustness for Bangla Event Detection in Noisy Text
summary: Event detection (ED) systems are typically evaluated on clean, curated text, leaving their robustness to real-world noise largely unexplored, particularly for low-resource languages such as Bangla. We introduce a generalized Bangla news event ontology and a benchmark comprising 9,979 annotated sentences across 40 event subtypes, spanning clean news text, real-world Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) transcripts, and orthographically corrupted text. We systematically evaluate fine-tuned encoder-only models (BanglaBERT and XLM-R) alongside instruction-tuned decoder-only large language models (Llama 3 and Gemma 3). Our results reveal a clear architectural trade-off: encoder models achieve higher performance on clean text but degrade substantially under noise, whereas decoder-only LLMs are markedly more robust, particularly when event triggers are corrupted. We further show that embedding annotation guidelines during instruction tuning establishes a higher performance baseline on noisy text but yields inconsistent reductions in performance degradation across noisy conditions. Finally, model scaling consistently improves the robustness of decoder-only LLMs, while combined training on clean and noisy data serves as an effective regularization strategy that disproportionately benefits encoder architectures, significantly narrowing the robustness gap.

AI.tldr: They build a Bangla event-detection benchmark across clean and noisy text and show encoder models are strong on clean data but brittle under noise, while decoder LLMs are more robust, and that scaling and mixed clean/noisy training improve robustness.
AI.motivation: Most event detection systems are tested only on clean, carefully edited text, so their robustness to real-world noise (like ASR errors or spelling corruption) is unclear, especially for low-resource languages such as Bangla. There is also a need for a structured event ontology and benchmark to study robustness across architectures and training strategies in this setting.
AI.method: They design a generalized Bangla news event ontology with 40 event subtypes and create a 9,979-sentence benchmark covering clean news, ASR transcripts, and orthographically corrupted text. They then systematically evaluate fine-tuned encoder-only models (BanglaBERT, XLM-R) and instruction-tuned decoder-only LLMs (Llama 3, Gemma 3), including variants where annotation guidelines are embedded in the instructions. They further study the impact of model scaling and of training jointly on clean and noisy data on robustness.
AI.result: Encoders achieve higher ED performance on clean text but their performance drops sharply under noise, whereas decoder-only LLMs are substantially more robust, particularly when event triggers are corrupted. Incorporating guidelines during instruction tuning raises performance on noisy inputs but does not consistently reduce performance degradation across all noise types. Larger decoder LLMs show improved robustness with scale. Training on a mixture of clean and noisy data acts as regularization that particularly helps encoder models, narrowing the robustness gap relative to decoder LLMs.
AI.conclusion: There is a clear robustness trade-off between encoder and decoder architectures for Bangla event detection: encoders excel on clean text but are brittle to noise, while decoder-only LLMs are inherently more noise-robust. Providing task guidelines and scaling up decoder models improves noisy-text performance, and mixed clean+noisy training is especially effective at regularizing encoder models and closing the robustness gap. The new ontology and benchmark enable systematic robustness evaluation for Bangla ED in realistic conditions.

title: Beyond expert users: agents should help users construct preferences, not just elicit them
summary: Agents typically assume an expert user -- one with well-formed preferences about what they want -- and default to clarifying questions whenever the task is underspecified. We argue this assumption is unrealistic. Users often lack the domain knowledge to have completely specified preferences; if asked about their preference on some feature, the user may be unable to answer without the agent helping the user to learn some domain knowledge needed to form a preference for that feature, e.g., via examples or explanations. To formalize these principles, we draw on the Search-Experience-Credence framework from Information Economics to introduce CoPref, a model of how users construct preferences based on agent dialog actions. We then study these ideas concretely in agentic recommender systems, proposing CoShop, an interactive benchmark. In CoShop, an agent converses with and makes recommendations for a CoPref user. The agent's performance depends on whether it can help the user gain the knowledge needed to specify the task well. Evaluating five frontier models, we find that no agent exceeds 56% accuracy on CoShop despite five turns of interaction. Failures stem not from agents' ability to find items, but from how little the interaction expands what users know about what they want.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that current AI agents wrongly assume users already have clear, fully formed preferences, and instead introduces a framework and benchmark to study how agents should help users *form* those preferences through interaction.
AI.motivation: Many interactive agents and recommender systems assume an “expert user” who can immediately answer clarifying questions about their preferences. In reality, users often lack the domain knowledge needed to state what they want and must first learn about options and attributes. This mismatch leads to ineffective clarification dialogues and poor recommendations. The authors are motivated to formally capture how users’ preferences are constructed during interaction, and to evaluate whether current models can effectively teach users about the space of options so that preferences can be formed, not just elicited.
AI.method: The authors adapt the Search–Experience–Credence (SEC) framework from Information Economics to define CoPref, a formal model of preference construction where a user’s preferences develop as they gain domain knowledge via an agent’s dialog actions (like explanations and examples). They instantiate this model in the context of agentic recommender systems by creating CoShop, an interactive benchmark. In CoShop, an agent engages in multi-turn dialogue with a simulated CoPref user, aiming to recommend suitable items. The benchmark is designed so that success requires helping the user learn about relevant features, not merely querying pre-existing preferences. They then evaluate five state-of-the-art models on CoShop, measuring recommendation accuracy over five interaction turns.
AI.result: On the CoShop benchmark, none of the five frontier models achieve more than 56% recommendation accuracy even after five turns of interaction. Analysis shows that models are generally capable of locating items in the catalog, but their dialogues do not meaningfully increase the user’s (simulated) knowledge about important product attributes. As a result, the user’s preferences remain under-specified and recommendations are often suboptimal, revealing a key limitation of current agentic recommenders in supporting preference formation.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that assuming users have fully formed preferences is flawed for many real-world settings. Effective agents must support preference construction by teaching users about the option space, not just asking clarifying questions. The CoPref framework provides a way to formalize this process, and the CoShop benchmark exposes that current frontier models fall well short of this goal, with recommendation errors largely due to weak knowledge-expanding interactions. The work suggests that future research should focus on dialog strategies and model capabilities that explicitly help users learn and refine what they want over the course of interaction.

title: Bridging Scientific Heritage: An Arabic--Russian Parallel Corpus and LLM Benchmark for Sustainable Knowledge Transfer
summary: Russian and Arabic are among the major languages of scientific communication. Language barriers impede the exchange of research results between these communities, which affects international collaboration and the progress of sustainability-related research. We present a benchmark for Arabic--Russian scientific translation. The benchmark includes a hybrid parallel corpus of about 27,000 sentence pairs, compiled from scientific abstracts and general-domain texts (religion, news, conversations). We fine-tune three multilingual language models -- mT5-base (580M parameters), NLLB-200-distilled-1.3B (1.3B), and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct (7B) -- using LoRA with ranks 8, 16, 32, and 64. The Qwen2.5-7B model with QLoRA (rank 8) yields BLEU 23.15, chrF 43.89, BERTScore 0.906, and COMET 0.758. These are +4.36 BLEU and +0.051 COMET above the zero-shot baseline. Few-shot prompting with three examples does not improve performance, indicating that domain-specific fine-tuning is required. We release the models, the corpus, and the evaluation code. By lowering the language barrier for scientific texts, the work enables knowledge exchange between Arabic-speaking and Russian-speaking researchers. It contributes to sustainable partnerships (UN SDG 17) and innovation infrastructure (SDG 9), aligning with the conference's focus on technology-driven sustainable development.

AI.tldr: They build and release a new Arabic–Russian scientific translation benchmark and fine-tuned multilingual models, showing that domain-specific training significantly improves translation quality over zero‑shot and few‑shot prompting.
AI.motivation: Russian and Arabic are both important languages in science, but lack of high‑quality machine translation between them hinders sharing research results, international collaboration, and especially sustainability-related work. Existing resources for this language pair, particularly in the scientific domain, are limited, so there is a need for a dedicated benchmark and models.
AI.method: They construct a hybrid parallel corpus (~27k sentence pairs) combining scientific abstracts with general-domain data (news, conversations, religious and other texts). They then fine-tune three multilingual models—mT5-base, NLLB-200-distilled-1.3B, and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct—using (Q)LoRA at multiple ranks (8, 16, 32, 64) and evaluate them with BLEU, chrF, BERTScore, and COMET. They also test few-shot prompting with three in-context examples to compare against fine-tuning.
AI.result: The best system is Qwen2.5-7B with QLoRA rank 8, achieving BLEU 23.15, chrF 43.89, BERTScore 0.906, and COMET 0.758, improving over the zero-shot baseline by +4.36 BLEU and +0.051 COMET. Few-shot prompting with three examples fails to outperform zero-shot or fine-tuned models, underscoring that generic models need domain-specific adaptation for this task.
AI.conclusion: A dedicated Arabic–Russian scientific translation benchmark and LoRA-fine-tuned models substantially improve translation quality compared to zero-shot or few-shot approaches. Domain-specific fine-tuning is necessary for high-quality scientific translation. By releasing the corpus, models, and evaluation tools, the work helps reduce language barriers between Arabic- and Russian-speaking researchers and supports sustainable international research collaboration, in line with UN SDG 9 and 17.

title: Investigating Multi-Agent Deliberation in Law
comment: This manuscript has been accepted for presentation at the AIDA2J Workshop during the 21st International Conference of AI & Law in Singapore, June 8 2026
summary: Artificial Intelligence is increasingly applied to the field of law, and has the potential to increase access to justice. One particular movement that is gaining traction is that of agentic AI, wherein AI agents, based on Large Language Models (LLMs) can take autonomous actions. In particular, multi-agent approaches in the legal domain remain largely unexplored. In this paper, we investigate multi-agent deliberation methods for legal reasoning tasks using LLMs. We explore multi-agent deliberation (MAD) and introduce two novel multi-agent frameworks inspired by courtroom procedures and legal argumentation. Our experiments on both legal and non-legal benchmarks reveal that multi-agent frameworks achieve comparable overall performance to baseline large language models, but produce significantly distinct answers. Notably, these approaches can successfully solve cases that the baseline fails to address, and vice versa. We conduct a qualitative evaluation and highlight scenarios where multi-agent frameworks outperform monolithic approaches. For example, multi-agent approaches appear better suited for answering questions that require critical thinking from multiple perspectives. Our work positions multi-agent systems as a promising direction for AI in the legal domain, while demonstrating the potential of law-inspired multi-agent approaches for deliberation.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how multi-agent, law-inspired AI systems based on LLMs deliberate and reason about legal problems, comparing them to single-model baselines.
AI.motivation: AI is increasingly used in law to improve access to justice, but current approaches are mostly single LLMs. The potential of agentic and especially multi-agent LLM systems for legal reasoning is underexplored. The authors want to see whether multi-agent deliberation can provide better or complementary capabilities compared to standard monolithic models.
AI.method: They design and test multi-agent deliberation (MAD) methods for legal reasoning with LLMs, introducing two new multi-agent frameworks inspired by courtroom procedures and legal argumentation. They compare these frameworks against strong baseline LLMs on both legal and general (non-legal) benchmarks, and perform qualitative analysis of when multi-agent setups help or hurt.
AI.result: Multi-agent frameworks reach overall performance similar to strong baseline LLMs but generate substantially different answers. They can correctly solve instances that baseline models miss, while sometimes failing on ones the baseline gets right. Qualitative analysis shows specific classes of tasks—e.g., requiring multi-perspective critical thinking—where multi-agent methods tend to do better.
AI.conclusion: Law-inspired multi-agent LLM systems are a promising approach for legal AI. Even when they do not uniformly outperform single models, they provide complementary strengths, especially on problems requiring deliberation from multiple viewpoints, suggesting multi-agent deliberation as a valuable direction for future legal AI systems.

title: Linguistic Distancing on Social Media: Indicators of Emotion Regulation Across Age Groups
comment: 13 pages, 3 figures, Computational Affective Science Workshop
summary: Managing our emotional responses to events is key to emotional well-being, a process referred to as emotion regulation in psychology. Previous work has established that the degree to which we distance events is a type of emotion regulation. When we psychologically distance from events there can be markers in our language. These markers have been referred to as linguistic distancing. We build upon a previous metric to operationalize linguistic distancing, and explore how it changes across the lifespan. We explore this systematically by analyzing large amounts of social media text, a venue where people express their emotions. By investigating how distancing varies across age groups we can better understand how emotion regulation varies with age and provide initial benchmarks on social media data. We provide additional evidence further strengthening the hypothesis that linguistic distancing occurs in proportionally more instances with age. These findings align with past work in psychology which indicate improved well-being with older age. Better understanding how linguistic distancing changes with age is important because it functions as a marker of well-being and can inform effective health interventions. We provide a foundation for further exploring emotion regulation through linguistic distancing in text data.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how linguistic signs of emotional distancing in social media language change across the lifespan and shows that older people use more such distancing, consistent with better emotion regulation and well-being with age.
AI.motivation: Emotion regulation is crucial for well-being, and one important strategy is psychological distancing from emotional events. Prior work suggests this distancing leaves linguistic traces (linguistic distancing), but we know little about how these traces vary with age in naturalistic, large-scale settings. Understanding age-related patterns can clarify how emotion regulation develops over the lifespan and support health interventions using text data.
AI.method: The authors extend an existing metric that quantifies linguistic distancing in text. They then apply this operationalization to large-scale social media data—where users naturally express emotions—to systematically compare levels of linguistic distancing across different age groups.
AI.result: Analysis of social media posts reveals that linguistic distancing appears more frequently and/or more strongly in older age groups, providing converging evidence that the use of distancing in language increases with age.
AI.conclusion: Linguistic distancing in text is a useful marker of emotion regulation and well-being, and it tends to increase with age. The work offers initial benchmarks for linguistic distancing in social media, aligns with psychological findings of improved well-being in older adults, and establishes a foundation for leveraging text-based measures of distancing in future research and health interventions targeting emotion regulation.

title: Why Solve It Twice? Hierarchical Accumulation of Skills for Transfer-Efficient ML Engineering
comment: 19 pages. Accepted at the ICML 2026 Workshop on Deep Learning for Code (DL4C)
summary: ML engineering agents waste compute rediscovering known techniques because every competition is a cold start. We present HASTE, a hierarchical multi-agent system that organizes cross-competition knowledge into three scope tiers (global, domain, and competition-specific), each coupled to a matching agent level. An orchestrator coordinates domain specialists and promotes learning between tiers via LLM-driven abstraction. A controlled ablation provides evidence for scoped loading: holding a 159-skill inventory constant across 8 competitions, tiered loading achieves a 100% medal rate while flat loading reaches only 62.5%, the same medal rate as loading no skills, and consumes 2x the output tokens. On the full MLE-Bench Lite benchmark (22 Kaggle competitions), HASTE reaches a medal rate of 77.3% using Claude Sonnet 4.6 at 12h per competition. In a cold-start run, the system begins with no accumulated skills. In warm-start runs, it reloads skills learned from earlier competitions, using only global and domain-level skills for transfer across competitions. Warm starts use 52% fewer refinement iterations, and the fraction of proposed changes kept by the agent rises from 42% at low inventory to 85% once 50+ skills are available. These results suggest that better knowledge organization can partly substitute for model strength and compute budget in ML-engineering agents.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces HASTE, a hierarchical multi-agent system for ML engineering that organizes reusable skills across competitions, achieving higher medal rates and efficiency than flat or no skill loading on MLE-Bench Lite.
AI.motivation: ML engineering agents tackling ML competitions repeatedly waste compute and effort rediscovering already-known techniques because each competition starts from scratch with no structured reuse of prior knowledge. Existing systems lack an effective way to represent, organize, and transfer skills across different problems and domains, leading to low data and compute efficiency.
AI.method: The authors design HASTE, a three-tier hierarchical multi-agent architecture with global, domain, and competition-specific knowledge scopes, each paired with an agent layer. An orchestrator coordinates domain specialist agents and uses LLM-based abstraction to promote learning and transfer between tiers. Skills are represented as structured units and loaded according to scope (tiered loading) rather than as a flat list. They run controlled ablations with a fixed inventory of 159 skills across 8 competitions, comparing tiered versus flat loading and no skills, and evaluate on the MLE-Bench Lite benchmark of 22 Kaggle competitions using Claude Sonnet 4.6, measuring medal rate, token usage, refinement iterations, and acceptance rate of proposed changes under cold-start and warm-start scenarios.
AI.result: In the ablation with a fixed 159-skill inventory over 8 competitions, tiered (scoped) loading achieves a 100% medal rate, while flat loading achieves only 62.5%, equal to the baseline of using no skills, and also consumes twice the output tokens compared to tiered loading. On the full MLE-Bench Lite benchmark (22 Kaggle competitions), HASTE attains a 77.3% medal rate with 12 hours per competition. In warm-start settings where previously learned skills are reloaded at global and domain levels, the system requires 52% fewer refinement iterations than cold starts, and the acceptance rate of proposed code/model changes rises from 42% with a small skill inventory to 85% once more than 50 skills are available.
AI.conclusion: Organizing skills hierarchically across global, domain, and competition-specific scopes substantially improves the effectiveness and efficiency of ML-engineering agents in Kaggle-style competitions. Structured knowledge loading outperforms simply adding more skills in a flat fashion, yielding higher medal rates, reduced token usage, fewer refinement iterations, and higher quality proposals. These findings indicate that careful knowledge organization and transfer can partially compensate for limited model strength and compute resources, suggesting a promising direction for building more capable and efficient ML engineering agents.

title: From Propositional to Perceptual Asymmetry: Extending Frictive Policy Optimization to Asymmetric Partial Information Dialogue
comment: 11 pages. To appear in Proceedings of SIGDIAL 2026
summary: Frictive Policy Optimization (FPO; Pustejovsky et al., 2025) treats friction in collaborative dialogue -- misalignment, misunderstanding, repair -- as an epistemic signal essential to common-ground construction, rather than noise to be minimized. However, FPO and its implementations assume shared perceptual contexts, where friction arises from differently interpreted propositions over the same scene, which we define as propositional asymmetry. We extend FPO to perceptual asymmetry, where participants hold asymmetric partial information and the same referring expression yields different denotations depending on whose information state grounds the reference. We evaluate this through cross-corpora analysis and LLM probing on referentially asymmetric dialogue tasks, primarily the HCRC MapTask (Anderson et al., 1991). We find that FPO's friction functional is empirically valid only when evaluated from within each participant's information horizon: different landmark configurations produce qualitatively distinct grounding failure modes, with a small class of ambiguous configurations driving a disproportionate share of misunderstandings through trajectories that appear successful but silently diverge. The LLM probe confirms that having the "right perspective" matters more than having all perspectives: the informed single viewpoint outperforms omniscient access to both participants' contexts. We propose two annotation refinements: subtype decomposition of pending grounding states and accommodation-aware alignment classification.

AI.tldr: Extends Frictive Policy Optimization from shared to asymmetric perceptual contexts, showing that friction in dialogue must be evaluated from each participant’s information horizon and proposing refined grounding annotations.
AI.motivation: Existing FPO work treats friction (misunderstanding, repair) as epistemically valuable but assumes participants share a perceptual scene, so disagreements are only about proposition interpretation. Real dialogue often involves asymmetric partial information where the same referring expression can denote different entities for each participant. The authors aim to understand how FPO behaves under these more realistic referential asymmetries and to diagnose failure modes in such settings.
AI.method: Conceptually generalize FPO from propositional to perceptual asymmetry, formally modeling participants with distinct, partial information states that yield different denotations for identical referring expressions. Empirically, perform cross-corpora analysis and large language model probing on referentially asymmetric dialogue tasks, focusing on the HCRC MapTask corpus, to examine grounding failures under different landmark configurations and to compare performance of models with single-agent versus omniscient perspectives.
AI.result: They show that the friction functional central to FPO is empirically valid only when computed from within each participant’s own information horizon; globally aggregating perspectives obscures its predictive power. Different map landmark configurations induce qualitatively distinct grounding failure modes, with a small set of inherently ambiguous configurations accounting for a disproportionate share of misunderstandings via trajectories that look successful but actually diverge. LLM experiments further demonstrate that an informed single-perspective model outperforms a model with omniscient access to both participants’ contexts on referential grounding tasks.
AI.conclusion: FPO can extend to perceptual asymmetry, but friction must be evaluated relative to each participant’s local information state rather than a merged global context. Ambiguous landmark configurations play an outsized role in miscommunication, often via stealth divergence. For modeling and annotation of collaborative dialogue, it is better to track perspectival information horizons; the authors propose refined schemes that decompose pending grounding subtypes and add accommodation-aware alignment labels to better capture how common ground is negotiated under asymmetry.

title: RoPoLL: Robust Panel of LLM Judges
summary:
