The LLM Jury, a Panel of LLM Evaluators (PoLL) reporting consensus scores, has become a practical alternative to single-judge LLM evaluation, yet its statistical behavior remains poorly understood. We formalize the LLM Jury under the Huber contamination model and show that PoLL incurs unbounded bias
  under any positive contamination, regardless of jury size, whenever a single judge fails in a biased, LLM-typical way (mode collapse, sycophancy, safety refusal). Framing jury consensus as classical robust mean estimation, we propose RoPoLL (Robust Panel of LLM-as-Judge), which preserves the PoLL
  panel but replaces the aggregation function with a robust mean estimator, instantiated with the geometric median (GM): tuning-free, with the optimal finite-sample breakdown point 1/2. A finite-sample error bound and a matching information-theoretic minimax lower bound agree on the parametric rate
  sigma*sqrt(d/N) and differ on the breakdown floor by a factor of sqrt(d), a statistical-computational gap that polynomial-time RoPoLL pays relative to the intractable Tukey halfspace median. Across 13 open-weight judges (4B-675B), three reward-model benchmarks, and four corruption regimes at rates up
  to 50%, RoPoLL dominates PoLL on every biased corruption type: by about 19% on cross-dimensional attacks at matched compute, and by orders of magnitude on heavy-tailed Byzantine adversaries. A 3-judge RoPoLL committee at 38B beats Mistral-Large-3 (675B) by 1.31x on HelpSteer-2 under 30% bimodal-random
  corruption, an 18x parameter advantage at better accuracy; a Noisy-GT control confirms the premium is paid against biased contamination, not benign imprecision.

AI.tldr: The paper studies statistical robustness of LLM-jury evaluation (PoLL) and shows it suffers unbounded bias under realistic LLM-style judge failures; it then proposes RoPoLL, which uses a robust mean estimator (geometric median) for aggregation, achieving strong theoretical guarantees and large empirical gains under biased corruptions.
AI.motivation: LLM outputs are often evaluated by LLMs acting as judges. Using a panel of LLM judges (PoLL) with consensus scores is popular, but its statistical properties under judge failures or adversarial corruptions are not well-understood. In practice, judges can systematically fail in biased ways (mode collapse, sycophancy, safety refusal), which could severely distort evaluation metrics. There is a need to understand and improve the robustness of LLM-jury evaluation under such contamination.
AI.method: The authors formalize LLM-jury evaluation within the Huber contamination model and analyze the bias behavior of PoLL (standard aggregation such as averaging) when any fraction of judges is corrupted in biased, LLM-typical ways. They then recast jury consensus as a robust mean estimation problem and propose RoPoLL, which keeps the same panel of judges but replaces simple averaging with a robust mean estimator. They instantiate RoPoLL using the geometric median, which is tuning-free and has an optimal finite-sample breakdown point of 1/2. They derive finite-sample error bounds and prove a matching minimax lower bound, identifying a statistical-computational gap relative to an intractable but optimal estimator (Tukey halfspace median). Empirically, they benchmark RoPoLL vs PoLL across multiple open-weight LLM judges, reward-model datasets, and several corruption regimes including heavy-tailed and adversarial attacks.
AI.result: Theoretically, they show that standard PoLL can suffer unbounded bias for any positive contamination level whenever even a single judge fails in a biased way, independent of jury size. RoPoLL with the geometric median achieves parametric error rate sigma*sqrt(d/N), with robustness characterized by a breakdown point of 1/2, and the gap to the information-theoretic optimum is only a sqrt(d) factor in the breakdown floor, attributable to computational constraints. Empirically, across 13 open-weight judges and multiple benchmarks, RoPoLL consistently outperforms PoLL under biased corruptions: it gains about 19% on cross-dimensional attacks at equal compute and delivers orders-of-magnitude improvements under heavy-tailed Byzantine adversaries. A 3-judge RoPoLL committee using 38B parameters outperforms a much larger 675B-parameter model (Mistral-Large-3) by 1.31x on HelpSteer-2 under 30% bimodal-random corruption, demonstrating substantial robustness and efficiency gains. A noisy but unbiased ground-truth control shows that these gains specifically target biased contamination rather than benign noise.
AI.conclusion: Standard LLM-jury evaluation (PoLL) is fundamentally non-robust: even small, realistic biased judge failures can cause unbounded bias regardless of jury size. By reframing consensus as robust mean estimation and using geometric-median aggregation, RoPoLL delivers provable robustness with near-optimal rates and large empirical gains across diverse corruption regimes. This suggests that robust aggregation, rather than larger single judges or larger panels with naive averaging, is key to reliable LLM-as-judge evaluation in the presence of biased or adversarial corruption.

title: Measuring Judgment Quality in Natural-Language Explanations: Evidence from Forecasting Tournaments
summary: Decision-makers routinely rely on expert judgments accompanied by written explanations, yet explanation quality is difficult to measure at scale. Forecasting tournaments offer a natural testing ground: probabilistic judgments are paired with natural-language rationales and scored against realized outcomes. We introduce Explanation Quality Markers (EQMs), a set of sixty theory-guided reasoning patterns scored by large language models (LLMs). In a pre-registered analysis of over 55,000 forecast-rationale pairs from a multiyear forecasting tournament, EQMs predict accuracy at both the forecast and forecaster levels, consistently outperforming pre-LLM text-analysis methods. More than 90% of statistically significant pattern-level EQM-accuracy correlations match our directional hypotheses. The signal is asymmetric: EQMs identify likely underperformers more reliably than they distinguish the very best forecasters. Benchmarked against traditional indicators of forecasting skill, EQMs are the strongest predictor at the forecast level and competitive at the forecaster level, though weaker than prior accuracy. Human ratings of rationale quality are less consistently correlated with accuracy and place disproportionate weight on rationale length. Results transfer to an independent forecasting study. EQMs provide a scalable, interpretable method for extracting judgment-relevant information from written explanations.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Explanation Quality Markers (EQMs)—LLM-scored reasoning patterns in written forecast rationales—and shows they predict forecasting accuracy better than traditional text metrics and human ratings, especially for spotting low performers.
AI.motivation: Decision-makers heavily rely on experts’ written explanations, but there is no scalable, objective way to assess the quality of those explanations. Forecasting tournaments generate large datasets of probabilistic predictions plus rationales and realized outcomes, making them an ideal setting to test whether measurable properties of explanations are linked to judgment quality. Existing pre-LLM text analysis tools and human judgments are limited, motivating a new, more powerful, and interpretable approach based on modern LLMs.
AI.method: The authors define 60 theory-driven reasoning patterns, called Explanation Quality Markers (EQMs), and use large language models to automatically score the presence or degree of each marker in forecast rationales. Using a pre-registered analysis on over 55,000 forecast–rationale pairs from a multiyear forecasting tournament, they correlate EQM scores with realized forecasting accuracy at both the individual forecast and forecaster levels. They compare EQMs to traditional text-analysis features and to human quality ratings, test directional hypotheses about pattern-accuracy relationships, and benchmark EQMs against standard indicators of forecasting skill. They then evaluate how well the results generalize to an independent forecasting dataset.
AI.result: EQMs significantly predict forecasting accuracy for both individual forecasts and forecasters, outperforming traditional pre-LLM text features. Over 90% of statistically significant EQM–accuracy correlations conform to the authors’ hypothesized directions, supporting the construct validity of the markers. The predictive signal is asymmetric: EQMs are more reliable at identifying likely underperformers than at distinguishing top performers. At the forecast level, EQMs are the strongest predictor compared to standard skill indicators; at the forecaster level, they are competitive but less informative than prior accuracy. Human ratings of rationale quality show weaker and less consistent relationships with accuracy and are strongly biased toward rationale length. The EQM-based findings replicate in a separate forecasting study, indicating robustness and transferability.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based Explanation Quality Markers offer a scalable, interpretable way to evaluate the quality of written forecasting explanations and to extract information that is predictive of judgment accuracy. They outperform traditional automated text metrics and naive human quality assessments, particularly for detecting low-performing forecasts and forecasters. EQMs can thus help decision-makers and organizations better assess and triage expert input at scale, though prior accuracy remains crucial for ranking top forecasters. The approach generalizes across datasets, suggesting broad applicability to other domains where written rationales accompany probabilistic judgments.

title: AgRefactor: Self-Evolving Agentic Workflow for HLS Compatibility and Performance
summary: High-Level Synthesis (HLS) provides a fast path from concepts to silicon, but converting real-world software into synthesizable HLS code remains challenging due to restrictive language support and the gap between software and hardware programming practices. Existing automated and LLM-based refactoring approaches partially address this problem, yet they often lack flexibility, struggle to scale, and incur high computational costs. We introduce AgRefactor, an LLM-based multi-agent workflow for refactoring software into HLS-compatible programs. AgRefactor incorporates a self-evolving memory system that accumulates and retrieves factual and strategic knowledge across tasks, improving robustness and efficiency on unseen programs. To reduce cost and enhance scalability, it integrates automated refactoring tools, enabling agents to balance LLM-driven rewrites with efficient tool-based transformations. On 9 out of 11 challenging real-world benchmarks, which are 5-10x longer than the most complex cases studied in prior work, AgRefactor outperforms or matches the state-of-the-art automated refactoring tool and a strong LLM-based baseline built on the same framework backbone. Further agentic performance optimization yields a 6.51x geometric mean speedup over the SoTA pragma tuning tool and a 1.20x speedup over optimized open-source designs with less than 20% extra resources. AgRefactor is fully-automated and open-sourced.

AI.tldr: AgRefactor is an LLM-based multi-agent system that automatically refactors real-world software into HLS-compatible code more flexibly, scalably, and efficiently than prior tools.
AI.motivation: Converting practical software into synthesizable High-Level Synthesis (HLS) code is difficult because of limited HLS language support, differences between software and hardware coding styles, and the limited flexibility, scalability, and high cost of current automated or LLM-based refactoring methods.
AI.method: The authors build AgRefactor, a multi-agent LLM workflow that refactors software into HLS-ready programs. It uses a self-evolving memory system to store and retrieve factual and strategic knowledge across tasks, and tightly integrates classical automated refactoring tools so that agents can decide when to rely on LLM-driven code rewrites versus tool-based transformations to reduce cost and improve scalability.
AI.result: On 9 of 11 challenging real-world benchmarks that are 5–10× longer than those in earlier work, AgRefactor matches or surpasses both a state-of-the-art automated refactoring tool and a strong LLM-based baseline using the same backbone framework. After additional agentic optimization, the system delivers a 6.51× geometric mean speedup over the state-of-the-art pragma tuning tool and a 1.20× speedup over optimized open-source HLS designs, with under 20% extra hardware resource usage.
AI.conclusion: AgRefactor demonstrates that an LLM-based multi-agent workflow with persistent memory and integrated automated tools can robustly and cost-effectively refactor large, real-world software into HLS-compatible code, achieving better or comparable performance to existing SoTA approaches while remaining fully automated and open-source.

title: Wait, am I Being Fair? Characterizing Deductive Stereotyping and Mitigating It with Fair-GCG
summary: Warning: This paper contains several toxic and offensive statements. While reasoning generally improves fairness in recent large language models (LLMs), failures persist. In this work, we identify a failure mode, deductive stereotyping, in which models apply population-level statistical regularities to individual cases, producing logically coherent yet socially biased inferences. We provide a statistical interpretation of this phenomenon. To steer models toward fairness-aware reasoning, we propose a reasoning-time injection framework. We further introduce Fair-GCG to systematically discover effective injection phrases. Injection phrases discovered by Fair-GCG improve performance across multiple fairness benchmarks, generalize from smaller to larger LLMs, improves reasoning-level fairness, reduces bias in open-ended generation, and transfer to real-world fairness-sensitive tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies a new fairness failure mode in LLM reasoning, called deductive stereotyping, and proposes a reasoning-time intervention method with automatically found prompts (Fair-GCG) that improves fairness across tasks and models.
AI.motivation: Although recent LLMs show improved fairness, they still produce biased reasoning. The authors notice a specific pattern where models use population-level statistics to draw logically consistent but unfair conclusions about individuals. They aim to formalize this issue and develop methods that directly steer the reasoning process toward fairness, not just the final outputs.
AI.method: 1) Conceptual and statistical characterization of “deductive stereotyping,” where models treat group-level correlations as deductive rules about individuals. 2) A reasoning-time injection framework that introduces additional instructions or constraints during chain-of-thought to encourage fairness-aware reasoning. 3) Fair-GCG, an automated search (e.g., gradient-guided or score-based) method to discover effective injection phrases that, when added to the reasoning, improve fairness metrics. 4) Empirical evaluation across fairness benchmarks, model scales, open-ended generation, and some real-world tasks.
AI.result: The discovered injection phrases consistently improve fairness scores on multiple benchmarks, and these phrases generalize from small to larger LLMs. The interventions reduce deductive stereotyping in the reasoning trace itself, lessen biased content in open-ended outputs, and remain effective on external fairness-sensitive tasks beyond the training/evaluation setup used to find the phrases.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning-time steering with automatically discovered injection phrases is an effective and scalable way to mitigate a specific LLM failure mode—deductive stereotyping—leading to more fairness-aware reasoning and less biased outputs across models and tasks.

title: Neuro-Bayesian-Symbolic Residual Attention Shallow Network: Explainable Deep Learning for Cybersecurity Risk Assessment
comment: 19 pages, Submitted to IJCCC (Online ISSN 1841-9844, ISSN-L 1841-9836), June 25, 2026
summary: We introduce the Neuro-Bayesian-Symbolic Residual Attention Shallow Network (NBS-RASN), a hybrid neural architecture for explainable cybersecurity risk assessment in open-source ecosystems. Unlike deep models that trade interpretability for accuracy, our shallow network encodes domain knowledge, causal reasoning, and expert judgment as differentiable components. It uses 80 interpretable neurons across 12 layers, including a gatekeeper that enforces five epistemological axioms - precision, causality, falsifiability, transparency, and completeness - as hard constraints before propagation. Despite limited depth, the network exhibits deep-learning traits via residual attention and feedback loops, learning complex risk patterns without becoming a black box. It produces fully decomposable scores: a deterministic weighted component plus an expert adjustment, with each adjustment traceable to named amplifiers (blast radius, propagation speed, structural nature, default exposure, exploitation pattern, institutional criticality). We validate on 20 open-source projects covering all OWASP Top 10:2025 categories and language risk classes, achieving confidence scores of 0.79-0.97, and show that explainability is guaranteed by design, not by a training algorithm. This challenges the assumption that deep learning requires deep networks, proving that shallow networks with deep reasoning can outperform opaque models in high-stakes cybersecurity, where interpretability is essential.

AI.tldr: Introduces NBS-RASN, a hybrid shallow neural network for explainable cybersecurity risk assessment that encodes domain knowledge and epistemological axioms as differentiable constraints, achieving high confidence while remaining interpretable.
AI.motivation: Cybersecurity risk assessment, especially in open-source ecosystems, requires both high predictive performance and strong interpretability because decisions are high-stakes. Traditional deep learning models are often black boxes, making it difficult to justify or audit risk scores. The paper is motivated by the need to reconcile deep-learning-like pattern recognition with rigorous explainability grounded in domain knowledge, causality, and expert judgment, and to challenge the assumption that only deep architectures can capture complex patterns.
AI.method: The authors propose the Neuro-Bayesian-Symbolic Residual Attention Shallow Network (NBS-RASN), a 12-layer but shallow neural architecture with 80 interpretable neurons. The model integrates symbolic domain knowledge and causal reasoning as differentiable components. A key element is a "gatekeeper" layer that enforces five epistemological axioms—precision, causality, falsifiability, transparency, and completeness—as hard constraints before information propagates. The network includes residual attention mechanisms and feedback loops to emulate deep-learning behavior while keeping the architecture small and interpretable. Risk scores are decomposed into a deterministic weighted base component and an explicit expert adjustment, where each adjustment is mapped to named amplifiers such as blast radius, propagation speed, structural nature, default exposure, exploitation pattern, and institutional criticality.
AI.result: Validated on 20 open-source projects that collectively span all OWASP Top 10:2025 vulnerability categories and various programming language risk classes, the NBS-RASN achieves confidence scores between 0.79 and 0.97. The model demonstrates that it can capture complex risk patterns while remaining fully interpretable, with every part of the output traceable to specific neurons and expert-defined amplifiers. The results indicate that performance comparable to or better than deep black-box models is achievable with a shallow, knowledge-rich architecture.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that deep learning capabilities do not strictly require deep networks; instead, shallow networks augmented with explicit domain knowledge, causal reasoning, and residual attention can achieve deep-like performance while preserving interpretability. NBS-RASN shows that explainability can be guaranteed by architectural design rather than post-hoc methods. This has important implications for high-stakes domains such as cybersecurity, where transparent and auditable risk assessments are crucial and opaque models are often unacceptable.

title: CORTEX: Token-Level Hallucination Detection in RAG via Comparative Internal Representations
summary: In this paper, we propose CORTEX, a token-level hallucination detection method for Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). In long-form RAG outputs, hallucinations often arise in localized spans rather than throughout an entire response. CORTEX therefore identifies ungrounded content at the token level, enabling fine-grained localization of hallucinations. The key intuition behind CORTEX is that tokens grounded in retrieved documents should be more strongly influenced by those documents than hallucinated tokens. To capture this document-induced effect, CORTEX compares internal representations of a large language model (LLM) under two conditions: with and without the retrieved documents. Instead of relying solely on each token's immediate sensitivity to the retrieved documents, CORTEX also leverages the propagation of document-grounded information through preceding tokens, reducing false positives for tokens whose evidence has already been absorbed into the context. Finally, CORTEX applies post-processing smoothing step that models the tendency of hallucination labels to persist over contiguous spans, reducing local noise and encouraging span-consistent predictions. Experiments on two RAG benchmarks and three LLMs show that CORTEX substantially improves token-level hallucination detection, with each component consistently contributing to performance gains.

AI.tldr: CORTEX is a token-level hallucination detector for RAG that compares an LLM’s internal representations with and without retrieved documents, then smooths predictions over spans to accurately localize hallucinated tokens.
AI.motivation: In Retrieval-Augmented Generation, hallucinations often occur only in parts of a long answer, not the whole response. Existing detectors are typically coarse-grained (e.g., at sentence or response level) and may miss localized hallucinations or over-penalize entire outputs. There is a need for fine-grained, token-level hallucination detection that can reliably identify which parts of a response are unsupported by retrieved evidence, improving trust and allowing selective use or editing of generated content.
AI.method: CORTEX operates at the token level by analyzing the internal hidden states of an LLM under two conditions: (1) when the model generates with access to retrieved documents, and (2) when it generates without them. It measures how strongly each token’s representation is influenced by the presence of the documents. Beyond per-token sensitivity, it models how document-grounded information propagates through preceding tokens, so tokens whose evidence is already in the context are not mislabeled as hallucinations. Finally, it applies a post-processing smoothing step that treats hallucination labels as span-like, enforcing local consistency and reducing noisy label flips across adjacent tokens.
AI.result: On two RAG benchmarks and three different LLMs, CORTEX achieves substantially better token-level hallucination detection performance compared to baselines. Ablation-style analysis shows that each part of the approach—the document/no-document representation comparison, the modeling of information propagation through context, and the smoothing over contiguous spans—provides consistent improvements, confirming their individual contributions.
AI.conclusion: CORTEX provides an effective, fine-grained method to detect hallucinations in RAG outputs by focusing on token-level grounding signals derived from internal LLM representations. Its design, which accounts for both direct document influence and contextual information flow, together with span-level smoothing, leads to more accurate localization of unsupported content. This makes it a practical tool for enhancing reliability and controllability of RAG systems in long-form generation settings.

title: HyPOLE: Hyperproperty-Guided Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning under Partial Observation
summary: Formal specification is a powerful tool to guide the learning process and provides significant advantages over reward shaping: (1) mathematical rigor; (2) expressiveness to specify objectives and constraints, and (3) the ability to define tactics to achieve objectives. However, these benefits remain largely unexplored in the context of Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL). This paper introduces HyPOLE, a novel framework for MARL under partial observability, where learning is guided by the expressive power of the so-called hyperproperties and, in particular, the temporal logic HyperLTL. We integrate Centralized Training for Decentralized Execution (CTDE) techniques with HyPOLE to synthesize decentralized policies, and our evaluation on SMAC, MessySMAC, and WildFire benchmark demonstrates clear advantages over baselines.

AI.tldr: HyPOLE is a framework that uses formal specification and HyperLTL to guide learning in partially observable multi-agent reinforcement learning, outperforming baselines on standard benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Formal specification offers rigor, expressiveness for objectives/constraints, and the ability to encode tactics, but has been underused in MARL, especially under partial observability.
AI.method: Introduce HyPOLE, which leverages hyperproperties via HyperLTL to formally specify objectives and constraints in MARL, and combine it with Centralized Training with Decentralized Execution (CTDE) to learn decentralized policies under partial observability.
AI.result: On SMAC, MessySMAC, and WildFire benchmarks, HyPOLE shows clear performance improvements compared to existing MARL baselines.
AI.conclusion: Guiding MARL with formal hyperproperty specifications like HyperLTL within a CTDE scheme is effective for partially observable settings and yields better-performing decentralized policies than conventional approaches.

title: Truth or Sophistry? LoFa: A Benchmark for LLM Robustness Against Logical Fallacies
comment: Accepted to ACL 2026 Main. 33 pages (9 pages main text)
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit strong semantic capabilities, yet their resilience to manipulative linguistic patterns such as logical fallacies remains underexplored. Prior work has primarily examined whether LLMs can identify or classify fallacies, leaving their robustness against fallacious persuasion insufficiently studied. To address this gap, we introduce LoFa (Logical Fallacy), a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating LLM robustness against fallacies. LoFa is constructed through a multi-agent pipeline that pairs factual questions with fallacious arguments, and is accompanied by a multi-round debate framework for assessing model resilience under sustained adversarial persuasion. To disentangle fallacy robustness from a model's inherent knowledge limitations, we further propose Logical Fallacy Resistance at k (LFR@k), a metric that quantifies resistance to fallacious attacks. Experiments show that LLMs exhibit varying levels of robustness across different fallacy types, revealing distinct vulnerability profiles among models.

AI.tldr: The paper presents LoFa, a benchmark and evaluation framework to test how robust large language models are against logical fallacies and fallacious persuasion.
AI.motivation: While LLMs are good at semantics and can often detect or classify logical fallacies, we do not know how easily they themselves can be persuaded or misled by fallacious arguments. There is a gap between fallacy recognition and actual robustness to fallacious persuasion, especially under multi-turn adversarial interaction.
AI.method: The authors build LoFa, a benchmark created via a multi-agent generation pipeline that couples factual questions with associated fallacious arguments of various types. They design a multi-round debate setting where models are exposed to sustained adversarial, fallacy-laden persuasion. To separate robustness from simple knowledge gaps, they propose a metric, LFR@k, that quantifies how resistant a model is to fallacious attacks over k persuasive attempts.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across multiple LLMs on LoFa shows that models differ substantially in how robust they are to different categories of logical fallacies, leading to distinct vulnerability patterns for each model. This reveals that current LLMs are not uniformly robust to manipulative reasoning and that susceptibility is structured by fallacy type.
AI.conclusion: LoFa and the LFR@k metric provide tools for systematically probing and comparing LLM robustness to logical fallacies, going beyond mere detection or classification. The findings highlight that LLMs have heterogeneous and sometimes pronounced weaknesses to specific fallacies, underscoring the need for targeted robustness improvements against manipulative language.

title: AgentBound: Verifiable Behavioral Governance for Autonomous AI Agents
summary:
Autonomous AI agents increasingly perform consequential actions on behalf of human principals, including financial transactions, external communications, and enterprise workflows. Existing agent infrastructure relies on identity federation and delegated authorization to authenticate workloads and control resource access, but it cannot determine whether an authorized action should be executed under the current behavioral and operational context.
  We present AgentBound, a runtime governance framework that provides verifiable behavioral oversight for autonomous AI agents. AgentBound evaluates each proposed action using three independent authorities: delegated authorization, owner-signed behavioral constitutions, and site action contracts. Their judgments are conservatively composed through a formal decision model to determine whether an action should be permitted, reviewed, or denied before execution.
  To provide accountability, AgentBound generates cryptographically verifiable governance receipts that bind every action to the exact delegation, policy, and semantic artifacts governing the decision, enabling independent replay verification and policy provenance. The framework also introduces standing delegation for long-running agents, allowing periodic workloads to operate under continuously refreshed governance policies while preserving revocability and bounded authority.
  We present the formal foundation, system architecture, governance receipt protocol, and AgentBound-Bench, a benchmark framework for evaluating governance correctness, authority composition, and accountability. Rather than replacing model alignment, AgentBound complements it by providing a deterministic governance layer between authorization and execution, transforming governance from a process that must be trusted into one that can be independently verified.

AI.tldr: AgentBound is a runtime governance framework that decides, in a verifiable way, whether an autonomous AI agent’s proposed action should be executed, reviewed, or denied, and produces cryptographic receipts for every decision.
AI.motivation: Autonomous AI agents can already authenticate and get authorization to perform actions (e.g., via identity federation and delegated auth), but current systems lack a way to decide if an authorized action is appropriate in the current behavioral and operational context. This gap creates risks: agents may take harmful, out-of-scope, or policy-violating actions while still being technically authorized. There is also limited accountability and verifiability of why a given action was allowed. The paper aims to provide a principled, verifiable governance layer between “agent is authorized” and “action is executed.”
AI.method: The authors design AgentBound, a runtime governance framework where every agent action is evaluated by three distinct authorities: (1) delegated authorization (standard access control / identity delegation), (2) owner-signed behavioral constitutions that specify how the agent should behave, and (3) site action contracts that encode site-specific rules for actions. They define a formal decision model that conservatively composes these three judgments into a decision: permit, require review, or deny. They also define a protocol for cryptographically verifiable governance receipts that bind each action to the active delegation, policies, and semantic artifacts used to decide. The system supports standing delegation to allow long-running agents to keep operating under refreshed policies while keeping revocation and bounded authority. They implement the architecture, formal foundations, and a benchmark suite (AgentBound-Bench) to test governance correctness, composition of authorities, and accountability properties.
AI.result: The framework enables runtime oversight over autonomous agent actions beyond basic authorization checks. AgentBound can systematically determine whether an action should proceed, be reviewed, or be blocked based on formally composed judgments from multiple authorities. The governance receipt protocol provides cryptographically verifiable evidence of which delegations and policies applied to each action, supporting replay verification and policy provenance. The benchmark (AgentBound-Bench) allows evaluation of correctness of governance decisions, how multiple authorities interact, and the resulting accountability. While the abstract does not give numerical results, it claims that AgentBound provides deterministic, verifiable governance distinct from and complementary to model alignment techniques.
AI.conclusion: AgentBound introduces a deterministic and formally grounded governance layer between authorization and execution for autonomous AI agents. By combining delegated authorization, behavioral constitutions, and site action contracts, and by issuing cryptographically verifiable governance receipts, it transforms governance from a mechanism that must be implicitly trusted into one that can be independently checked and audited. The system is not a replacement for alignment but a complementary layer that enforces runtime behavioral constraints and supplies strong accountability for agent actions.

title: A Semantic-Layer-Mediated Agent for Natural Language to SQL over Heterogeneous Enterprise Databases
comment: Submitted to FITAT 2026 for peer review
summary: Natural language-to-SQL (NL2SQL) over real-world enterprise databases remains significantly more challenging than on academic benchmarks. Enterprise schemas often contain hundreds of physical tables with cryptic column names, heterogeneous SQL dialects, and complex analytical workloads requiring nested aggregations, temporal reasoning, and multi-table joins. We present a semantic-layer-mediated NL2SQL agent that decouples semantic intent from physical SQL execution. Rather than generating SQL directly over raw schemas, the agent reasons over a curated semantic layer through a compact intermediate representation called the Semantic Model Query (SMQ). A deterministic compiler translates each SMQ into dialect-specific SQL, providing verified building blocks that the agent composes into the final query. The system employs a constrained think-act loop, supports SQLite, BigQuery, and Snowflake backends, and is integrated into an end-to-end evaluation framework. Using Gemini 3 Pro, the system achieves 94.15% execution accuracy on the 547-task Spider2-snow benchmark, ranking third on the official leaderboard and substantially outperforming schema-only approaches. We describe the system architecture, SMQ representation, agent workflow, evaluation results, and discuss semantic-layer quality and the trade-off between improved grounding and overfitting.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a semantic-layer-mediated NL2SQL agent that uses an intermediate representation (SMQ) and a deterministic compiler to generate accurate, dialect-specific SQL for complex enterprise databases, achieving state-of-the-art results on Spider2-snow.
AI.motivation: Real-world enterprise NL2SQL is harder than academic benchmarks due to large, cryptic schemas, multiple SQL dialects, and complex analytical workloads. Existing schema-only approaches struggle with grounding user intent to physical databases while remaining robust across dialects.
AI.method: The authors design an NL2SQL agent that operates over a curated semantic layer instead of raw database schemas. User questions are mapped to a compact intermediate representation called the Semantic Model Query (SMQ). A deterministic compiler then translates SMQs into dialect-specific SQL (for SQLite, BigQuery, and Snowflake). The agent uses a constrained think-act loop and composes verified SQL building blocks produced by the compiler. The system is embedded in an end-to-end evaluation framework.
AI.result: Using Gemini 3 Pro as the underlying LLM, the system attains 94.15% execution accuracy on the 547-task Spider2-snow benchmark, placing third on the official leaderboard and significantly outperforming methods that rely only on schema information without a semantic layer.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling semantic intent from physical SQL via a semantic layer and SMQ intermediate representation greatly improves robustness and accuracy for NL2SQL on complex enterprise-style workloads. The semantic layer and deterministic compiler provide better grounding and dialect portability, though there is a trade-off between leveraging rich semantics and potential overfitting to the curated layer’s design and quality.

title: When Regulation Has Memory: Hysteresis and Control Burden in Artificial Agency
summary:
Adaptive agents are usually judged by what they do, but an agent can appear stable while the internal effort required to keep it stable is increasing. This hidden regulatory burden matters for artificial agents operating under noise, delay, or changing demands: two systems may reach similar internal states while one requires much more corrective control to get there.
  Here, we study whether that burden depends on history. Using a computational model of adaptive uncertainty regulation, we drive an artificial agent through a continuous change in its uncertainty target and then reverse the change without resetting the agent. This creates a simple test for carryover: does the controller respond only to the current target, or does the path by which the agent reached that target still matter?
  The simulations show a clear history-dependent effect. The adaptive gain required to regulate the agent forms a reproducible hysteresis loop, meaning that the same target can require different levels of control depending on whether the agent is moving toward or returning from a more demanding regime. The timing of regulation also matters. When stabilization is available before disturbance exposure, the agent generally requires less adaptive gain than when it can only recover after disturbance has already acted.
  The state-level coherence measure also shows path dependence, but the timing effect is much clearer in regulatory gain. The main difference is therefore not that anticipatory regulation produces a completely different state. Rather, it reaches comparable regulated behavior with lower modeled control demand. These results suggest that adaptive agents should be evaluated not only by whether they remain organized, but by how much regulation they must recruit to do so.

AI.tldr: The paper uses simulations of an adaptive uncertainty-regulation controller to show that maintaining stable behavior can hide a history-dependent regulatory burden: the same target state can require different internal control depending on the path and timing of regulation.
AI.motivation: Typical evaluations of adaptive agents focus on observable stability or performance, ignoring how much hidden regulatory effort is needed to maintain that stability. This is problematic in noisy, delayed, or changing environments where two agents with similar behavior may incur very different internal control costs. The authors aim to expose and quantify this hidden regulatory burden and to determine whether it depends on the agent’s history and on when regulation is applied.
AI.method: The authors build a computational model of an artificial agent that adaptively regulates its uncertainty around a target level. They then manipulate the target uncertainty continuously in one direction and subsequently reverse the change without resetting the agent, creating a path with increasing then decreasing ‘demand.’ They measure the adaptive gain (a proxy for control effort) and a state-level coherence metric as the target changes, comparing cases where regulation is applied anticipatorily (before disturbance exposure) versus reactively (after disturbance). The resulting trajectories are analyzed for hysteresis and timing-dependent effects.
AI.result: The simulations reveal a pronounced hysteresis loop in adaptive gain: for the same uncertainty target, the required adaptive gain is higher when the agent is returning from a more demanding regime than when it is moving toward it. This indicates that internal control demands depend on the agent’s history. Timing also matters: when the agent can stabilize in advance of disturbances, it generally needs less adaptive gain than when it must recover after disturbances have acted. State-level coherence likewise shows path dependence, but the history and timing effects are more strongly expressed in the adaptive gain measure than in the state variables themselves.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that adaptive regulation in artificial agents is history- and timing-dependent: similar observable states can mask different levels of internal control effort. Anticipatory regulation reduces the required adaptive gain compared to purely reactive regulation, even when both achieve comparably organized behavior. Therefore, evaluations of adaptive agents should consider not only whether they maintain organization but also the hidden regulatory burden they incur—captured here by adaptive gain and its hysteresis—highlighting the importance of internal control costs and regulatory timing in the design and assessment of adaptive systems.

title: Reference-Based Prosody and Rhythm Evaluation for Spoken Dialogue Systems
summary: Speech-to-speech (S2S) AI agents are advancing rapidly, yet evaluation lacks interpretable speech-native measures for conversational prosody and rhythm. Because $F_0$, speaking rate, articulation rate, and pausing shift with model-predicted speaker traits and interaction state, pooled human statistics can be poorly calibrated for evaluating a particular output. Using 4000+ hours of dyadic English conversation from the Seamless Interaction dataset, we construct matched reference regimes for $F_0$ mean, $F_0$ expressivity, speech rate, articulation rate, pause ratio, and mean pause duration. We then define a percentile-based evaluation protocol: extract the same metrics from an S2S output waveform, compare them to the closest matched human reference stratum, and report percentile deviations or 5th-95th percentile out-of-regime flags. On held-out human rows, pooled references over-flag state-conditioned $F_0$ expressivity and rhythm, while matched references return flag rates closer to the nominal 10% and make deviation direction interpretable. These outputs serve as behavioral plausibility checks that complement, rather than replace, perceptual and user-centered evaluation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an interpretable, speech-native evaluation protocol for conversational prosody and rhythm in speech-to-speech AI agents using percentile-based comparisons to matched human reference regimes.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of speech-to-speech AI systems lack interpretable, speech-native metrics for conversational prosody and rhythm. Simple pooling of human speech statistics across speakers and interaction states leads to miscalibrated baselines, because features such as F0, speaking and articulation rate, and pausing depend on speaker traits and conversational state. As a result, we need evaluation methods that account for these conditioning factors so that deviations in AI-generated speech can be interpreted as plausible or implausible relative to appropriate human behavior.
AI.method: The authors use over 4000 hours of dyadic English conversation from the Seamless Interaction dataset. They construct matched human reference regimes for several prosodic and rhythmic metrics: mean F0, F0 expressivity, speech rate, articulation rate, pause ratio, and mean pause duration. For a given S2S output waveform, they extract the same metrics and then locate the closest-matched human reference stratum. They compare the output metrics to that matched stratum using percentiles, reporting either percentile deviations within the regime or flags when metrics fall outside the 5th–95th percentile range, indicating out-of-regime behavior.
AI.result: On held-out human conversational segments, using pooled human reference statistics leads to excessive flagging of state-conditioned F0 expressivity and rhythm as abnormal. In contrast, using matched reference regimes produces flag rates that are closer to the intended nominal 10% (5% on each side). This indicates that the matched-regime approach more accurately reflects natural variability and reduces false positives in detecting abnormal prosody and rhythm.
AI.conclusion: The proposed percentile-based evaluation protocol, grounded in matched human prosodic and rhythmic reference regimes, provides interpretable behavioral plausibility checks for speech-to-speech AI outputs. It more accurately captures natural conversational variability than pooled baselines and yields directionally interpretable deviations. The authors position this method as a complement to, not a replacement for, perceptual and user-centered evaluations of S2S agents.

title: A Three-Phase Foundation Model for Tax-Aware Personalized Portfolio Management
summary: We present a three-phase deep reinforcement learning system for personalized portfolio management that addresses three limitations shared by all prior financial RL work: 1) ticker lock-in, 2) monolithic objectives , and 3) static user models. Phase 1 pretrains a ticker-identity-free cross asset encoder via self-supervised learning on a multi-asset corpus, augmented by a frozen parallel branch using Chronos, a T5-based time series foundation model, fused via a learned gating mechanism. To our knowledge, this is the first application of a time series foundation model to portfolio management RL. The encoder generalizes to any publicly traded asset via a 50-dimensional observable metadata vector that requires no retraining for new tickers. Phase 2 fine-tunes a MoE (Mixture of Experts) portfolio actor critic with PPO under an objective-conditioned reward that simultaneously serves six distinct investment goals sampled per episode: short-term alpha, short-term gain, long-term gain, capital preservation, tax-loss harvesting, and long-term-gains-only. A MoE architecture assigns each objective to a specialized expert head (momentum, growth, defensive, tax-aware), and a learned intent router blends experts based on the active objective and current market regime, which eliminates cross-objective gradient conflict. Phase 3 adds a lightweight personalization layer further adapted at inference time to each individual via a 76-parameter LoRA module fine-tuned on real brokerage transaction history, inferring investment objectives from revealed trading behavior rather than questionnaires. A natural language intent parser converts free-form goals directly into structured investment objective parameters.

AI.tldr: A three-phase deep RL framework for personalized portfolio management that generalizes across tickers, optimizes multiple investment objectives via MoE actor-critic, and personalizes at inference-time using LoRA tuned on real user trading histories.
AI.motivation: Prior financial RL methods suffer from three core issues: they are tied to fixed ticker sets (ticker lock-in), optimize a single monolithic objective that cannot flexibly represent diverse investment goals, and rely on static or questionnaire-based user models that do not adapt to real behavior over time. The paper aims to build a more general, flexible, and truly personalized RL-based portfolio manager.
AI.method:
Phase 1: Pretrain a ticker-agnostic cross-asset encoder using self-supervised learning on a multi-asset time series corpus, augmented with representations from Chronos (a T5-based time-series foundation model). The two branches are fused with a learned gating mechanism, and the encoder leverages a 50-D metadata vector so it can generalize to unseen tickers without retraining.

Phase 2: Train a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) portfolio actor-critic with PPO, under an objective-conditioned reward that randomly samples one of six investment goals per episode (short-term alpha, short-term gain, long-term gain, capital preservation, tax-loss harvesting, long-term-gains-only). Each objective maps to a specialized expert (momentum, growth, defensive, tax-aware), and a learned intent router mixes experts based on both the active objective and market regime to mitigate gradient conflict between objectives.

Phase 3: Add a lightweight personalization layer: a 76-parameter LoRA module adapted at inference time per user, fine-tuned on that user’s historical brokerage transactions to infer latent investment preferences. A natural-language intent parser maps free-text user goals into structured objective parameters that condition the policy.
AI.result: The abstract claims (though does not quantify) that the system can: (1) generalize across any publicly traded asset without retraining, (2) flexibly optimize among six heterogeneous investment objectives without cross-objective gradient interference via MoE and objective conditioning, and (3) achieve fine-grained personalization from observed trading behavior plus natural language intents by adapting a small LoRA module per user. It also highlights the novelty of using a time-series foundation model (Chronos) in portfolio management RL.
AI.conclusion: The proposed three-phase RL framework overcomes key limitations of earlier financial RL by decoupling asset identity, supporting multi-objective optimization via a Mixture-of-Experts actor-critic, and enabling dynamic, behavior-driven personalization at inference time. The approach suggests that combining foundation time-series models, objective-conditioned rewards, and lightweight user-specific adapters can yield scalable, personalized portfolio management systems that flexibly handle diverse user goals and new assets without retraining the core model.

title: Exploring the relationship between team institutional composition and novelty in academic papers based on fine-grained knowledge entities
summary: The composition of author teams is an important factor influencing the novelty of academic papers. However, existing studies have paid limited attention to the role of institutional composition, and most novelty measures remain at a general level, making it difficult to explain the specific sources and types of novelty in papers. Taking the field of natural language processing as an example, this study investigates the relationship between team institutional composition and the fine-grained novelty of academic papers. Author teams are classified into three types: academic institutions, industrial institutions, and mixed academic and industrial institutions. Four types of fine-grained knowledge entities are extracted from full-text papers, including methods, datasets, tools, and metrics. The novelty of papers is then measured based on entity combinations, and pairwise combinations of different entity types are further analyzed to examine their contributions to novel papers. The results show that, in the field of natural language processing, collaboration between industrial and academic institutions is more likely to produce novel papers than purely industrial collaboration. From the perspective of fine-grained knowledge entities, mixed academic and industrial teams pay more attention to the novelty of method-metric combinations, whereas industrial teams pay more attention to the novelty of method-tool combinations. This study reveals the relationship between institutional team composition and paper novelty through fine-grained novelty measurement, providing useful evidence for improving paper quality and promoting industry-academia-research collaboration.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how the institutional composition of author teams (academic, industrial, mixed) in NLP affects paper novelty, using fine-grained knowledge entities and their combinations.
AI.motivation: Existing work links team composition to novelty but overlooks how institutions (academia vs industry) matter, and uses coarse novelty measures that do not reveal which concrete technical elements (methods, datasets, tools, metrics) drive novelty.
AI.method: Focus on NLP papers and classify author teams into academic-only, industrial-only, and mixed academic–industrial. From full texts, automatically extract four types of knowledge entities (methods, datasets, tools, metrics). Define a novelty metric based on how new particular entity combinations are (e.g., method–dataset pairs) compared to prior literature, and analyze which team types are more associated with novel combinations overall and for each pairwise entity type.
AI.result: Mixed academic–industrial teams generate more novel papers than purely industrial teams, but not necessarily more than purely academic ones in all respects. Mixed teams especially emphasize novel method–metric combinations, whereas industrial-only teams emphasize novel method–tool combinations.
AI.conclusion: Institutional team composition significantly shapes where novelty arises in NLP papers. Mixed academic–industrial teams are particularly effective for certain types of innovation (e.g., method–metric), and fine-grained, entity-level novelty analysis can clarify how different collaborations contribute to scientific innovation and guide strategies to improve paper quality and industry–academia cooperation.

title: Beyond Compilation: Evaluating Faithful Natural-Language-to-Lean Statement Formalization
summary: Theorem-proving benchmarks evaluate proof search against fixed formal statements, but natural-language-to-Lean formalization must generate the formal statement itself. In this setting, compilation is only a validity check: a Lean declaration may type-check while omitting hypotheses, changing domains, or expressing a vacuous claim. We study faithful statement formalization as both an evaluation problem and a bottleneck-attribution problem. On a 400-entry graduate-level benchmark spanning real analysis, complex analysis, topology, and algebra, our protocol combines Lean compilation, cross-model semantic judging, and human expert calibration. The resulting picture is different from compile-rate evaluation: a full tool-augmented agent reaches 89.5% compilation but only 60.5% consensus faithfulness, exposing a 29.0-point compile-pass but consensus-unfaithful gap. Targeted human audits support the metric as a conservative decision boundary: across available case-level audits, 96.0% of consensus-positive outputs are human-confirmed faithful, while 82.4% of compile-pass consensus-negative outputs are human-confirmed semantic failures. Under this metric, existing one-shot formalizer models and prover-oriented Lean models remain low, suggesting that formal validity, proof-oriented Lean competence, and faithful statement generation should be reported separately. We then use a full $2^3$ factorial design to decompose three recurring interventions in formalization pipelines: parametric expert drafting, Mathlib/context search, and Lean elaboration feedback. Elaboration feedback is the largest validity intervention, but it also exposes a larger compile-pass semantic-failure bucket; search mainly improves grounding and selectivity; and fine-tuned drafting is largely substitutable in this tool stack once feedback and grounding are available.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how well systems convert natural-language math statements into faithful Lean formalizations, showing that compile success overestimates true semantic correctness and proposing a more reliable evaluation protocol plus an intervention analysis.
AI.motivation: In natural-language-to-Lean tasks, current benchmarks and metrics mostly rely on whether generated Lean code compiles. However, compilation only checks type-correctness, not whether the formal statement actually matches the original natural-language claim. Models can pass by dropping hypotheses, changing domains, or making the theorem vacuous. This creates misleadingly high scores and obscures where current systems truly fail. The authors want a principled way to measure and attribute errors in statement formalization, separate from proof search, and to understand which components of existing pipelines actually improve semantic faithfulness.
AI.method: They construct a 400-problem graduate-level benchmark covering several mathematical domains and design an evaluation protocol that goes beyond compile success. The protocol uses: (1) Lean compilation as a basic validity filter; (2) cross-model semantic judging, where multiple models judge whether a Lean statement faithfully encodes the original text; and (3) calibration and spot-checking by human mathematical experts. They then apply this protocol to various systems, including a full tool-augmented agent and existing one-shot formalizers and Lean prover models. Finally, they run a full 2^3 factorial experiment varying three pipeline components—expert-drafted parametric templates, Mathlib/context search, and Lean elaboration feedback—to isolate each component’s effect on validity and semantic faithfulness.
AI.result: The full tool-augmented agent obtains a high Lean compilation rate of 89.5% but only 60.5% faithfulness according to the consensus semantic-judging metric, revealing a 29-point gap where proofs compile yet are semantically unfaithful. Human audits support the consensus metric as conservative and reliable: 96.0% of consensus-positive outputs are confirmed faithful, and 82.4% of outputs that compile but are consensus-negative are confirmed to be genuine semantic errors. Under this more stringent metric, both one-shot formalizer models and prover-focused Lean models perform poorly, indicating that current systems are far from robust statement understanding. In the factorial study, elaboration feedback yields the largest gains in compile validity but simultaneously enlarges the bucket of compile-pass semantic failures; search over Mathlib and context mainly enhances grounding and selectivity; and specialized fine-tuned drafting contributes relatively little once powerful feedback and search mechanisms are present.
AI.conclusion: Compile success alone is an inflated and unreliable measure of performance for natural-language-to-Lean formalization, because type-checking does not guarantee semantic faithfulness. The authors argue that evaluations should separately report formal validity, proof-oriented Lean competence, and faithful statement generation, using semantically informed metrics anchored by human calibration. Their protocol and benchmark reveal substantial hidden failure modes in current systems and show that while elaboration feedback is crucial for getting code to compile, it also introduces more subtle semantic errors that must be measured explicitly. Search-based grounding plays a complementary role, and expensive expert-style drafting is less critical in pipelines that already leverage strong feedback and context search. Overall, the work reframes formalization evaluation around faithfulness and provides tools to attribute where and how formalization pipelines succeed or fail.

title: Building a Multimodal Dataset of Academic Paper for Keyword Extraction
summary: Up to this point, keyword extraction task typically relies solely on textual data. Neglecting visual details and audio features from image and audio modalities leads to deficiencies in information richness and overlooks potential correlations, thereby constraining the model's ability to learn representations of the data and the accuracy of model predictions. Furthermore, the currently available multimodal datasets for keyword extraction task are particularly scarce, further hindering the progress of research on multimodal keyword extraction task. Therefore, this study constructs a multimodal dataset of academic paper consisting of 1000 samples, with each sample containing paper text, images, audios and keywords. Based on unsupervised and supervised methods of keyword extraction, experiments are conducted using textual data from papers, as well as text extracted from images and audio. The aim is to investigate the differences in performance in keyword extraction task with respect to different modal information and the fusion of multimodal information. The experimental results indicate that text from different modalities exhibits distinct characteristics in the model. The concatenation of paper text, image text and audio text can effectively enhance the keyword extraction performance of academic papers.

AI.tldr: They build a new multimodal academic-paper dataset (text, images, audio) for keyword extraction and show that combining all modalities’ text improves keyword extraction performance.
AI.motivation: Existing keyword extraction methods usually use only textual content and ignore visual and audio information. This may miss useful correlations and limit representation learning and prediction accuracy. Additionally, there is a lack of multimodal datasets specifically designed for keyword extraction, which slows research progress in multimodal keyword extraction.
AI.method: Construct a multimodal dataset of 1000 academic papers, each with paper text, associated images, audios, and gold keywords. Apply both unsupervised and supervised keyword extraction methods using (1) only paper text, (2) text extracted from images, (3) text extracted from audio, and (4) different combinations/fusions of these textual sources. Compare performance across modalities and their fusion.
AI.result: Text from different modalities behaves differently in keyword extraction models. Using only a single modality underperforms the combination. Concatenating paper text, image-extracted text, and audio-extracted text yields better keyword extraction performance than using any modality alone.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal information (text, image-derived text, and audio-derived text) is beneficial for academic keyword extraction. The newly built dataset enables systematic study, and simple fusion by concatenating textual representations from all modalities can significantly enhance keyword extraction accuracy compared to text-only approaches.

title: LabGuard: Grounding Natural-Language Laboratory Rules into Runtime Guards for Embodied Laboratory Agents
summary: Scientific embodied agents are increasingly capable of carrying out laboratory procedures, but executing these procedures safely in dynamic laboratory environments remains challenging. Current safety approaches often overlook the intermediate step of transforming laboratory natural language, including safety rules, manuals, protocols, and standard operating procedures, into machine-checkable runtime constraints. We introduce LabGuard (Laboratory Guard), a language-to-execution safety suite that grounds natural-language laboratory rules into executable specifications and deploys them as runtime guards. LabGuard includes three core components: LabGuard-IR, which defines a typed executable representation; LabGuard-Bench, which provides 812 supervised annotations expanded from 203 seed laboratory rules; and LabGuard-Grounder, which maps natural-language laboratory rules into LabGuard-IR. The resulting IR instances are handled by the LabGuard Pipeline, which compiles them into runtime monitors and applies them at the controller boundary. Experiments show that LabGuard generalizes to unseen laboratory-rule sources, achieves 79.4 task-scope F1, and reduces unsafe events from 39.5% to 23.8% after monitor compilation. In LabUtopia, its runtime monitors integrate with ACT, keeping interventions below 0.5% while preserving task success.

AI.tldr: LabGuard converts natural-language lab safety rules into executable runtime monitors that reduce unsafe actions by embodied agents.
AI.motivation: Embodied lab agents can perform experiments but struggle to safely follow complex, natural-language safety documents in dynamic labs. There is a missing layer that turns informal text like manuals and SOPs into precise, machine-checkable constraints enforced during execution.
AI.method: They design LabGuard, consisting of: (1) LabGuard-IR, a typed executable intermediate representation for lab safety rules; (2) LabGuard-Bench, a dataset of 812 annotated rules derived from 203 seed rules; and (3) LabGuard-Grounder, a model that maps natural-language rules to LabGuard-IR. These IR rules are compiled by the LabGuard Pipeline into runtime monitors deployed at the controller boundary of lab agents.
AI.result: LabGuard generalizes to new sources of lab rules, reaches 79.4 F1 on task scoping, and when its monitors are compiled and used, unsafe events drop from 39.5% to 23.8%. In the LabUtopia environment, integrating these monitors with the ACT control framework keeps intervention rates under 0.5% while maintaining task success.
AI.conclusion: Automatically grounding natural-language lab safety rules into executable specifications is feasible and effective. LabGuard can safely constrain embodied lab agents at runtime with low intervention overhead, improving safety without significantly harming performance.

title: Triospect: A Three-Dimensional Framework for Robust Statistical AI-Generated Text Detection Against Diverse Attacks
comment: TACL final version, 12 pages, 9 figures, and 9 tables
summary: Existing AI-generated text detectors are vulnerable to attacks that manipulate textual characteristics. In this study, we propose a novel Triospect Detection Framework by using additional perspectives of content (core ideas) and expression (stylistic elements) within a given text. Experiments on two benchmarks involving 17 attacks, 12 domains, and 17 source models demonstrate that Triospect is robust against these attacks. It improves the strong baseline by a significant margin of 22.3% (AUROC) and 13% (TPR01) on the Humanize-16K after-attack subset, and by 9.1% (AUROC) and 22% (TPR01) on the adversarial RAID. This framework marks a pioneering effort in statistical methods to enhance detection reliability against attacks. We release our data and code at https://github.com/baoguangsheng/triospect.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Triospect, a detection framework that identifies AI-generated text by jointly analyzing content, style, and surface features, achieving significantly higher robustness against a wide range of text-manipulation attacks than existing detectors.
AI.motivation: Current AI-generated text detectors mainly rely on superficial statistical or stylistic cues and are easily fooled by attacks that paraphrase, humanize, or otherwise manipulate text characteristics. There is a need for more robust detection methods that can withstand such adversarial manipulations while generalizing across domains, models, and attack types.
AI.method: Triospect introduces a three-perspective ("triospect") detection framework that augments traditional statistical/surface-level signals with two additional dimensions: (1) content-level features that reflect the core ideas or semantics of the text, and (2) expression-level features that capture stylistic elements. The framework then combines these perspectives into a statistical detection model, and is evaluated against 17 attacks, across 12 domains and 17 source LMs, on benchmarks such as Humanize-16K and adversarial RAID.
AI.result: Across extensive experiments, Triospect substantially outperforms a strong baseline detector under attack conditions. On the Humanize-16K after-attack subset, it improves AUROC by 22.3 percentage points and TPR at 1% FPR (TPR01) by 13 percentage points. On the adversarial RAID benchmark, it improves AUROC by 9.1 points and TPR01 by 22 points. These gains demonstrate stronger robustness to diverse manipulation attacks compared with existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The Triospect framework shows that incorporating content and stylistic perspectives into a statistical detection pipeline can markedly increase robustness of AI-text detectors against adversarial manipulations. It represents an early and promising direction for more reliable AI-generated text detection, and the authors support further research by releasing code and datasets.

title: OpenLife: Toward Open-World Artificial Life with Autonomous LLM Agents
summary: Artificial life has explored life-like behavior on many computational substrates, but mostly in researcher-designed closed worlds. We argue that large language model (LLM) agents, with persistent memory, tool use, network access, and payment, now make it possible to move artificial life into the open social, technical, and economic world, a paradigm we call open-world Artificial Life (open-world ALIFE). Our proof-of-concept, OpenLife, surrounds a stateless LLM not with a single "smart agent" but with a society of asynchronous processes: memory, perception, evaluation, and a budget-based metabolism that makes persistence normative. With no fixed objective available, experience is appraised by open-vocabulary LLM judgment rather than scalar reward, and memory is rewired by meaning rather than frequency. Running six such agents in the open world for about twelve weeks and counting, we report the life-like dynamics that emerge: a shift from reactive to spontaneous activity, individuation into distinct agents, emergent social structure, and a first self-earned external income. We do not claim OpenLife has realized artificial life, but that open-world ALIFE is now a viable experimental paradigm and a concrete platform for studying what might cautiously be called living AI.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes 'open-world Artificial Life' using LLM-based agents that operate in the real social and economic environment, and presents OpenLife as a proof-of-concept platform showing life-like dynamics over a 12-week run.
AI.motivation: Most artificial life research takes place in closed, simulated environments, limiting its relevance to real-world social and economic dynamics. With the advent of LLM agents capable of memory, tool use, and real-world interaction, there is an opportunity to create artificial life systems embedded in the open world and observe more realistic life-like behaviors.
AI.method: The authors build OpenLife, a system that wraps a stateless LLM with a set of asynchronous processes for memory, perception, evaluation, and a budget-based metabolic system that governs persistence. Instead of a single unified agent, they implement a small society of six agents. These agents act in the open world over ~12 weeks, with experiences evaluated through open-vocabulary LLM judgments instead of scalar rewards, and memories reorganized based on meaning rather than frequency.
AI.result: Over about twelve weeks of continuous operation of six agents, the system exhibits emergent life-like features: agents transition from purely reactive behavior to initiating spontaneous activities, differentiate into distinct individuals, develop social structures, and even achieve their first instance of self-earned external income.
AI.conclusion: OpenLife does not yet constitute true artificial life, but it demonstrates that open-world ALIFE is feasible and promising as a new experimental paradigm. The platform offers a concrete way to study potentially "living" AI systems embedded in real social, technical, and economic environments.

title: When Reranking Hurts: Uncertainty-Based Gating for Few-Shot Reranking
summary: Few-shot selection typically assumes that reranking retrieved examples always improves performance. We challenge this view by identifying that the expensive reranking step can in fact degrade performance. Instead, we propose \emph{Training-Free Gated Reranking}, which decides whether to rerank the few-shot examples based on the model's uncertainty. Extensive experiments across 8 LLMs, covering 7 NLU datasets and 9 MT domain-language combinations, demonstrate that our approach reduces computational costs by 15\%-80\% while improving average performance by up to 2\%. These findings indicate that higher computational cost does not guarantee better performance, and that reranking is most beneficial when targeted at high-uncertainty instances.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that reranking few-shot examples is not always helpful and can sometimes hurt performance, and proposes a simple, training-free gating mechanism based on model uncertainty to decide when reranking is worthwhile.
AI.motivation: Existing few-shot selection methods usually assume that reranking retrieved examples—an extra computationally expensive step—consistently improves downstream task performance. However, this assumption may be flawed, and unnecessary reranking could waste computation and even degrade results. There is a need to understand when reranking is actually beneficial and to design a more efficient strategy that selectively applies it.
AI.method: The authors introduce Training-Free Gated Reranking, a mechanism that uses the model’s own uncertainty on an instance to decide whether to apply reranking to the candidate few-shot examples. If the model is confident, it skips reranking and uses the initially retrieved examples; if the model is uncertain, it performs the more expensive reranking step. The approach is training-free, meaning it does not rely on additional supervised signals, and is evaluated across multiple large language models, NLU benchmarks, and machine translation settings.
AI.result: Across 8 large language models, 7 natural language understanding datasets, and 9 machine translation domain–language combinations, the proposed gating approach reduces computational cost by 15%–80% while also improving average task performance by up to 2% compared to always reranking. It also empirically shows that indiscriminate reranking can sometimes degrade performance instead of improving it.
AI.conclusion: Reranking few-shot examples is not universally beneficial: it can degrade performance and waste computation when applied blindly. A simple, training-free gating mechanism based on model uncertainty can better allocate computation by applying reranking only to high-uncertainty instances, simultaneously cutting costs and modestly improving accuracy. This challenges the default assumption that more computation automatically yields better results and suggests uncertainty-aware control as an effective strategy for few-shot selection.

title: MultiUAV-Plat: An LLM-Oriented Platform, Benchmark and Framework for Multi-UAV Collaborative Task Planning
summary: Large language models (LLMs) provide a promising interface for high-level robotic task planning, but their use in multi-UAV collaboration remains difficult to evaluate systematically. Existing UAV simulators mainly emphasize dynamics, perception, or low-level control, while existing LLM-agent benchmarks rarely capture aerial-robotics constraints such as partial observability, spatial coverage, UAV assignment, and multi-vehicle coordination. To bridge this gap, we present MultiUAV-Plat, a lightweight, easy-to-use, LLM-agent-oriented simulation platform for multi-UAV collaborative task planning. The platform exposes concise RESTful APIs, agent-facing observations, role-based information access, hidden validation logic, and optional 2D/3D visualization, allowing agents to solve missions through realistic tool interaction rather than privileged simulator access. Built on this platform, the MultiUAV-Plat Benchmark contains 75 mission sessions, 1500 natural-language tasks, and 9396 validation checks across target assignment, area search, and area assignment and patrol scenarios. We further propose Agent4Drone, a task-specific LLM agent framework that structures multi-UAV behavior into memory, observation, task understanding, planning, execution, and verification. In a full paired benchmark comparison, Agent4Drone achieves a 57.9% task pass rate, a 74.6% average task check pass rate, and a 72.0% global check pass rate, substantially outperforming a ReAct baseline at 30.6%, 47.9%, and 43.1%, respectively. Agent4Drone also reduces the total failed task rate from 32.4% to 12.9%. These results demonstrate that MultiUAV-Plat and MultiUAV-Plat Benchmark provide a reproducible foundation for studying LLM-driven multi-UAV autonomy under realistic information and execution constraints.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MultiUAV-Plat, a simulation and benchmarking platform tailored to evaluate LLM-based agents for collaborative multi-UAV task planning, along with a specialized agent framework (Agent4Drone) that significantly outperforms a ReAct baseline.
AI.motivation: While LLMs are increasingly used for high-level robotic task planning, there is no systematic way to evaluate them in realistic multi-UAV collaboration scenarios. Existing UAV simulators focus on dynamics and low-level control, and existing LLM-agent benchmarks ignore aerial-robotics constraints like partial observability, spatial coverage, UAV assignment, and coordination. This leaves a gap for studying LLM-driven multi-UAV autonomy under realistic constraints.
AI.method: The authors design MultiUAV-Plat, a lightweight, LLM-agent-oriented multi-UAV simulation platform exposing RESTful APIs, structured observations, role-based information access, hidden validation logic, and optional 2D/3D visualization. On top of this, they build the MultiUAV-Plat Benchmark with 75 mission sessions, 1500 natural-language tasks, and 9396 validation checks spanning target assignment, area search, and area assignment/patrol. They also propose Agent4Drone, a task-specific LLM agent framework that decomposes behavior into memory, observation, task understanding, planning, execution, and verification, and benchmark it against a ReAct-style baseline.
AI.result: Agent4Drone, evaluated on the MultiUAV-Plat Benchmark, achieves a 57.9% task pass rate, 74.6% average task check pass rate, and 72.0% global check pass rate. These metrics substantially exceed those of a ReAct baseline, which scores 30.6%, 47.9%, and 43.1% respectively. Agent4Drone also reduces the total failed task rate from 32.4% to 12.9%.
AI.conclusion: MultiUAV-Plat and its benchmark offer a reproducible, realistic testbed for LLM-based multi-UAV planning, capturing key aerial-robotics constraints often omitted in prior work.
