The Agent4Drone framework demonstrates that task-specialized LLM agents can markedly improve multi-UAV coordination performance over generic ReAct baselines in this environment.

title: What Counts as an Error? Dual-Reference Benchmarking for Atypical ASR
summary: ASR systems have been often reported to underperform on atypical speech. An often conflated compounding factor is the existence of two valid transcription references: verbatim (actual produced speech, including repetitions/prolongations) and intended (the canonical form of the text with disfluencies removed) in atypical speech recognition depending on context and use-case. Most ASR evaluations conflate this duality into a single ground truth and reward systems that delete disfluencies, ignoring verbatim faithfulness. We benchmark 11 ASR models from encoder-decoder, CTC and transducer families using both verbatim and intended references on atypical stuttered speech as a case study. Our quantitative assessment underlines the disparity in model performance and rankings using the two transcript styles. Through this analysis, we highlight the importance of selecting a suitable transcription reference for valid model selection depending on the use-case, particularly for atypical ASR.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how different ASR models perform on stuttered (atypical) speech when evaluated with two types of transcripts—verbatim vs. intended—and shows that model rankings change significantly depending on which reference is used.
AI.motivation: ASR systems usually perform poorly on atypical speech such as stuttering. In such settings, there are often two legitimate transcript styles: verbatim (including repetitions, prolongations, and disfluencies) and intended (cleaned-up canonical text). Current evaluations typically mix or ignore this duality, rewarding models that remove disfluencies and overlooking how faithfully they capture what was actually spoken. This can misguide model selection for critical applications involving atypical speech.
AI.method: The authors benchmark 11 automatic speech recognition models representing three major architectural families—encoder-decoder, CTC, and transducer—on a corpus of atypical stuttered speech. They evaluate each model using two separate reference transcripts: (1) verbatim transcripts that preserve disfluencies and (2) intended transcripts where disfluencies are removed. They compare quantitative performance metrics and model rankings under each reference style to analyze discrepancies.
AI.result: The evaluation reveals substantial differences in ASR performance metrics and in the relative ranking of models when switching between verbatim and intended references. Some models appear stronger when judged on intended (cleaned) transcripts but perform comparatively worse when faithfulness to verbatim disfluent speech is required, and vice versa. This demonstrates that using a single, mixed evaluation reference can obscure these distinctions.
AI.conclusion: The choice of transcription reference—verbatim versus intended—critically affects ASR evaluation and model selection for atypical speech, such as stuttered speech. Researchers and practitioners must align the reference style with the downstream use-case: applications needing faithful capture of what was spoken should use verbatim references, while those prioritizing fluent, cleaned text may use intended references. Properly separating and choosing transcript styles is essential for valid benchmarking and deployment of atypical ASR systems.

title: DDIAgents: Mechanism-Conditioned Context Flow for Drug-Drug Interaction Prediction
summary: Drug-drug interaction (DDI) prediction is essential for medication safety, yet it requires reasoning over heterogeneous biomedical evidence whose relevance changes across interaction mechanisms. We propose DDIAgents, a mechanism-conditioned multi-agent framework that performs DDI prediction through dynamic knowledge orchestration. Given a drug pair, a planner agent instantiates specialized expert agents, routes mechanism-relevant knowledge sources to each agent, and aggregates their analyses through a conclusion agent. By adapting context flow to the inferred interaction mechanism, DDIAgents reduces irrelevant information, supports complementary expert reasoning, and produces interpretable agent-level rationales. Extensive experiments on realistic DDI prediction benchmarks show that DDIAgents consistently outperforms existing feature-based, graph-based, LLM-based, and agent-based baselines. Beyond prediction performance, DDIAgents demonstrates how multi-agent systems can organize heterogeneous scientific knowledge for adaptive and interpretable AI4Science reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DDIAgents, a mechanism-conditioned multi-agent framework that uses dynamic knowledge orchestration to improve drug-drug interaction prediction and interpretability over heterogeneous biomedical evidence.
AI.motivation: Accurately predicting drug-drug interactions is critical for medication safety, but existing methods struggle to reason over diverse and mechanism-specific biomedical evidence. Different interaction mechanisms (e.g., pharmacokinetic vs. pharmacodynamic) require focusing on different subsets of heterogeneous knowledge, and current models often mix irrelevant information, limiting both performance and interpretability. There is a need for an approach that can adaptively organize and exploit mechanism-relevant evidence while providing transparent reasoning.
AI.method: The authors propose DDIAgents, a multi-agent system where, given a drug pair, a central planner agent infers or conditions on the likely interaction mechanism and dynamically creates specialized expert agents tailored to that mechanism. The planner routes mechanism-relevant biomedical knowledge sources (e.g., different databases or evidence types) to the appropriate experts. Each expert agent performs focused reasoning and produces an analysis, which is then collected and synthesized by a conclusion agent to yield the final DDI prediction and associated rationale. The framework emphasizes dynamic context flow, mechanism conditioning, and modular expert specialization.
AI.result: On realistic DDI prediction benchmarks, DDIAgents consistently surpasses a range of strong baselines, including traditional feature-based models, graph-based methods, single large language model approaches, and prior agent-based systems. The experiments show improvements in predictive accuracy and robustness, as well as qualitative gains in the relevance and clarity of the generated rationales. The system effectively filters out irrelevant information and leverages complementary expertise across agents.
AI.conclusion: DDIAgents demonstrates that a mechanism-conditioned multi-agent architecture can more effectively organize and exploit heterogeneous biomedical knowledge for DDI prediction than existing approaches. By dynamically orchestrating specialized agents and routing context according to interaction mechanisms, it enhances performance while providing interpretable, agent-level explanations. The work highlights the broader potential of multi-agent systems for adaptive, interpretable AI4Science reasoning over complex scientific domains.

title: SeKV: Resolution-Adaptive KV Cache with Hierarchical Semantic Memory for Long-Context LLM Inference
summary: Large language models increasingly operate over long contexts, where the KV cache becomes a dominant memory bottleneck: its size grows linearly with sequence length and must be retained throughout decoding, making full GPU caching prohibitively expensive without compression. Existing KV cache compression methods struggle to balance efficiency with faithful context preservation. Token eviction discards information, while semantic grouping fixes compression decisions at prefill time; neither can recover token-level detail from a compressed span once it becomes relevant during generation. As a solution, we propose SeKV, a resolution-adaptive semantic KV cache that organizes context into entropy-guided semantic spans and stores them across a GPU-CPU memory hierarchy without discarding information. Each span keeps a lightweight summary vector on GPU for coarse routing and a low-rank SVD basis on CPU for on-demand token-level reconstruction. A trained zoom-in mechanism selectively expands query-relevant spans during decoding, enabling precise retrieval without materializing the full KV cache on GPU. SeKV enables adaptive token-level reconstruction while keeping the base LLM fully frozen and adding fewer than 0.05% trainable parameters. Across four benchmarks, SeKV improves over the strongest semantic compression baseline by 5.9% on average while reducing GPU memory by 53.3% versus full KV caching at 128K context. Code is available on https://github.com/AmirAbaskohi/SeKV.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SeKV, a resolution-adaptive semantic KV cache for long-context LLMs that compresses and offloads key-value states across GPU and CPU while allowing on-demand token-level reconstruction, significantly reducing GPU memory with better accuracy than prior compression methods.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are applied to very long context lengths, the key-value (KV) cache memory grows linearly with sequence length and must be preserved for decoding, making full GPU caching expensive or infeasible. Existing KV compression techniques either drop information (token eviction) or compress into semantic groups that cannot be later decompressed at token granularity, leading to loss of important details during generation. There is a need for a method that reduces GPU memory usage without irreversibly discarding token-level information and while keeping the base model unchanged.
AI.method: SeKV builds a semantic, resolution-adaptive KV cache. During prefill, the context is partitioned into semantic spans using an entropy-guided criterion. For each span, a small summary vector is stored on GPU and a low-rank SVD basis for the span’s KV representations is stored on CPU. During decoding, a trained zoom-in mechanism uses the GPU-resident summaries to route queries and identify which spans are relevant. Relevant spans are reconstructed at token level on demand from the CPU-side low-rank representation and brought onto GPU, avoiding the need to keep the full KV cache in GPU memory. The base LLM remains frozen and the added components introduce fewer than 0.05% additional trainable parameters.
AI.result: On four long-context benchmarks at up to 128K tokens, SeKV outperforms the strongest prior semantic compression baseline by an average of 5.9% while reducing GPU memory consumption by 53.3% relative to full, uncompressed KV caching. This demonstrates that SeKV achieves a better trade-off between memory efficiency and task performance than existing methods.
AI.conclusion: SeKV provides an effective approach to KV cache compression for long-context LLMs by combining semantic span organization, GPU-CPU memory hierarchy, and on-demand reconstruction. It preserves token-level information, maintains the base model untouched, and uses very few extra parameters while substantially cutting GPU memory and improving accuracy over prior semantic compression techniques. This makes it a practical solution for scaling LLMs to very long contexts without prohibitive GPU memory costs.

title: Revealing Safety-Critical Scenarios for UTM via Transformer
summary: Unmanned Traffic Management (UTM) systems are cloud-based platforms designed to manage and coordinate multiple aerial vehicles remotely. UTM systems are safety-critical which cannot tolerate failures like crash or collision. To reveal latent vulnerabilities, there are neither optimal failure-exposing demonstrations nor clear reward signals. Additionally, UTM's self-healing capability introduces the ``long-tail effect'' of critical failures. We propose framing UTM vulnerability discovery as a sequence modeling problem amenable to transformer-based RL architectures. Our approach leverages attention mechanisms to directly model the relationship among system states, and predict optimal actions. Our framework introduces a Policy Model that generates targeted test scenarios and an Action Sampler that enforces domain constraints. We use a risk-based reward function to guide exploration. Through extensive evaluation on a 700-hour simulation study, we demonstrate an 8$\times$ improvement in vulnerability discovery efficiency compared to expert-guided testing. It also discovers critical edge cases that traditional methods have missed.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a transformer-based reinforcement learning framework to automatically discover safety vulnerabilities in Unmanned Traffic Management (UTM) systems, greatly improving efficiency over expert-guided testing.
AI.motivation: UTM systems coordinate multiple unmanned aerial vehicles and are safety-critical, so failures like crashes are unacceptable. However, uncovering hidden vulnerabilities is challenging due to the lack of optimal failure-exposing test demonstrations, unclear reward signals for critical failures, and the self-healing behavior of UTM that makes rare but severe failures hard to expose and evaluate (long-tail effect). There is a need for an automated, scalable, and more effective way to generate challenging test scenarios that reveal such vulnerabilities.
AI.method: The authors formulate UTM vulnerability discovery as a sequence modeling problem and apply transformer-based reinforcement learning to it. They design a Policy Model that uses attention mechanisms to capture relationships among system states and to generate targeted test scenarios (i.e., sequences of actions). An Action Sampler component ensures that generated actions obey domain-specific constraints. A risk-based reward function is introduced to guide RL exploration toward potentially dangerous or high-risk states, even without explicit failure labels. The whole framework is evaluated via large-scale simulation.
AI.result: In a 700-hour simulation campaign, the proposed framework improves vulnerability discovery efficiency by a factor of eight compared with expert-guided testing procedures. It also discovers critical edge cases—rare but dangerous failure scenarios—that traditional or expert-driven testing methods failed to reveal.
AI.conclusion: Treating vulnerability discovery in UTM as a sequence modeling task and using transformer-based RL with attention and risk-based rewards is effective for uncovering critical safety issues. The framework not only accelerates the discovery of vulnerabilities but also finds important edge cases that manual and traditional testing miss, suggesting that such learning-based approaches can significantly enhance the safety assurance process for complex autonomous traffic management systems.

title: TAG-DLM: Diffusion Language Models for Text-Attributed Graph Learning
summary:
Text-attributed graphs (TAGs), where each node carries a natural language description, require models to jointly reason over text and graph topology. Existing approaches often handle the two modalities separately: graph neural networks operate on shallow text features, while hybrids of LLMs and graphs use the language model mainly as a text encoder and delegate structure learning to a separate graph module. We propose method that unifies textual reasoning and graph message passing within a masked diffusion language model, a language model with bidirectional attention and generative decoding. For each graph instance, method linearises a sampled local neighbourhood into a token sequence and injects graph structure through a topology attention mask, which realises message passing over the graph. Because the diffusion language model can both interpret and generate text, the method adapts to different tasks simply by changing the prompt, supporting node classification, link prediction, and cross-dataset transfer with no target-specific fine-tuning.
  Experiments show that method outperforms graph neural networks, graph transformers, and LLM-based baselines on all three TAG benchmarks across two tasks, improving over the strongest baseline by up to 3.9 points.

AI.tldr: A unified masked diffusion language model is proposed for text-attributed graphs, performing both textual reasoning and graph message passing via an attention mask, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple TAG benchmarks for several tasks without task-specific fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Previous methods for text-attributed graphs treat text and structure separately: GNNs operate on shallow text embeddings, and LLM-graph hybrids use LLMs only as encoders while relying on separate modules for structural reasoning. This separation limits the ability to jointly reason over text and graph topology in a flexible, task-agnostic way. The authors aim to build a single model that natively integrates language understanding and graph message passing, enabling more expressive reasoning and easier adaptation across tasks and datasets.
AI.method: They introduce a masked diffusion language model with bidirectional attention and generative decoding that directly performs message passing over text-attributed graphs. For each graph instance, they sample a local neighborhood, linearize it into a token sequence, and encode the graph structure via a topology attention mask that restricts which tokens can attend to which others, effectively implementing graph message passing in the transformer. The same diffusion LM is used for both understanding and generation: different downstream tasks (node classification, link prediction, cross-dataset transfer) are handled by changing the prompt, without target-specific fine-tuning.
AI.result: On three standard text-attributed graph benchmarks and two tasks (node classification and link prediction), their method consistently outperforms strong baselines, including graph neural networks, graph transformers, and LLM-based hybrid methods. The gains reach up to 3.9 percentage points over the best competing approach, indicating that tightly coupling text reasoning and structural message passing in a single diffusion LM yields superior performance.
AI.conclusion: Unifying textual reasoning and graph message passing inside a masked diffusion language model, using topology-aware attention over linearized neighborhoods, provides a powerful and flexible framework for learning on text-attributed graphs. This design supports multiple tasks through prompting alone and achieves state-of-the-art performance over diverse baselines, suggesting that future TAG models should more tightly integrate language modeling and graph structure rather than treating them as separate components.

title: The Past Is Prologue: A Plug-in Controller for Selective Updates in Sequentially Evolving LLM Memory
summary: Sequentially evolving LLM memory enables agents to reuse past experience, but existing systems usually deploy each locally generated memory update without checking whether it improves future behavior. As a result, updates that help the current task may overwrite useful knowledge, introduce over-specific rules, or bias the final memory toward recent examples. We propose Janus, a plug-in memory controller that decides whether to accept a candidate memory update or retain the previous memory. To make this decision efficient, Janus uses a Memory Momentum Trigger to identify suspicious deviations in the memory-update trajectory, and compares old and new memories on a compact hybrid evaluation set of coverage, boundary, and fresh tasks instead of replaying the full history. Janus is method-agnostic and wraps existing updaters without changing their update rules. Across six datasets, two backbone LLMs, and two memory updaters, Janus improves average accuracy by +2.7 to +4.6 points over the corresponding base updaters.

AI.tldr: Janus is a plug-in controller that decides when to accept or reject new memory updates for LLM-based agents, improving performance by avoiding harmful or over-specific updates.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM memory systems accept every incremental memory update without validation, which can overwrite useful knowledge, create overfitted rules, and bias memory toward recent experiences, ultimately degrading future performance. There is a need for a principled mechanism that can decide whether a new memory update truly benefits long-term behavior.
AI.method: Janus wraps around existing memory updaters as a controller. It introduces a Memory Momentum Trigger that flags suspicious deviations in the trajectory of memory updates. When triggered, Janus evaluates whether to keep the old memory or adopt the new update by comparing them on a compact hybrid evaluation set composed of coverage tasks (representing past behavior), boundary tasks (near decision boundaries), and fresh tasks (new situations), instead of replaying the full interaction history. It is agnostic to the underlying memory-update algorithm and does not modify their update rules.
AI.result: On six datasets, using two different backbone LLMs and two memory updaters, Janus consistently improves average accuracy by about +2.7 to +4.6 percentage points compared to the same updaters without Janus.
AI.conclusion: Selective control over LLM memory updates via Janus leads to more reliable and effective use of experience, mitigating issues like catastrophic overwriting and recency bias while remaining compatible with diverse existing memory-update methods.

title: Gated Multi-Graph Fusion via Graph Attention Networks for Alzheimer's Disease Detection
comment: 5 pages, 1 figure, 2 tables, and accepted in interspeech 2026 conference
summary: Spontaneous speech is a vital non-invasive biomarker for Alzheimer's Disease (AD), yet many systems overlook non-linear structural disruptions and clinical heterogeneity in pathological language. We propose a Multi-View Gated Graph Attention Network that transcribes audio via Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) to construct semantic, dependency, and co-occurrence graphs, characterizing speech through a "content-structure-flow" framework. Notably, the co-occurrence graph leverages Pointwise Mutual Information (PMI) from a normative corpus to quantify narrative logic and linguistic deviation. To address symptomatic diversity, an adaptive gated fusion mechanism dynamically integrates these views. Evaluated on the ADReSSo dataset, our model achieves 90.00% accuracy. Ablation results confirm that the PMI-based graph and heterogeneity-aware gating are essential for robust classification across diverse clinical populations. Our source code is publicly available at https://github.com/opeacc/AD.

AI.tldr: They build a multi-view graph neural model using ASR transcripts and PMI-based co-occurrence graphs to classify Alzheimer’s speech, achieving 90% accuracy on ADReSSo.
AI.motivation: Existing AD speech classifiers often ignore non-linear structural breaks in language and the clinical heterogeneity of symptoms, limiting robustness and sensitivity to pathological narrative structure.
AI.method: They transcribe spontaneous speech via ASR, then build three graphs per sample: semantic, syntactic dependency, and PMI-based co-occurrence graphs, forming a content-structure-flow representation. A Multi-View Gated Graph Attention Network encodes these graphs, and an adaptive gated fusion mechanism dynamically weights each view to handle heterogeneous symptom presentations.
AI.result: On the ADReSSo dataset, their approach reaches 90% accuracy for AD detection. Ablation studies show that both the PMI-based co-occurrence graph and the heterogeneity-aware gated fusion significantly contribute to performance and robustness across different patient profiles.
AI.conclusion: Modeling speech as multi-view graphs and explicitly encoding narrative logic via PMI, together with adaptive fusion across views, provides an effective and robust non-invasive biomarker for AD detection from spontaneous speech.

title: Scenario Generation for Testing of Autonomous Driving Systems Using Real-World Failure Records
comment: 9 pages, Appendix included. Paper accepted and presented at NeuS 2026
summary: To ensure safe on-road behavior, pre-deployment testing and failure discovery of Autonomous Driving Systems (ADS) is crucial. Present day simulation based testing methods focus largely on mathematical models for efficient search of optimal scenarios, assuming a fixed scenario representation. On the other hand, real-world testing involves substantial manual effort to design scenario templates for testing. These templates represent distinct failure scenarios consisting of pre-deployment vehicle movements, map types, etc. Historical failure records for ADS are a reliable source of real-world failure conditions, which can be used for scenario generation. In this work, we propose a scenario generation pipeline using categorical and contextual information available from historical records in natural language format. Our approach consists of modular LLM based synthetic scenario generation, compatible with the testing constraints of a given system. We successfully apply our method to generate a diverse set of scenarios for testing autonomous navigation on Metadrive simulator using the NHTSA ADS crash records. Our approach results in accurate and diverse scenario generation with a combination of 4 road types, 3 non ego vehicle movement types, including on road anomalies in the form of working zones. Generated scenarios align with the provided testing conditions, and reveals interesting failures of the system within a limited testing budget of 20 scenarios. Code is available at https://github.com/anjaliParashar/crash2scenario.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an LLM-based pipeline that converts natural-language historical crash reports into diverse, simulation-ready test scenarios for autonomous driving systems, uncovering failures under realistic constraints.
AI.motivation: Existing ADS testing relies either on mathematical search over fixed scenario encodings or labor-intensive manual template design, both poorly leveraging rich, text-based historical crash data. There is a need to systematically transform real-world failure descriptions into simulation scenarios to better test ADS before deployment.
AI.method: They build a modular pipeline that uses large language models to parse categorical and contextual information from natural-language crash records (NHTSA ADS reports) and synthesize structured scenarios. The pipeline enforces compatibility with simulator/testing constraints (e.g., available road types, traffic participants, and anomalies) and outputs configurations for the MetaDrive simulator, including road layouts, ego and non‑ego behaviors, and work-zone anomalies.
AI.result: Applied to NHTSA ADS crash data, the method automatically generates a compact set of 20 test scenarios in MetaDrive spanning 4 road types, 3 non‑ego vehicle movement types, and construction/work-zone anomalies. The generated scenarios match the requested testing conditions and are both accurate (faithful to constraints) and diverse (covering varied failure patterns). These scenarios expose previously unseen failures in the tested autonomous navigation system.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based synthesis of test scenarios from natural-language crash records is feasible and effective for ADS validation. By turning real-world failure descriptions into simulator-ready tests while respecting system constraints, the approach improves diversity and realism of test suites and can reveal meaningful failures within a limited testing budget.

title: Can LLMs Imagine Moral Alternatives Beyond Binary Dilemmas?
summary: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as moral advisors and agents, they need to address dilemmas between two competing values. However, existing research on LLMs with moral dilemmas overlooks a central aspect of human moral cognition: the ability to imagine alternatives that move beyond the given options. We introduce MoralAltDataset, a dataset of 307 moral dilemmas spanning narrative Advisor dilemmas and AI-facing Agent dilemmas, each augmented with compromise and reframed alternatives. We first examine whether humans and LLMs shift their judgments when such alternatives are introduced. Across 15 LLMs, we find that compromise alternatives are often preferred over either original option, substantially reshaping moral choice. We then evaluate the quality of LLM-generated alternatives against human-authored ones using pairwise preference and expert-based criteria. Results show that LLM-generated alternatives are often preferred and better satisfy fine-grained structural and ethical criteria, while revealing trade-offs between structural quality and practical feasibility.

AI.tldr: The paper builds and analyzes a dataset of moral dilemmas with added alternative options and shows that both humans and LLMs prefer compromise solutions, while LLMs can generate high-quality moral alternatives.
AI.motivation: Current work on LLMs in moral decision-making typically constrains models to choose between two pre-given options in a dilemma, ignoring a key human capacity: imagining new alternatives that can reconcile or reframe conflicting values. Since LLMs are being used as moral advisors and agents, it is crucial to understand whether they can propose such alternatives and how these compare to human-generated ones.
AI.method: The authors create MoralAltDataset, consisting of 307 moral dilemmas in two types: narrative Advisor dilemmas and AI-facing Agent dilemmas. Each dilemma is augmented with compromise alternatives (balancing competing values) and reframed alternatives (changing how the dilemma is posed). They measure how the presence of these alternatives affects moral judgments for humans and for 15 different LLMs. They also have LLMs generate their own alternatives and compare them to human-authored ones using pairwise preference judgments and expert-based evaluations on structural (e.g., clarity, coherence) and ethical criteria.
AI.result: Both humans and LLMs frequently shift their moral choices toward compromise options when such alternatives are presented, often preferring them over the original binary options, which significantly changes the distribution of moral decisions. LLM-generated alternatives are often preferred to human-authored ones and score higher on many structural and ethical dimensions, but they sometimes sacrifice practical feasibility, revealing a trade-off between ideal ethical structure and realistic implementability.
AI.conclusion: Considering only fixed binary choices underestimates the moral reasoning capabilities of both humans and LLMs. LLMs not only adapt their judgments in the presence of compromise alternatives but can also generate alternatives that are often judged superior to human-written ones, though they may be less feasible in practice. For deploying LLMs as moral advisors or agents, it is important to design systems that solicit, evaluate, and constrain such alternatives, balancing ethical quality with real-world practicality.

title: Beyond the Library: An Agentic Framework for Autoformalizing Research Mathematics
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated exceptional capabilities in mathematical reasoning, they frequently produce subtle errors that evade human detection. Formal mathematical languages like Lean 4 offer mechanical proof checking, strongly motivating the need for autoformalization: the automatic translation of natural language mathematics into verifiable code. Recent trends indicate that general-purpose LLMs, heavily optimized for standard programming, now outperform smaller models explicitly fine-tuned for Lean. Leveraging this shift, we introduce an agentic autoformalization framework powered by general coding LLMs. At the core of our system is an orchestrator that manages a multi-agent pipeline tailored for research-level mathematics. Because cutting-edge research frequently relies on concepts outside the scope of existing libraries like Mathlib, our system dynamically extends necessary type definitions and validates them via a novel Auxiliary Lemma technique before formalizing the primary theorems. We applied our approach to PutnamBench, producing machine-checked Lean proofs for a random sample of 32 problems. Furthermore, we evaluate our system on five papers from the ACM Symposium on Theory of Computing (STOC) spanning combinatorics, communication complexity, mechanism design, and learning theory, successfully formalizing their main theorems and validating the generated formalizations with human experts; for all five we also formalize the proofs alongside the statements, and notably two of them are proved with no axioms beyond Lean's kernel. All of our formalizations are available at https://beyondthelibrary.github.io/formal_arxiv .

AI.tldr: The paper presents an agent-based autoformalization system that uses powerful general-purpose coding LLMs to translate research-level natural-language mathematics into mechanically verified Lean 4 proofs, dynamically extending libraries and validating new definitions via an auxiliary-lemma technique, and demonstrates success on Putnam problems and STOC papers.
AI.motivation: LLMs are strong at mathematical reasoning but often generate subtle, hard-to-detect errors. Formal proof assistants like Lean can mechanically verify correctness, but writing formal proofs is labor-intensive and requires expertise. There is a growing need to automatically translate natural-language mathematics, including research-level results not covered by existing libraries like Mathlib, into verified formal code. At the same time, general-purpose coding LLMs have become so capable that they now outperform smaller Lean-specific models, suggesting the opportunity to build an autoformalization system on top of these general models.
AI.method: The authors design an agentic autoformalization framework built around an orchestrator that coordinates multiple LLM-based agents, each responsible for different stages of the formalization pipeline for research mathematics. The system operates in Lean 4, dynamically introducing new type and structure definitions when the target theorem relies on concepts not present in Mathlib. To ensure soundness of these extensions, they propose an Auxiliary Lemma technique: before attempting to formalize the main theorems, the system generates and proves strategically chosen auxiliary lemmas involving the new definitions, using Lean's proof checker to validate them. The pipeline is powered by state-of-the-art general-purpose coding LLMs rather than models fine-tuned specifically for Lean.
AI.result: Using this framework, the authors automatically produce machine-checked Lean proofs for a random sample of 32 problems from PutnamBench, a benchmark based on Putnam competition problems. They further test the system on five contemporary research papers from STOC covering combinatorics, communication complexity, mechanism design, and learning theory. For all five papers, the system formalizes the main theorems and their proofs in Lean. Human experts validate the resulting formalizations, and in two of the five cases the complete proofs are obtained without assuming any axioms beyond those in Lean's kernel.
AI.conclusion: The work shows that a carefully orchestrated, multi-agent framework built on top of general-purpose coding LLMs can reliably autoformalize both problem-solving benchmarks and advanced research-level mathematics into Lean 4, even when the results depend on concepts outside existing libraries. The auxiliary-lemma validation of new definitions helps maintain soundness. Their experiments indicate that modern coding LLMs, combined with agentic orchestration, offer a practical path toward scalable, trustworthy autoformalization of mathematical research, and the released formalizations provide a resource for further study and extension.

title: Probing Stylistic Appropriation using Large Language Models: An Evaluation Framework for Copyright Infringement under EU Law
summary: Large language models (LLM) trained on web-scale corpora generate output that may infringe copyright, yet existing technical safeguards focus narrowly on verbatim memorisation. EU copyright doctrine applies a broader standards: substantial similarity, which extends to stylistic choices, narrative structure, and creative elaboration. This mismatch between what current methods detect and what the law protects leaves a significant compliance gap. We introduce PSALM, an LLM-as-a-judge framework that operationalises EU copyright doctrine through ten evaluators assessing computational overlap, stylistic dimensions (writing style, narrative voice), content dimensions (character, plot, scene, world building), and statutory exceptions (parody, pastiche, quotation, scènes à faire). Applying PSALM to Llama~3.2 models fine-tuned on translated historical Dutch literary works, we find that: 1) instruction-tuned models exhibit non-trivial baseline stylistic similarity prior to corpus exposure; 2) fine-tuning induces systematic stylistic appropriation across all infringement-relevant dimensions, extending beyond verbatim memorisation to abstract narrative patterns; 3) Negative Preference Optimisation unlearning substantially reduces similarity but leaves detectable residual stylistic patterns. These findings indicate that safeguards targeting literal copying alone are insufficient to mitigate broader copyright risks. PSALM provides infrastructure for auditable, legally informed compliance evaluation, though the relationship between automated similarity scores and infringement determinations requires validation by legal experts. This work bridges qualitative legal standards and quantitative technical measurement, exposing fundamental tensions between generative AI and EU intellectual property law.

AI.tldr: The paper presents PSALM, an LLM-based evaluation framework that translates EU copyright standards—particularly substantial similarity—into measurable dimensions to assess copyright risks in large language models beyond verbatim copying.
AI.motivation: Current safeguards against copyright infringement in LLMs mostly detect verbatim memorization, but EU copyright law is based on a broader standard of substantial similarity, which includes stylistic and structural aspects of works. This creates a compliance gap, as existing technical tools do not align with what the law protects. The authors are motivated to build a method that operationalizes legal standards into technical evaluation metrics, allowing more realistic assessment and mitigation of copyright risks in generative AI under EU law.
AI.method: The authors design PSALM, an LLM-as-a-judge framework composed of ten evaluators. These evaluators score generated outputs along multiple dimensions mapped to EU copyright doctrine: (1) computational overlap; (2) stylistic aspects such as writing style and narrative voice; (3) content aspects including characters, plot, scenes, and world-building; and (4) statutory exceptions like parody, pastiche, quotation, and scènes à faire. They then apply PSALM to Llama 3.2 models fine-tuned on translated historical Dutch literary texts, examining stylistic similarity before and after fine-tuning and evaluating the effect of Negative Preference Optimization (NPO) unlearning on reducing infringement-relevant similarities.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation with PSALM shows: (1) instruction-tuned Llama 3.2 models already exhibit noticeable stylistic similarity to the literary corpus even before fine-tuning; (2) fine-tuning on the corpus induces systematic stylistic appropriation across all dimensions relevant to infringement, not just literal copying but also higher-level narrative patterns and style; (3) applying Negative Preference Optimization unlearning significantly decreases measured similarity but does not fully eliminate residual stylistic patterns detectable by PSALM.
AI.conclusion: Safeguards that only target literal or near-verbatim copying are inadequate to address the broader copyright risks posed by LLMs under EU substantial similarity standards. PSALM serves as a legally informed, auditable framework to evaluate these risks across style and content dimensions and to assess the impact of mitigation techniques like unlearning. However, automated similarity scores are not themselves legal determinations of infringement and must be interpreted and validated by legal experts. The work highlights structural tensions between generative AI training practices and EU intellectual property law while offering a concrete tool to study and manage those tensions.

title: Cross-Domain Feature Expansion for Tabular Medical Data via Knowledge Graphs Injection
summary: Acquiring comprehensive cross-domain biomedical profiles is often costly and time-consuming, resulting in severe data scarcity in medical research. To address this challenge, we propose MedKGTab, a knowledge-injected framework specifically engineered for cross-domain feature expansion in tabular medical data. MedKGTab seeks to infer uncollected biomedical features from available ones by exploiting their inherent statistical dependencies and established medical correlations. By employing a row-column dual-attention mechanism, MedKGTab operates directly on raw structured tabular data, inherently capturing exact numerical distributions without the structural loss caused by tokenization. Crucially, MedKGTab integrates data-driven statistical priors with the SPOKE biomedical knowledge graph, achieving an optimal synergy between the data and knowledge channels. Within this synergy, the representations derived from the data channel are modulated by the injected biomedical knowledge, ensuring the final generated data are grounded in empirical medical research. Experimental results demonstrate that MedKGTab achieves high data fidelity and realistic data representation in cross-domain feature expansion. It outperforms both SOTA medical large models (e.g., Baichuan M3-plus) and specialized tabular models designed for medical data generation. Furthermore, MedKGTab consistently delivers superior performance across various data generation scenarios, whether inferring missing features within the same dataset or generalizing across different medical cohorts.

AI.tldr: MedKGTab is a knowledge-injected framework that expands missing biomedical features in tabular medical data by combining dual-attention over raw tables with a biomedical knowledge graph, achieving high-fidelity cross-domain data generation that outperforms existing models.
AI.motivation: Biomedical datasets are often incomplete and expensive to extend with new clinical, laboratory, or omics features. This lack of comprehensive, cross-domain profiles limits downstream research and modeling. Existing medical generative models and LLMs struggle with numerical precision and structured dependencies in tabular data, and they rarely leverage formal biomedical knowledge graphs effectively. A method is needed that can accurately infer uncollected features from observed ones, while grounding the inference in both data statistics and curated medical knowledge.
AI.method: The authors propose MedKGTab, a framework for cross-domain feature expansion in tabular medical data. It directly consumes raw structured tables and uses a row-column dual-attention mechanism to model dependencies both across patients (rows) and across features (columns), preserving exact numerical distributions without text tokenization. MedKGTab fuses two channels: (1) a data channel that learns statistical priors and dependencies from observed tabular data, and (2) a knowledge channel that injects biomedical relations from the SPOKE knowledge graph. Representations from the data channel are modulated by the knowledge-aware representations to generate missing or uncollected features for each patient. The model is trained to reconstruct or generate features with high fidelity under different missingness or cross-cohort scenarios.
AI.result: Experiments show that MedKGTab creates realistic and faithful synthetic or imputed biomedical features, achieving high data fidelity and more realistic data distributions than baselines. It outperforms state-of-the-art medical large models such as Baichuan M3-plus and dedicated tabular data generation models across multiple benchmarks. Performance gains are observed for both intra-dataset feature inference (filling in missing features within the same cohort) and cross-domain generalization (inferring features for different medical cohorts).
AI.conclusion: MedKGTab effectively addresses data scarcity in biomedical research by expanding cross-domain features in tabular datasets. Its combination of dual-attention over raw tables and biomedical knowledge graph injection yields more accurate, knowledge-grounded feature inference than existing medical LLMs and tabular generators. The framework is broadly applicable to diverse medical data generation and imputation tasks, improving the completeness and utility of clinical and biomedical datasets.

title: When the Database Fails: Prompting LLM Dialogue Agents for Safe Recovery in Task-Oriented Dialogue
summary: Large language models used in task-oriented dialogue often produce fluent but unsafe responses when backend database calls fail, return empty results, or surface mismatched information, inventing venues, confirmations, or booking details not grounded in the database. We study a lightweight prompting-based recovery approach that improves robustness without retraining or additional model calls. We compare three response strategies, including a guided recovery prompt conditioned on structured database status, across six open-weight model families (DeepSeek-R1, Gemma-2, Llama-3, Mistral, Phi-3, and Qwen-2.5) and four database conditions: empty result, wrong-domain retrieval, API error, and clean retrieval. Using fault-injected benchmarks built on two structurally different datasets, MultiWOZ 2.2 (5 domains) and SGD (20 domains), we find that naive agents hallucinate on 30.5% of failure turns on MultiWOZ and 20.9% on SGD. Our Guided-Retry strategy reduces hallucination by 50% on MultiWOZ (30.5 to 15.3%) and by 42% on SGD (20.9 to 12.2%) without retraining. However, residual hallucination remains substantial (6-37% across models), with wrong-domain failures the hardest case. Results are consistent across both datasets and all six model families, and human annotation shows substantial agreement while supporting the validity of the automatic commitment-safety metric.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that a simple, prompt-based guided-retry strategy, conditioned on database status, can significantly reduce hallucinated responses by task-oriented dialogue LLMs when database calls fail, without retraining or extra model calls, though notable hallucinations remain.
AI.motivation: Task-oriented dialogue systems powered by large language models often interact with backend databases (e.g., booking or information APIs). When those database calls fail, return empty or mismatched results, or otherwise misbehave, current LLM agents still tend to produce fluent but fabricated responses (hallucinations) such as confirming nonexistent bookings. This poses safety and reliability risks in practical applications. There is a need for lightweight mechanisms that make LLM agents robust to such database failures without costly retraining or complex multi-call pipelines.
AI.method: The authors introduce and compare three prompting-based response strategies for LLM agents, focusing on a Guided-Retry strategy that explicitly conditions the response on structured database status signals (e.g., empty result, wrong-domain retrieval, API error, clean retrieval). They evaluate six open-weight LLM families (DeepSeek-R1, Gemma-2, Llama-3, Mistral, Phi-3, Qwen-2.5) across four database conditions: empty result, wrong-domain retrieval, API error, and clean retrieval. To systematically probe robustness, they construct fault-injected benchmarks using two different multi-domain task-oriented dialogue datasets, MultiWOZ 2.2 (5 domains) and SGD (20 domains). They measure hallucination rates using an automatic commitment-safety metric and validate it with human annotation, also analyzing residual error patterns by failure type and model family.
AI.result: Naive LLM-based dialogue agents hallucinate frequently when database calls fail: 30.5% of failure turns on MultiWOZ 2.2 and 20.9% on SGD. The proposed Guided-Retry prompting strategy substantially reduces hallucinations without any model retraining or additional inference calls, lowering hallucination from 30.5% to 15.3% on MultiWOZ (a 50% relative reduction) and from 20.9% to 12.2% on SGD (a 42% relative reduction). Nonetheless, residual hallucination rates remain nontrivial, ranging from 6% to 37% depending on model and condition, with wrong-domain retrieval failures being the most challenging case. The improvements and error patterns are consistent across both datasets and all six model families, and human annotators show substantial agreement while supporting the reliability of the automatic metric.
AI.conclusion: A simple, lightweight, prompting-based guided-retry mechanism, driven by explicit database status, can meaningfully improve the robustness and safety of task-oriented dialogue LLMs in the face of database failures, without retraining or extra calls. However, even with this approach, hallucinations persist at non-negligible levels, especially under wrong-domain retrieval, indicating that more advanced methods or deeper integration between LLM reasoning and database grounding are needed. The consistent cross-dataset and cross-model results, along with human validation, suggest that commitment-safety metrics and structured failure-aware prompting are promising directions for safer deployed dialogue systems.

title: ClawArena-Team: Benchmarking Subagent Orchestration and Dynamic Workflows in Language-Model Agents
summary: Production large language-model (LLM) agents are increasingly deployed not as lone problem-solvers but as managers: a main model creates specialized subagents, delegates work, and orchestrates their parallel, asynchronous returns through dynamic workflows. Whether one model can actually run such a team is largely unmeasured: existing benchmarks score a policy's own task-solving or a fixed multi-agent system's emergent behavior, but none isolate the management ability of the single LLM acting as leader. We introduce ClawArena-Team, a benchmark of 41 multi-turn, multimodal, multi-directory scenarios spanning 258 evaluation rounds and 72 staged updates that measures this management ability. The main agent is deliberately constrained: it natively perceives only text and directly accesses only part of the workspace. It commands a fixed, locally served subagent pool, so score differences reflect management skill, not raw capability. All scoring is execution-based with no LLM judge: an overall score -- the Subagent-Management Score (SMS) -- multiplies task correctness by a least-privilege and modality-routing factor. Across twelve proprietary, community-hosted, and self-hosted models, experiments show that the management bottleneck is privilege granting rather than perception (no model exceeds 50% workspace-permission precision); that cost and management quality are decoupled (API cost spans over 100 times while the overall score spans under 4 times, with the cheapest open models on the Pareto frontier); and that most leaderboard scores cluster within a 9.9-point band while orchestration behaviors diverge by more than an order of magnitude. Code and data will be released.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes ClawArena-Team, a benchmark to measure how well a single LLM can manage and orchestrate a team of subagents, introducing a Subagent-Management Score (SMS) and revealing that privilege management, not perception, is the main bottleneck.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations mostly test either a single LLM solving tasks directly or emergent behavior in fixed multi-agent systems. They do not isolate and quantify the "management" ability of one LLM acting as a team leader that must coordinate specialized subagents. As LLMs are increasingly used as managers that delegate and orchestrate work, there is a need to systematically measure this specific capability.
AI.method: The authors build ClawArena-Team, a benchmark of 41 multi-turn, multimodal, multi-directory scenarios with 258 evaluation rounds and 72 staged updates. The leader LLM is constrained to text-only perception and partial workspace access, and must manage a fixed pool of locally served subagents. Performance is evaluated via execution-based metrics without LLM judges, producing a Subagent-Management Score (SMS) that combines task correctness with penalties or rewards tied to least-privilege permission granting and appropriate modality routing.
AI.result: Testing twelve proprietary, community-hosted, and self-hosted LLMs, the authors find: (1) management performance is primarily limited by poor privilege granting behavior, with no model achieving more than 50% precision in workspace-permission assignments; (2) monetary cost and management quality are largely decoupled, with API costs varying by over 100x but SMS varying by less than 4x and some inexpensive open models lying on the Pareto frontier; and (3) while aggregate scores occupy a narrow 9.9-point band, the qualitative orchestration patterns (e.g., how models delegate, route modalities, and stage workflows) vary by more than an order of magnitude.
AI.conclusion: A single LLM’s ability to act as an effective manager of subagents is a distinct capability that current systems only partially possess and that is not well predicted by model cost or raw capabilities. Privilege management is a key bottleneck, and even models with similar benchmark scores can differ greatly in orchestration style. ClawArena-Team provides a concrete, execution-based way to measure and compare this management ability, and the authors plan to release code and data to support further research and improvement in LLM-based orchestration.

title: LOPA: Enhancing Spoken Language Assessment via Latent Ordinal Prototype Alignment
summary: Fueled by increasing model scale and multimodal inputs, Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have emerged as a promising paradigm for Spoken Language Assessment (SLA). While effective, this paradigm often overlooks the intrinsic ordinal structure of language acquisition. This paper works around the necessity of large-scale MLLMs by introducing Latent Ordinal Prototype Alignment (LOPA) for SLA, a prototype-based regularizer that enforces an ordinal geometric prior directly on the latent space. Coupled with Semantic-Anchored Layer Routing (SALR), which adaptively harvests multi-depth representations from a frozen Whisper encoder, our framework achieves an RMSE of 0.361. This performance rivals billion-parameter systems without the need for LLM-based fine-tuning. Further analysis reveals that SALR's synergy with LOPA offers interpretable, criterion-aligned preferences, thereby supporting an efficient and ordinal-aware modeling alternative to current scaling-centric models for SLA.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a lightweight, interpretable framework for Spoken Language Assessment that matches billion-parameter MLLMs by enforcing ordinal structure in a frozen speech encoder’s latent space instead of scaling model size.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal large language model approaches for Spoken Language Assessment are effective but depend on huge models and often ignore that language proficiency scores are ordinal (ordered levels) rather than arbitrary categories or pure regression targets. The authors want a more efficient method that respects this ordinal nature and avoids expensive fine-tuning of large MLLMs.
AI.method: They introduce Latent Ordinal Prototype Alignment (LOPA), which adds a prototype-based regularization term that imposes an ordinal geometric prior directly on the encoder’s latent space, ensuring that learned representations align with ordered proficiency levels. They also propose Semantic-Anchored Layer Routing (SALR), a mechanism that adaptively selects and combines multi-depth (multi-layer) representations from a frozen Whisper speech encoder based on semantic relevance, instead of fine-tuning the encoder or using a huge MLLM backbone.
AI.result: The proposed system achieves a root mean squared error (RMSE) of 0.361 on Spoken Language Assessment, reaching performance comparable to billion-parameter multimodal LLM systems, despite not performing LLM-based fine-tuning and using a more compact architecture centered on a frozen Whisper encoder with LOPA/SALR modules.
AI.conclusion: By shaping the latent space with ordinal prototypes and adaptively routing semantic features from a frozen speech encoder, the framework delivers efficient, interpretable, and ordinal-aware Spoken Language Assessment. This challenges the current trend of relying on ever-larger multimodal LLMs and demonstrates that structure-aware modeling can rival scaling-centric approaches.

title: HealthAgentBench: A Unified Benchmark Suite of Realistic Agentic Healthcare Environments for Challenging Frontier AI Agents
summary: As AI agents become increasingly capable of complex, long-horizon reasoning, rigorous and holistic evaluation is essential for measuring progress toward real-world healthcare applications. We introduce HealthAgentBench, a suite of 54 agentic healthcare tasks across 7 categories each with its unique environment. The benchmark suite spans diverse workflows throughout the patient journey and a broad range of modalities. Each task is designed to replicate an end-to-end clinical workflow: given minimal instructions, an agent must explore raw healthcare data, operate within a complex environment, and execute multi-step solutions that go beyond naive prompting. A final task success rate is reported to provide a single, interpretable metric for HealthAgentBench overall performance for each agent. Evaluating frontier agents on HealthAgentBench, we find that overall task success rate remains low, underscoring the difficulty of the suite. The strongest and the most cost effective agent, Codex GPT-5.5, achieves only approximately 42% success rate. Beyond aggregate performance, HealthAgentBench reveals nuanced strengths and weaknesses across task categories. Frontier agents show promise in automatically developing research modeling pipelines over EHR data, but medical imaging remains especially challenging, particularly for Claude Code models, while Codex GPT-5.5 shows emerging capability. Tasks that combine large search spaces with compositional reasoning requirements remain difficult for all current agents. Together, these results suggest that HealthAgentBench provides a challenging and realistic benchmark with substantial room for future progress. We release our benchmark at https://github.com/microsoft/HealthAgentBench.

AI.tldr: HealthAgentBench is a benchmark of 54 complex, realistic healthcare agent tasks to evaluate long-horizon, multi-step reasoning of AI agents, showing current systems only reach about 42% success and leaving large room for improvement.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations for AI in healthcare often focus on narrow QA or simple tasks that don’t reflect end-to-end clinical workflows or long-horizon, agentic behavior. As AI agents become more capable, there is a need for a rigorous, holistic benchmark that mirrors real-world healthcare complexity across multiple data modalities, environments, and stages of the patient journey.
AI.method: The authors design HealthAgentBench, a benchmark suite of 54 agentic healthcare tasks grouped into 7 categories, each with its own environment. Tasks cover diverse patient workflows and modalities (e.g., EHR, imaging), and are structured as end-to-end clinical workflows: with minimal instructions, an AI agent must explore raw healthcare data, navigate complex environments, and execute multi-step plans beyond simple prompting. They evaluate multiple frontier AI agents on these tasks and measure overall task success rate as a single interpretable metric per agent, while also analyzing performance by task category.
AI.result: Overall task success rates on HealthAgentBench are low, indicating the suite is challenging. The best-performing and most cost-effective agent, Codex GPT-5.5, reaches only about 42% task success. Agents show relative strength in tasks involving automatic development of research modeling pipelines over EHR data. However, medical imaging tasks are especially difficult, with Claude Code models performing poorly, while Codex GPT-5.5 shows some emerging capability. Tasks that combine large search spaces with compositional reasoning remain challenging for all agents.
AI.conclusion: HealthAgentBench is a difficult and realistic benchmark for evaluating agentic AI in healthcare, exposing nuanced strengths and weaknesses across task types and modalities. Current frontier agents are far from solving the benchmark, underscoring substantial room for improvement in long-horizon, multimodal, and compositional reasoning for real-world healthcare applications. The benchmark is released publicly to support further research and development.

title: BlockPilot: Instance-Adaptive Policy Learning for Diffusion-based Speculative Decoding
summary: Speculative decoding accelerates inference by using a lightweight draft model to generate candidate tokens in parallel, and are then verified by the target model, enabling lossless acceleration. Recently, diffusion-based speculative decoding further improves parallelism by generating multiple tokens per forward pass via block-level diffusion, achieving state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance. However, existing methods adopt a fixed inference block size and assume a uniform optimal decoding strategy across all inputs. In this paper, we show that this assumption is suboptimal, as the optimal block size varies across samples and plays a critical role in speculative decoding performance. Moreover, these values exhibit a clear local structure, concentrating around the training block size, which reduces the problem to a low-dimensional and structured decision space. Based on these insights, we propose BlockPilot, a sample-adaptive policy that predicts the optimal block size from the prefilling representation. Specifically, we formulate block size selection as a lightweight policy learning problem and propose an instance-adaptive decision mechanism that predicts the optimal block size based on the representation of the prefilling stage. The prediction is performed only once after prefilling, allowing for seamless integration. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method is plug-and-play, introduces minimal overhead, and consistently improves efficiency, achieving an acceptance length of 5.92 and a 4.20$\times$ speedup on Qwen3-4B under temperature $T=1$.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces BlockPilot, a sample-adaptive policy that selects the optimal diffusion block size for speculative decoding, improving efficiency and speed over fixed-block approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing speculative decoding methods, including diffusion-based ones, use a fixed block size for all inputs, implicitly assuming a uniform optimal decoding strategy. However, this is suboptimal because the best block size varies per sample and significantly affects performance. The authors observe that optimal block sizes tend to cluster around the training block size, revealing a low-dimensional, structured decision space that can be exploited to improve decoding efficiency.
AI.method: The authors propose BlockPilot, which treats block size selection as a policy learning problem. From the representation obtained in the prefilling stage of the target model, a lightweight policy network predicts the optimal diffusion block size for that specific input. This decision is made once after prefilling and then used throughout decoding, making the approach plug-and-play. The method integrates with diffusion-based speculative decoding frameworks without modifying their core mechanisms, and leverages the observed local structure of optimal block sizes around the training block size.
AI.result: In experiments, BlockPilot consistently improves the efficiency of diffusion-based speculative decoding while adding minimal overhead. On the Qwen3-4B model with temperature T=1, it achieves an acceptance length of 5.92 and a 4.20× speedup compared to the baseline with fixed block size, and maintains lossless acceleration by preserving the target model’s output distribution.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, sample-specific selection of diffusion block size is crucial for maximizing speculative decoding performance. By exploiting the structured, low-dimensional nature of the optimal block size space and using prefilling representations for one-shot policy prediction, BlockPilot provides a practical, plug-and-play way to significantly accelerate inference over prior fixed-block diffusion-based speculative decoding methods.

title: AI-Assisted Discovery of Convex Relaxations via Dual Agents
summary: Recent work shows that LLM agents can improve sharp-constant inequalities by searching for extremal constructions, which yield upper bounds. We address the complementary side: a lower bound holds for every admissible function and follows from a convex relaxation of the nonconvex problem, with tighter relaxations giving stronger bounds. We instantiate the autoresearch paradigm to discover such relaxations: a coding agent proposes valid tightening constraints, a theory agent verifies each one and searches for counterexamples, and every reported bound is certified by an explicit dual-feasible point checked in rigorous interval arithmetic. On two optimization constants studied by \citet{tao2025alphaevolve} - the first autocorrelation inequality ($C_{6.2}$) and the Erdős minimum-overlap constant ($C_{6.5}$) - we improve the certified lower bounds from $1.28$ to $1.2937$ and from $0.379005$ to $0.37912$, respectively.

AI.tldr: They use an LLM-based autoresearch system to improve certified lower bounds on two extremal combinatorial/analytic constants by discovering better convex relaxations, achieving modest but rigorous numerical improvements.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM agents have been used to search for extremal examples that give sharp upper bounds in inequalities, but the complementary task of systematically improving lower bounds (which must hold for all admissible functions) is harder and less explored. Lower bounds can be obtained via convex relaxations of nonconvex optimization problems; tighter relaxations give better bounds, yet designing them is mathematically intricate and typically manual. The authors want an automated, rigorous pipeline that lets AI propose such relaxations and convert them into certified lower bounds.
AI.method: They formulate the lower-bound problem as a nonconvex optimization over admissible functions and then relax it to a convex program. An autoresearch framework is set up with multiple agents: (1) a coding agent that proposes new valid tightening constraints to strengthen the convex relaxation; (2) a theory agent that checks these constraints, including searching for counterexamples; and (3) a certification step that verifies every reported bound via an explicit dual-feasible solution, using rigorous interval arithmetic to ensure numerical correctness. This loop iteratively refines the relaxation and improves the certified lower bound.
AI.result: On two specific constants previously studied with LLM agents by Tao et al.—the first autocorrelation inequality constant C_{6.2} and the Erdős minimum-overlap constant C_{6.5}—their system improves the best known certified lower bounds. For C_{6.2}, the bound increases from 1.28 to 1.2937. For C_{6.5}, it increases from 0.379005 to 0.37912. These gains, though numerically modest, are fully rigorous and demonstrate that the framework can push sharp constants from below, complementing prior upper-bound work.
AI.conclusion: The paper shows that LLM-based autoresearch can do more than find extremal constructions for upper bounds: it can also help design tighter convex relaxations that yield rigorously certified lower bounds on difficult mathematical constants. Their multi-agent, verification-centric pipeline successfully improves two nontrivial constants, suggesting that automated discovery of valid inequalities and dual certificates is a viable methodology for advancing extremal combinatorics and analysis.

title: Linguistic Bias Mitigation for Spoofing Detection via Gradient Reversal and A Variational Information Bottleneck
summary: Rapid advancements in generative speech technology have compromised the reliability of voice biometrics. While current spoofing detectors excel when assessed under in-domain conditions, generalisation to out-of-domain settings is often poor. We show that this can be due to linguistic bias. A reliance on linguistic cues observed in training data can then compromise robustness to cross-data. We propose a linguistic-invariant spoofing detection framework utilizing teacher-student adversarial learning. The linguistic-aware teacher model, pre-trained on linguistic content of an external dataset, guides the student detector via gradient reversal to minimize the linguistic information. To prevent the inadvertent removal of non-linguistic cues, we incorporate a Variational Information Bottleneck to enable suppression of principal cues. Across nine DF Arena datasets, our method achieves up to a 36.2% relative reduction in the EER compare to the baseline.

AI.tldr: The paper tackles poor cross-domain generalization in spoofing detection for voice biometrics by removing linguistic bias using a teacher-student adversarial framework with an information bottleneck, achieving large EER reductions on multiple datasets.
AI.motivation: Generative speech technology makes it easy to create convincing spoofed speech, undermining voice biometrics. Existing spoofing detectors work well only when test data is similar to training data; they fail to generalize to new domains. One major reason is linguistic bias: models overfit to linguistic content (words, phonemes) present in training sets, instead of focusing on spoof-relevant, language-agnostic cues. There is a need for a spoofing detection method that is robust across datasets and languages by being invariant to linguistic information.
AI.method: They design a linguistic-invariant spoofing detector based on teacher-student adversarial learning. A teacher network is first pre-trained on an external dataset specifically to be linguistic-aware (i.e., to recognize or encode linguistic content). During training of the student spoofing detector, they use a gradient reversal layer to adversarially minimize the amount of linguistic information in the student’s internal representation, guided by the teacher. To avoid also discarding important non-linguistic spoofing cues, they integrate a Variational Information Bottleneck that encourages the model to selectively suppress only principal (linguistic) cues while retaining discriminative non-linguistic information useful for spoof detection. The framework is evaluated on nine DF Arena datasets.
AI.result: On nine different DF Arena datasets, the proposed method yields up to a 36.2% relative reduction in Equal Error Rate (EER) compared to a baseline spoofing detector, indicating substantially improved robustness and cross-dataset generalization.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that linguistic bias is a key factor hurting out-of-domain performance in spoofing detection and that explicitly enforcing linguistic invariance via teacher-student adversarial learning, combined with a Variational Information Bottleneck, can significantly improve generalization across datasets. Their framework better focuses on non-linguistic spoofing cues and achieves markedly lower EERs, suggesting a promising direction for robust voice anti-spoofing in the presence of advanced generative speech technologies.

title: Agentic RAG-VLM: Affordance-Aware Retrieval-Augmented Generation with Self-Reflective Planning for Robotic Grasping
summary: Generalizable robotic grasping in cluttered environments is essential for deploying manipulators in unstructured human spaces, yet existing VLM-based methods rely on visual similarity for object matching, neglecting physical affordances such as handle graspability and material fragility, and operate open-loop without spatial reasoning or failure recovery, limiting their effectiveness when objects are densely packed or physically diverse. We present Agentic RAG-VLM, a unified framework that bridges VLM-based semantic understanding and physically grounded grasp execution by integrating retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) with vision-language models (VLMs) and agentic self-reflective planning. Agentic RAG-VLM introduces three tightly coupled components: (1) a Hierarchical Affordance-Aware RAG (HAA-RAG) that encodes four-dimensional affordance descriptors, including type, material, fragility, and graspable region, and retrieves strategies by functional affordance compatibility rather than visual appearance; (2) a Scene Graph Constraint Reasoner that constructs spatial relationship graphs from VLM perception and translates proximity, occlusion, and support constraints into concrete grasp parameter adjustments; and (3) an Agentic Self-Reflective Pipeline with a 14-type failure taxonomy and three-level adaptive retry for closed-loop grasp refinement. Evaluated on a 12-task benchmark spanning single-grasp, interactive, and long-horizon scenarios with 360 trials per configuration, Agentic RAG-VLM achieves 78.3 percent overall success, a 53.3 percentage-point absolute gain over VLM-only baselines, demonstrating that affordance-aware retrieval, scene graph reasoning, and agentic recovery are jointly essential for robust manipulation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Agentic RAG-VLM, a framework that combines affordance-aware retrieval, scene-graph-based spatial reasoning, and self-reflective closed-loop control to achieve robust robotic grasping in cluttered, unstructured environments, significantly outperforming VLM-only baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing VLM-based grasping approaches mostly rely on visual similarity for object matching and operate open-loop, ignoring key physical affordances (e.g., handles, fragility, material) and lacking spatial reasoning and failure recovery. This makes them unreliable in cluttered, dense, and physically diverse environments common in real-world human spaces. The authors aim to build a more generalizable, physically grounded system that can plan grasps based on functional affordances, reason about object relationships in 3D scenes, and adaptively recover from failures.
AI.method: The authors introduce Agentic RAG-VLM, which tightly couples three components. (1) Hierarchical Affordance-Aware RAG (HAA-RAG): encodes objects using four-dimensional affordance descriptors (type, material, fragility, graspable region) and retrieves grasp strategies based on functional affordance compatibility rather than purely visual features. (2) Scene Graph Constraint Reasoner: constructs a spatial relationship graph from VLM perception capturing proximity, occlusion, and support, then converts these constraints into concrete adjustments to grasp parameters. (3) Agentic Self-Reflective Pipeline: defines a 14-type failure taxonomy and a three-level adaptive retry mechanism, enabling closed-loop diagnosis and refinement of grasps based on observed failures.
AI.result: On a 12-task benchmark that includes single-grasp, interactive, and long-horizon scenarios with 360 trials per configuration, Agentic RAG-VLM achieves an overall success rate of 78.3%. This represents a 53.3 percentage-point absolute improvement over VLM-only baselines, showing substantial gains in robustness and reliability in cluttered and diverse settings.
AI.conclusion: Affordance-aware retrieval, explicit scene-graph-based spatial reasoning, and agentic self-reflective recovery together are crucial for robust robotic manipulation in cluttered, unstructured environments. By moving beyond visual similarity to functional affordances and integrating closed-loop failure handling, Agentic RAG-VLM significantly improves grasp success rates and demonstrates a practical path toward more generalizable, physically grounded robotic grasping in real-world settings.

title: Clinically Structured Rank-Gated LoRA for Cross-Benchmark Medical Question Answering
summary: Medical multiple-choice question answering requires parameter-efficient adaptation across heterogeneous knowledge domains and reasoning operations. A medication question, a diagnostic decision, a public-health item, and a nursing-action item may require different low-rank updates, while some recall items should preserve the base model's representation with only mild adapter intervention. We propose BiRG-LoRA, a single-adapter rank-gated LoRA method for medical question answering. BiRG-LoRA keeps one LoRA module per target layer but makes its rank dimension input-conditioned: for each question, a biaxial gate combines hidden semantic evidence with specialty/profession priors, clinical-operation priors, and their interaction to select a sparse top-$k$ subset of rank atoms. A scalar injection coefficient further controls the strength of the selected adapter update. Under a matched Qwen3-8B CMB-source protocol, BiRG-LoRA achieves the highest four-benchmark macro-average accuracy among trainable PEFT baselines and matched routing controls: 69.31% averaged over CMB, CMExam, MedQA, and MedMCQA. It improves over MoELoRA by 0.89 percentage points while using 28.1% fewer trainable parameters; a paired, benchmark-stratified bootstrap over final predictions gives a 95% confidence interval of [0.42, 1.37] for this macro-average gain. Basic controls show that BiRG-LoRA also improves over vanilla LoRA r16 and active-rank-matched LoRA r4 by 0.83 macro points, and an evaluation-time weak-axis perturbation check suggests that performance is not brittle to moderate tag noise. The results support a bounded claim: clinically structured rank allocation improves cross-benchmark medical QA under a matched single-seed protocol, while training-seed variance remains future work.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces BiRG-LoRA, a rank-gated, single-adapter LoRA method that adaptively allocates low-rank capacity per question for medical multiple-choice QA, achieving state-of-the-art macro-average accuracy across four medical benchmarks with fewer parameters than prior PEFT baselines.
AI.motivation: Medical multiple-choice QA spans heterogeneous domains (e.g., medications, diagnosis, public health, nursing) and reasoning types, so a single uniform low-rank adaptation (LoRA) is inefficient: different question types need different adaptations, and some simple recall questions should minimally disturb the base model. Existing PEFT and routing-based LoRA approaches either lack fine-grained, input-conditioned rank control or are parameter-inefficient. The motivation is to design a parameter-efficient method that can adaptively adjust how much and which low-rank capacity is used per question, aligned with clinical structure and task priors, to improve cross-benchmark performance in medical QA.
