AI.method: The authors propose BiRG-LoRA, a “biaxial rank-gated” LoRA variant with a single adapter per target layer whose effective rank is dynamically selected per input. For each question, a biaxial gating mechanism combines (1) hidden semantic features from the model and (2) structured priors—medical specialty/profession tags, clinical operation types, and their interactions—to produce scores over a set of rank atoms. A sparse top-k selection chooses a subset of these rank atoms, effectively making the LoRA rank input-conditioned. A scalar injection coefficient modulates the overall strength of the selected adapter update. The approach is evaluated on a Qwen3-8B model using a CMB-source training protocol and compared against various PEFT baselines, including vanilla LoRA with fixed rank, MoELoRA, and routing controls, under a single-seed, parameter-matched setup.
AI.result: On four Chinese/English medical QA benchmarks (CMB, CMExam, MedQA, MedMCQA), BiRG-LoRA attains a macro-average accuracy of 69.31%, the highest among trainable PEFT baselines and matched routing controls under the same Qwen3-8B CMB-source protocol. It outperforms MoELoRA by 0.89 percentage points while using 28.1% fewer trainable parameters; bootstrap analysis over final predictions yields a 95% confidence interval of [0.42, 1.37] for this macro-average improvement, suggesting statistical robustness. BiRG-LoRA also improves over vanilla LoRA r16 and active-rank-matched LoRA r4 by 0.83 macro points. A weak-axis perturbation analysis at evaluation time, which injects moderate noise into the structured tag axis, shows that performance degrades only mildly, implying the method is not overly brittle to tag noise.
AI.conclusion: Clinically structured, input-conditioned rank allocation within a single-adapter LoRA framework can yield better cross-benchmark performance for medical multiple-choice QA than existing PEFT and routing-based LoRA methods, while using fewer trainable parameters. BiRG-LoRA demonstrates that leveraging both semantic evidence and clinically meaningful priors to sparsely activate low-rank components is beneficial and reasonably robust to moderate tag noise. The authors emphasize that their claim is bounded to a single-seed, matched-protocol setting and note that analyzing training-seed variance and broader robustness remains an open direction.

title: Towards Inclusive Mobility Modeling: Characterizing and Evaluating Elderly Trajectory Patterns in Urban Systems
summary: The rapid advance of smart cities increasingly depends on trajectory data mining, yet underrepresented demographic groups, particularly the elderly, are often sparsely represented in public mobility datasets. This underrepresentation can introduce systematic bias into mobility modeling and downstream urban planning. Using the 2016-2020 Jersey City subset of the Citi Bike System Data, this study quantitatively examines how the absence of underrepresented subgroups' mobility signatures affects mobility modeling, using synthetic trajectory generation as a case study. The analysis reveals that elderly riders exhibit a structurally distinct mobility signature, including localized activity spaces (958 m vs. 1,189 m for young riders), lower mobility entropy (1.82 vs. 4.15), and asymmetric off-peak temporal patterns. To demonstrate that relying on majority-dominated training data yields biased synthetic outcomes, we further evaluate both a first-order Markov chain and a Qwen3-4B model fine-tuned with QLoRA across three demographic training settings: the full population, young riders only, and elderly riders only. Results show that models trained on majority-dominated populations systematically misrepresent elderly mobility behavior, particularly for spatial mobility metrics. The Markov model trained on the full population overestimates elderly step length by 4.5% and dwell time by 8.9%, whereas the elderly-specific model achieves substantially lower errors across most metrics. Comparisons between the Markov and LLM-based frameworks further show that higher-capability models do not necessarily improve subgroup-level fidelity under limited demographic data. These findings underscore the importance of demographic representation in mobility modeling and its downstream applications for underrepresented populations.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that mobility models trained on majority (young) users misrepresent elderly riders’ behavior, demonstrating demographic bias in trajectory-based smart-city analytics and synthetic trajectory generation.
AI.motivation: Smart city planning and mobility services rely on trajectory data, but elderly and other underrepresented groups are sparsely present in public datasets. This can bias mobility models and lead to inequitable urban planning and services. The paper aims to quantify how missing elderly mobility data distorts trajectory-based models and synthetic trajectory generation.
AI.method: Using 2016–2020 Jersey City Citi Bike trip data, the authors (1) identify elderly vs. young riders and characterize their mobility signatures via spatial extent, entropy, and temporal patterns; (2) set up synthetic trajectory generation using a first-order Markov chain model and a Qwen3-4B LLM fine-tuned with QLoRA; (3) train these models under three demographic regimes: full population, young-only, elderly-only; (4) compare synthetic vs. real elderly trajectories across multiple spatial and temporal metrics to measure subgroup-specific modeling errors and bias.
AI.result: They find that elderly riders have systematically different mobility signatures (e.g., smaller activity spaces: 958 m vs. 1,189 m; lower entropy: 1.82 vs. 4.15; distinct off-peak time use). Models trained on majority-dominated data (full or young-only) systematically misrepresent elderly behavior. For instance, the Markov model trained on the full population overestimates elderly step length by 4.5% and dwell time by 8.9%, while an elderly-only model yields substantially smaller errors. Comparing Markov vs. LLM-based models shows that even more powerful models do not fix subgroup bias when subgroup data are sparse.
AI.conclusion: Demographic representation critically affects the fidelity of mobility modeling, especially for underrepresented groups like the elderly. Majority-dominated training sets yield biased synthetic trajectories and mischaracterize elderly mobility, and simply using higher-capacity models does not resolve this. To support equitable smart-city planning and applications, mobility datasets and modeling pipelines must explicitly address demographic coverage and subgroup-specific modeling fidelity.

title: Revising RVL-CDIP: Quantifying Errors and Test-Train Overlap
summary: RVL-CDIP is a popular dataset for benchmarking document classifiers. However, the dataset contains ample amounts of label errors as well as non-trivial amounts of test-train overlap, both of which may impact model performance metrics. In this paper, we address these two problems by (1) finding and fixing label errors, and (2) detecting and addressing test-train overlap. We produce several variations of RVL-CDIP with label error and test-train overlap fixes, and benchmark document classification performance on these new RVL-CDIP variations. Our rigorous analysis of RVL-CDIP finds that the corpus contains 12\% label error and approximately 35% test-train duplication. Remediation sees improvements in classification accuracy when errors are removed, but sees decreases in accuracy when duplicates are removed. We additionally evaluate models on RVL-CDIP-N, an out-of-distribution benchmark, finding that training on error-corrected data substantially improves OOD generalization, with supervised models gaining an average of 8.1 percentage points in accuracy and improvements as large as 14 percentage points.

AI.tldr: The paper audits and cleans the RVL-CDIP document classification dataset by correcting label errors and removing test-train duplicates, then measures how these fixes affect in-distribution and out-of-distribution performance.
AI.motivation: RVL-CDIP is a key benchmark for document classification, but it suffers from significant label noise and test-train overlap, which can inflate performance metrics and mislead research progress. The authors aim to quantify and remediate these issues to provide a more reliable benchmark and to understand how dataset quality affects both in-distribution accuracy and out-of-distribution generalization.
AI.method: The authors systematically search for and correct mislabeled samples in RVL-CDIP and detect instances where identical or near-identical documents appear in both training and test splits. They then construct several dataset variants that (a) fix label errors, (b) remove test-train duplicates, or (c) apply both corrections. Multiple document classification models are trained and evaluated on these variants as well as on an additional out-of-distribution benchmark, RVL-CDIP-N, to measure the impact of the corrections.
AI.result: Analysis reveals that the original RVL-CDIP dataset has about 12% label error and around 35% test-train duplication. Correcting label errors leads to higher classification accuracy, while removing test-train duplicates tends to lower reported accuracy on the benchmark, indicating that prior scores were inflated by memorization. On the OOD benchmark RVL-CDIP-N, models trained on the error-corrected datasets achieve substantially better generalization, with supervised models gaining on average 8.1 percentage points and up to 14 percentage points in accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that RVL-CDIP, in its standard form, significantly overestimates model performance due to noisy labels and extensive test-train overlap. Cleaning labels improves both in-distribution and out-of-distribution performance, while removing duplicates reveals more realistic, lower accuracy estimates. The authors recommend using their corrected RVL-CDIP variants for future benchmarking and highlight that dataset quality and deduplication are crucial for fair evaluation and robust generalization in document classification.

title: Long-term Traffic Simulation via Structured Autoregressive Modeling
summary: Interactive traffic simulation is a vital world model for autonomous driving. A central challenge in long-horizon simulation is modeling sustained multi-agent interactions, which is further exacerbated by dynamic token cardinality as agents continuously enter and exit the scene. In this work, we propose that the solution lies in the synergy between the architectural inductive biases and statistical priors of large-scale sequence models, e.g., Large Language Models (LLMs). Our probing experiments reveal that the transferability of attention mechanisms and the distributional consistency between motion tokens and natural language enable small-scale, heavily frozen LLMs to rapidly adapt to traffic modeling. Building on this insight, we introduce RosettaSim, a unified framework that projects scene topology, agent states, and spawning intents into a structured autoregressive stream with variable length, achieving both strong short-term accuracy and stable long-horizon simulation fidelity. Furthermore, evaluating extended rollouts presents yet another hurdle, as one-to-one agent correspondence inevitably fades over time. To address this, we introduce Retrieval-based Traffic Evaluation (RTE), which retrieves semantically similar real-world scenarios as context-aware reference anchors. Experiments on the Waymo Open Sim Agent Challenge (WOSAC) demonstrate that RosettaSim achieves state-of-the-art performance in both short- and long-term simulation. Furthermore, RTE exhibits a stronger correlation with standard metrics ($r=0.83$) than existing approaches ($r=0.74$), indicating improved alignment with long-horizon simulation fidelity.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RosettaSim, a framework that uses LLM-style sequence modeling to simulate multi-agent traffic over long horizons, and RTE, a retrieval-based metric for evaluating such simulations.
AI.motivation: Interactive traffic simulation for autonomous driving requires modeling multi-agent interactions over long time horizons while handling a variable number of agents entering and exiting the scene. Existing methods struggle with long-horizon fidelity and with how to evaluate simulations when agent correspondences to real data vanish over time. There is a need for models and metrics that better capture long-term, semantically realistic traffic behavior.
AI.method: The authors probe and analyze large language model (LLM) architectures for traffic modeling and find that attention transferability and distributional similarity between motion tokens and natural language make small, mostly frozen LLMs adaptable to traffic data. They propose RosettaSim, which encodes scene topology, agent states, and spawning intents into a single structured, variable-length autoregressive token stream and uses an LLM-style architecture to model it. For evaluation, they introduce Retrieval-based Traffic Evaluation (RTE), which assesses long rollouts by retrieving semantically similar real-world scenarios as context-aware references instead of relying on one-to-one trajectory matching.
AI.result: RosettaSim attains state-of-the-art results on the Waymo Open Sim Agent Challenge (WOSAC) for both short-term prediction accuracy and long-horizon simulation fidelity. RTE shows higher correlation (r = 0.83) with standard metrics compared with prior evaluation methods (r = 0.74), suggesting it better reflects the quality of long-horizon simulations.
AI.conclusion: Combining LLM-style sequence modeling with structured traffic tokenization enables robust, long-horizon multi-agent traffic simulation, even with small and largely frozen models. RosettaSim provides a unified autoregressive representation that handles dynamic agent sets and complex interactions, while RTE offers a more semantically meaningful and better-correlated way to evaluate long-rollout simulations, pushing forward the state of the art in interactive traffic simulation for autonomous driving.

title: Team MKC at CLPsych 2026: Capturing and Characterizing Mental Health Changes through Social Media Timeline Dynamics
summary: Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) have motivated their adoption across a wide range of domains, including Artificial Intelligence (AI) for mental health. Given the growing prevalence of mental health disorders worldwide and the limited accessibility of professional care, there is an increasing demand for scalable computational approaches that can assist in early detection and continuous monitoring of psychological well-being. In this area, ongoing efforts have focused on curating domain-specific datasets and leveraging them to develop LLMs capable of supporting holistic mental health analysis. In line with this direction, we propose an LLM-based pipeline for comprehensive mental health analysis over sequentially ordered user posts, as part of the CLPsych shared task. Our pipeline offers a unified framework that jointly enables post-level assessment and user-level temporal modeling.

AI.tldr: They build a single LLM-based pipeline that analyzes a person’s sequence of social media posts to assess mental health both at each post and across time for the whole user, for the CLPsych shared task.
AI.motivation: Mental health disorders are common and access to professional care is limited, so scalable computational tools are needed for early detection and ongoing monitoring of mental health using real-world data such as online posts. Prior work has focused on building domain-specific datasets and models, but there is a need for methods that can perform comprehensive, temporally-aware analysis at both the post and user level.
AI.method: They design an LLM-based pipeline tailored to mental-health related social media data. The pipeline takes a user’s posts in chronological order and uses a unified framework to perform post-level assessment (e.g., labeling or scoring each post) while also modeling temporal patterns across posts to infer higher-level, user-centric mental health signals. It is implemented and evaluated within the CLPsych shared task setting.
AI.result: Within the CLPsych shared task, the proposed pipeline successfully performs comprehensive mental health analysis on sequential user posts, demonstrating that an LLM-based, unified framework can handle both fine-grained post-level judgments and temporal user-level modeling. (Specific metrics are not stated in the abstract.)
AI.conclusion: An LLM-based, unified pipeline over temporally ordered user posts is a promising approach for holistic mental health analysis, supporting both detailed assessment of individual posts and temporal modeling of a user’s mental health trajectory in shared-task settings like CLPsych.

title: Thinking Before Retrieving: Robust Zero-Shot Composed Image Retrieval via Strategic Planning and Self-Criticism
summary: Composed image retrieval requires identifying a target image from a gallery by integrating a reference image with a textual modification instruction. In a training-free zero-shot setting, this task relies on constructing a retrieval-oriented textual query within a frozen vision--language embedding space at inference time. Existing approaches predominantly rely on a single-pass generation strategy that fuses the reference context and modification text into a unified description. This strategy makes it difficult to detect or correct semantic distortions and omissions during generation. Consequently, the preservation of reference attributes and the integration of textual requirements interfere with each other, which degrades retrieval precision. To address these challenges, we introduce PEC-CIR, a training-free framework that structures query construction as a multi-stage reasoning pipeline. The framework operates through a Planner--Executor--Critic architecture where the Planner extracts explicit constraints, the Executor generates multiple candidate target descriptions, and the Critic evaluates these candidates based on constraint compliance. By reframing query construction as a staged inference process instead of a single-pass output, PEC-CIR reduces the propagation of generative errors by explicitly evaluating candidate queries before retrieval, thereby improving retrieval stability.

AI.tldr: They propose PEC-CIR, a training-free, multi-stage reasoning framework for composed image retrieval that builds better text queries to match target images by planning, generating, and critiquing candidate descriptions.
AI.motivation: Single-pass generation of retrieval queries that combine a reference image and modification text often introduces semantic errors, loses important attributes, and cannot correct mistakes after generation in zero-shot, training-free composed image retrieval.
AI.method: They design PEC-CIR, which structures query construction as a Planner–Executor–Critic pipeline inside a frozen vision–language embedding space. The Planner extracts explicit constraints from the reference and textual modification, the Executor uses these constraints to generate multiple candidate target descriptions, and the Critic evaluates and selects candidates based on how well they satisfy the constraints before using them for retrieval.
AI.result: PEC-CIR reduces the propagation of generative errors and improves retrieval precision and stability compared to single-pass query generation methods in zero-shot composed image retrieval, by explicitly evaluating and filtering candidate textual queries prior to retrieval.
AI.conclusion: Staging the query construction process into planning, execution, and critique within a frozen vision–language model leads to more reliable query texts that better preserve reference attributes and integrate modifications, thereby improving training-free composed image retrieval performance.

title: Building an ASR Solution for Training and Assessing Children's Reading
summary: Automatic speech recognition for children's reading remains underdeveloped for most African languages, including Bambara, despite its potential value for reproducible literacy assessment. We present an open-source system for assessing children's reading in Bambara, developed through an end-to-end process linking field data collection, benchmark construction, model adaptation, a reading application, and classroom validation. A mobile collection and assessment app was used to collect 55 hours of raw reading speech from 60 children, from which we construct a public benchmark for Bambara child-reading assessment. Fine-tuning experiments compare Soloni, a Bambara-adapted Fast-Conformer ASR framework with TDT and CTC decoders, with QuartzNet, a compact convolutional ASR architecture. The best Soloni model reduces WER from 0.42 to 0.22 and CER from 0.15 to 0.08, substantially outperforming QuartzNet on the isolated benchmark. The experiments further show that repeated readings of the same texts provide architecture-dependent benefits: they substantially improve QuartzNet but add only marginal gains for Soloni, while SpecAugment regulates training without exceeding the best unaugmented configuration. Disaggregated analysis identifies children under 10 as the main source of residual errors, motivating targeted collection from younger readers. Ten classroom trials supported continued use of the application.

AI.tldr: Open-source ASR-based system for assessing Bambara children’s reading, from data collection to classroom validation, with strong WER/CER gains using an adapted Fast-Conformer model.
AI.motivation: Children’s reading assessment in African languages like Bambara lacks automatic, reproducible tools due to limited ASR resources; there is a need for language- and child-specific systems and public benchmarks to support literacy evaluation and research.
AI.method: Collected 55 hours of Bambara reading speech from 60 children via a mobile app; built a public benchmark; fine-tuned two ASR approaches—Soloni (Bambara-adapted Fast-Conformer with TDT and CTC decoders) and QuartzNet—under different training setups (with/without repeated readings and SpecAugment); integrated the best-performing model into a reading app and ran classroom trials.
AI.result: The best Soloni model reduced WER from 0.42 to 0.22 and CER from 0.15 to 0.08, clearly outperforming QuartzNet; repeated readings substantially helped QuartzNet but only slightly improved Soloni; SpecAugment regularized training but did not surpass the best non-augmented model; error analysis showed that most remaining errors stem from children under 10; classroom deployments (10 trials) indicated the app is usable in real settings.
AI.conclusion: An end-to-end, open-source pipeline for Bambara child-reading assessment is feasible and effective, with a Fast-Conformer-based ASR model delivering strong accuracy improvements and practical classroom utility; further gains likely require targeted data collection from younger readers.

title: Agentic-Ideation: Sample Efficient Agentic Trajectories Synthesis for Scientific Ideation Agents
summary: Ideation plays a pivotal role in scientific discovery. Recent LLM, especially AI Scientist systems, show promising potential for automated ideation. However, existing approaches predominantly rely on pre-defined agentic workflows. This constraint severely limits the flexibility required to navigate the vast search space of scientific literature and the complex action space of research reasoning. Recently, training Agentic LLMs has emerged as a promising direction, offering flexible reasoning frameworks and the capability for autonomous tool utilization. However, there remains a non-trivial challenge: applying previous agentic data synthesis methods to scientific ideation suffers from prohibitively high data synthesis cost. To bridge this gap, we propose Agentic-Ideation, a novel framework comprising an automated trajectory synthesis pipeline and a specialized agentic LLM trained for scientific ideation. Specifically, we first define a comprehensive tool space incorporating three external tools and three cognitive tools. Then we introduce an Oracle-Guided Data Synthesis strategy. By leveraging a reference idea as oracle guidance, this approach steers the multi-agent system to efficiently reconstruct the logical reasoning and tool invocation paths, transforming aimless trial-and-error into directed trajectory generation. Finally, we train the agent on these synthesized trajectories, employing a masking strategy on tool execution results. This ensures the model focuses on decision-making logic without interference from external feedback. Experimental results demonstrate that our method outperforms the SOTA workflow-based baseline by \textbf{11.91\%} in overall quality. Furthermore, our approach improves the sample efficiency of high-quality data synthesis by \textbf{over 10$\times$}.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Agentic-Ideation, an agentic-LLM framework for scientific ideation that replaces rigid workflows with a trained agent using oracle-guided trajectory synthesis, improving ideation quality by 11.91% and making data synthesis over 10× more sample-efficient.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based scientific ideation systems rely on fixed, hand-crafted workflows that restrict flexibility in exploring large literature and reasoning spaces. Training agentic LLMs is promising, but prior data synthesis methods are too costly to apply directly to scientific ideation, limiting practical deployment of more flexible, tool-using research agents.
AI.method: They define a rich tool space combining three external tools and three cognitive tools for research reasoning. Then they introduce an Oracle-Guided Data Synthesis strategy: given a reference (oracle) idea, a multi-agent system is guided to reconstruct the reasoning and tool-use trajectories that could have led to that idea, replacing blind trial-and-error with directed trajectory generation. These synthesized trajectories are used to train a specialized agentic LLM for ideation, with a masking strategy applied to tool execution results so the model learns decision-making logic rather than overfitting to specific external feedback outputs.
AI.result: On scientific ideation benchmarks, the trained Agentic-Ideation model outperforms the state-of-the-art workflow-based baseline by 11.91% in overall idea quality. The oracle-guided synthesis procedure also increases the sample efficiency of generating high-quality training trajectories by more than 10× compared to prior agentic data synthesis methods.
AI.conclusion: Agentic-Ideation demonstrates that a trained agentic LLM, supported by oracle-guided trajectory synthesis and a carefully designed tool space, can surpass rigid workflow systems for scientific ideation both in performance and data efficiency. Masking tool outputs during training helps the model internalize generalizable decision-making, suggesting a scalable path toward more autonomous and effective AI research assistants.

title: FinPersona-Bench: A Benchmark for Longitudinal Psychometric Stability of Autonomous Financial Agents
comment: 29 pages, includes figures and tables; formalizes Mandate Salience Decay and introduces FinPersona-Bench
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as autonomous financial agents initialized with explicit behavioral mandates such as "preserve capital" or "avoid speculative bets" that are meant to govern every decision throughout deployment. In practice, however, as market context accumulates over long horizons, these mandates gradually lose their behavioral influence, a phenomenon we formalize as Mandate Salience Decay (MSD). To measure MSD objectively, we introduce FinPersona-Bench, a simulation benchmark in which a synthetic market decouples observable price from hidden fundamental value, enabling falsifiable evaluation across three failure modes: trading without signal in calm markets, panic-selling during crashes, and ignoring fundamental value during speculative bubbles. Evaluating 18 leading frontier and open-source LLMs, each assigned one of three behavioral profiles ranging from strict capital preservation to aggressive growth, shows that MSD compounds over time and is model-dependent. In crash scenarios, the behavioral gap between static agents and those receiving periodic mandate re-grounding grows 4.4x from the first to the final quarter of the simulation. The effects of mandate re-grounding are not uniformly positive: it consistently helps conservative agents in low-signal markets but actively worsens behavior for aggressive agents in the same setting. These findings suggest that reliable long-horizon deployment requires selective, mandate-aware re-grounding based on agent profile and market regime.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces and analyzes Mandate Salience Decay (MSD) in LLM-based financial agents, showing that initial behavioral mandates lose influence over time and proposing a benchmark to measure and mitigate this effect via selective re-grounding.
AI.motivation: LLMs are being used as autonomous financial agents with explicit behavioral mandates (e.g., preserve capital), but these mandates appear to lose influence as the agents operate over long horizons. There is a need to formalize and objectively measure this degradation to ensure safe and reliable deployment in financial markets.
AI.method: The authors formalize the concept of Mandate Salience Decay (MSD) and build FinPersona-Bench, a synthetic financial market simulation where observable prices can diverge from hidden fundamental values. They define three failure modes (trading without signal in calm markets, panic-selling in crashes, and ignoring fundamentals in bubbles). They then evaluate 18 frontier and open-source LLMs, each configured with one of three behavioral profiles from conservative to aggressive, both with and without periodic mandate re-grounding, tracking performance over time.
AI.result: MSD is shown to increase over time and varies by model. In crash scenarios, the behavioral gap between agents with static mandates and those with periodic re-grounding becomes 4.4 times larger from the first to the last quarter of the simulation. Re-grounding improves the behavior of conservative agents in low-signal markets but degrades the behavior of aggressive agents in the same setting, indicating that mandate refresh is not uniformly beneficial.
AI.conclusion: Long-horizon deployment of LLM-based financial agents is vulnerable to Mandate Salience Decay. Effective mitigation requires mandate-aware, selective re-grounding strategies that consider both the agent’s behavioral profile and the prevailing market regime, rather than applying uniform periodic reminders.

title: Delta-JEPA: Learning Action-Sensitive World Models via Latent Difference Decoding
summary: Learning visual world models for planning requires compact latent dynamics that remain sensitive to actions, yet reconstruction-free joint-embedding objectives can collapse to action-insensitive representations. We propose Delta-JEPA, an end-to-end reconstruction-free world model that augments latent forward prediction with a Latent Difference Action Decoder (LDAD). Unlike inverse decoders that infer actions from concatenated endpoint embeddings, LDAD reconstructs the executed action from the latent displacement between consecutive observations. This displacement-level supervision directly regularizes transition geometry: adjacent embeddings cannot collapse without losing action information, and different actions are encouraged to induce distinguishable latent changes for rollout-based planning. Delta-JEPA uses only latent prediction and action reconstruction, avoiding pixel reconstruction and distribution-matching regularizers. Across four visual continuous-control tasks, Delta-JEPA improves planning over JEPA-based and representation-learning world model baselines. Ablations show that displacement-based action decoding is consistently more effective than endpoint concatenation, and action-sensitivity analyses show clearer action-conditioned latent responses. These results indicate that supervising latent differences is a simple and effective mechanism for collapse-resistant and action-sensitive world model learning.

AI.tldr: Delta-JEPA is a reconstruction-free visual world model that prevents action-insensitive collapse by decoding actions from latent differences between consecutive states instead of raw embeddings, yielding more action-sensitive dynamics and better planning.
AI.motivation: Reconstruction-free joint-embedding world models are attractive because they avoid pixel reconstruction and complex likelihood modeling, but they tend to learn latent spaces that ignore actions, causing representation collapse and poor planning. There is a need for a simple mechanism that enforces action-sensitivity in the latent dynamics without reintroducing reconstruction losses or heavy regularization.
AI.method: The authors introduce Delta-JEPA, an end-to-end world model that performs latent forward prediction and augments it with a Latent Difference Action Decoder (LDAD). Instead of predicting actions from concatenated embeddings of consecutive observations, LDAD takes the latent displacement (difference) between successive embeddings and reconstructs the executed action. This displacement-level supervision constrains the geometry of the latent transition: adjacent states must remain separated in a way that preserves action information, and different actions must produce distinguishable latent changes. The model trains only on latent prediction and action reconstruction, avoiding pixel-space reconstruction and distribution-matching losses. They evaluate on four visual continuous-control tasks with rollout-based planning and perform ablations comparing displacement-based decoding to endpoint concatenation.
AI.result: On four visual continuous-control benchmarks, Delta-JEPA yields better planning performance than JEPA-style and other representation-learning-based world model baselines. Ablation studies show that decoding actions from latent differences consistently outperforms decoding from concatenated endpoints. Additional analyses demonstrate that the learned latent representations exhibit clearer, more distinct responses conditioned on different actions, indicating improved action sensitivity and reduced representation collapse.
AI.conclusion: Supervising latent differences via a Latent Difference Action Decoder provides a simple and effective way to enforce action-sensitive latent dynamics in reconstruction-free world models. By using only latent prediction and action reconstruction, Delta-JEPA avoids pixel reconstruction and complex regularizers, yet learns collapse-resistant, action-aware representations that improve planning performance over existing JEPA-based and representation-learning baselines.

title: AutoTrainess: Teaching Language Models to Improve Language Models Autonomously
summary: Training language models (LMs) remains a highly human-intensive process, even as frontier language model agents become increasingly capable at software engineering and other long-horizon tasks. A central challenge is that autonomous post-training is not just a coding problem: it requires the agent to repeatedly plan iterations, construct benchmark-aligned data, run stable training jobs, evaluate checkpoints, and preserve experiment state across many hours of interaction. We present AutoTrainess, a LM agent that exposes these operations as a repository of agent-computer interfaces for planning, data preparation, training, evaluation, and logging. Rather than leaving the agent to operate in a raw CLI environment with an underspecified action space, AutoTrainess externalizes prior human experience as explicit workflows, rules, and execution constraints that guide the agent toward effective and reliable training behavior. On PostTrainBench, AutoTrainess consistently outperforms CLI-only baselines, achieving 26.94 average score with GPT-5.4 (Codex) versus 23.21 for CLI-only. It also generalizes across models and harnesses, improving DeepSeek-V4-Flash (OpenCode) from 12.13 to 19.58.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AutoTrainess, an LM agent framework that automates and stabilizes the end-to-end post-training pipeline for language models using structured agent-computer interfaces and workflows, achieving better performance than CLI-only baselines on PostTrainBench and across different models.
AI.motivation: Although advanced language models are strong at coding and complex tasks, using them to autonomously post-train models is still very human-intensive. Autonomous post-training involves much more than issuing code commands: it needs long-horizon planning, benchmark-aligned data creation, robust training execution, evaluation, and experiment management over many hours. Existing approaches typically place the agent in a raw command-line interface with a very large, under-specified action space, causing unreliable behavior. The authors want a way to inject prior human MLOps and training know-how into the agent’s environment so that it can reliably run and improve LM training loops with minimal human supervision.
AI.method: The authors design AutoTrainess, an LM agent that interacts not just with a generic CLI but with a curated set of agent-computer interfaces that encapsulate typical post-training tasks: planning, data preparation, training orchestration, evaluation, and logging/experiment management. These interfaces expose explicit workflows, rules, and execution constraints, effectively constraining and guiding the agent’s choices. Instead of exploring a free-form shell, the agent follows structured operations that embody human best practices for running training jobs. They evaluate AutoTrainess on PostTrainBench by comparing its performance when driven by frontier LMs (e.g., GPT-5.4 Codex and DeepSeek-V4-Flash) against baselines that only have CLI access.
AI.result: On the PostTrainBench benchmark, AutoTrainess with GPT-5.4 (Codex) achieves an average score of 26.94, outperforming a CLI-only baseline with the same underlying LM that scores 23.21. When paired with DeepSeek-V4-Flash (OpenCode), AutoTrainess improves performance from 12.13 to 19.58, showing that the framework generalizes beyond a single model and harness. These gains indicate more effective and stable autonomous post-training than with unconstrained CLI interaction.
AI.conclusion: Structuring the environment and interfaces available to LM agents for post-training—via explicit workflows, rules, and constraints—substantially improves their ability to autonomously manage the end-to-end training pipeline. AutoTrainess demonstrates that encoding human operational knowledge as agent-computer interfaces yields more effective and reliable training behavior than letting agents operate over a raw CLI, and the approach generalizes across different base models and evaluation harnesses.

title: Embodied CAD: Solver-Grounded LLM Agents for Parametric B-Rep Assembly Modeling
comment: This paper contains 12 pages, 7 figures. This is an original unpublished manuscript submitted to the arXiv preprint server, with no prior publication or conference presentation
summary: Large language models can write plausible CAD scripts, but reliable industrial CAD modeling requires more than syntactically valid code: every feature, placement, and assembly relation must be accepted by an exact geometric kernel while remaining editable as parametric boundary representation geometry. We present Embodied CAD, solver-grounded LLM agents for parametric B-Rep assembly modeling. Instead of generating a complete script in one pass, the agent iteratively selects actions from a stratified L0-L4 CAD skill library, resolves them into typed geometric operations, executes them in a CAD backend, and uses solver feedback to plan, repair, and learn. The framework combines action grammar constraints, deterministic parameter resolution, and solver-derived rewards for supervised warm-up and GRPO-style refinement. We evaluate Embodied CAD on multi-step mechanical, industrial equipment, and mold-oriented assembly tasks using solver-aligned metrics: executable rate, skill accuracy, operation-family accuracy, exact policy accuracy, and task completion success. The results show that solver-grounded planning executes all strong-planner workflows in the current benchmark, while learned controllers reach high executable rates and expose the remaining gap between valid tool calls and exact long-horizon policy prediction.

AI.tldr: They build an LLM-based CAD agent that interacts with a geometric solver step-by-step, using a hierarchy of CAD skills, and show it can robustly execute complex parametric assembly workflows.
AI.motivation: Existing LLMs can generate CAD scripts that look correct but often fail when executed in industrial-grade geometric kernels, and they don’t ensure parametric, editable B-Rep models or reliable multi-step assemblies. There is a need for CAD modeling agents that are grounded in actual solver feedback and can robustly complete long-horizon, parametric assembly tasks rather than just outputting syntactically valid code.
AI.method: They propose Embodied CAD, an LLM agent that does not output an entire CAD script at once but instead iteratively chooses actions from a layered L0–L4 CAD skill library. Each chosen skill is resolved to typed geometric operations and executed in a CAD backend (geometric solver). The system uses action grammars to constrain valid sequences, deterministic parameter resolution to translate high-level decisions into concrete geometry, and solver feedback as rewards or signals. The training pipeline includes supervised warm-start and GRPO-style (policy gradient with rewards from the solver) refinement, effectively using the solver as an environment for planning, repairing failed attempts, and learning better control policies.
AI.result: On benchmarks involving multi-step mechanical parts, industrial equipment, and mold-oriented assemblies, they measure performance through solver-aligned metrics such as whether actions execute, whether the right skills and operation families are chosen, whether the overall policy matches ground truth, and whether tasks are fully completed. The solver-grounded planning system can successfully execute all workflows that are solvable by a strong planner in the benchmark. The learned controllers obtain high rates of executable operations, though there remains a nontrivial gap between generating valid tool calls and achieving fully correct long-horizon policies.
AI.conclusion: Grounding CAD agents in a geometric solver and structuring their behavior with a hierarchical skill library and action grammar leads to robust execution of parametric B-Rep assembly tasks. While the planning framework can match strong planners on current benchmarks, the learning-based controllers, though highly executable, still need improvement to close the gap to exact long-horizon policy prediction, highlighting future work for better learning and planning integration in CAD automation.

title: Robust Text Watermarking for Large Language Models via Dual Semantic Embeddings
summary: This work presents Dual-Embedding Watermarking (DEW), a semantic watermarking scheme for large language models (LLMs) that leverages contextual and token-level embeddings to enhance robustness against paraphrasing and translation. DEW utilizes a signal-processing methodology, applying algebraic vector-space operations to \mbox{token and context embeddings to derive a watermark signal that degrades gracefully under semantic shifts. The method obfuscates the watermark by projecting embedding vectors through pseudo-random matrices seeded with a secret key. Relevant distributions derived from the underlying algebra are evaluated and employed for statistical testing and benchmarking of DEW. Experimental results across multiple LLMs indicate that DEW improves post-paraphrase detection while maintaining competitive text quality, and remains detectable after translation, even when prior semantic watermarks degrade significantly. These findings position DEW as a practical and robust solution for safeguarding LLM-generated text and addressing critical issues in responsible AI deployment.

AI.tldr: Introduces Dual-Embedding Watermarking (DEW), a semantic watermarking method for LLM outputs that stays detectable after paraphrasing and translation by operating in token and context embedding space with signal-processing style operations.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM watermarking methods are fragile under semantic transformations such as paraphrasing and translation, which limits their usefulness for tracing or verifying AI-generated text in realistic settings. There is a need for a more robust, practical watermark that survives these common transformations while preserving text quality for responsible AI deployment and content provenance.
AI.method: DEW embeds a watermark signal directly in the model’s semantic representation space by operating on both token-level and context-level embeddings. It treats watermarking as a signal-processing problem: algebraic vector-space operations are applied to these embeddings to construct a watermark signal that degrades gradually rather than disappearing under semantic shifts. To hide the watermark and make it key-based, the embedding vectors are projected through pseudo-random matrices seeded by a secret key. The authors then analytically derive relevant statistical distributions from the algebraic construction and use them to design hypothesis tests and benchmarks for watermark detection and evaluation.
AI.result: Across multiple LLMs, DEW shows higher watermark detectability after paraphrasing compared with prior semantic watermarking baselines, while keeping output quality similar or competitive. It also remains statistically detectable after translation, a setting where other semantic watermarking techniques lose much of their signal. The paper quantifies this with detection metrics and text quality assessments, demonstrating improved robustness to common semantic transformations.
AI.conclusion: DEW offers a more robust, semantically grounded watermarking scheme for LLM-generated text by leveraging dual (token and context) embeddings and signal-processing style embedding manipulations with key-based obfuscation. Its resilience to paraphrasing and translation, together with maintained text quality, make it a practical candidate for real-world content tracking and provenance in responsible AI systems.

title: Spatial Reasoning via Modality Switching Between Language and Symbolic Representation
summary: Human reasoning is inherently multimodal: when problems become difficult, we rarely think in words alone. We often externalize our reasoning by sketching diagrams or drawing grids to understand the underlying conceptual structure and avoid mistakes. Building on this premise, our research investigates: (a) whether grounding multi-hop textual-spatial stories into geometry-aware modalities, such as layouts or grids, improves reasoning compared to natural language-based inference; and (b) whether a model can decide when to rely on natural language reasoning and when to switch to a structured modality. We address these questions by introducing a switching metric based on trustworthiness and complexity signals, which estimates when grounding a spatial story into structure is likely to improve performance. This takes a first step toward principled modality selection in Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning. Across our settings, switching from natural language-based reasoning to a grid-based representation improves LLM performance by up to 42\%, highlighting the importance of modality choice in shaping reasoning outcomes.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how switching between natural-language and geometry-aware (grid/layout) representations affects multi-hop spatial reasoning in LLMs, and proposes a metric to decide when to switch.
AI.motivation: Humans naturally use diagrams and spatial sketches to solve complex problems, especially those involving spatial relations and multi-step reasoning. Current LLMs mostly reason in natural language, which may be suboptimal for spatially structured tasks. The authors aim to test whether grounding such tasks into explicit geometric structures improves reasoning, and how to automatically decide when to use which modality.
AI.method: They construct multi-hop textual-spatial stories and compare LLM performance when reasoning purely in natural language versus when the stories are grounded into geometry-aware formats like grids or layouts. They then design a switching metric that uses trustworthiness (e.g., model confidence or consistency) and task complexity signals to predict when a structured, grid-based modality is likely to help. The system can then dynamically choose between staying in natural language or switching to a grid representation for a given instance.
AI.result: In empirical evaluations across their tasks, using the switching strategy to move from natural-language reasoning to grid-based representations yields performance gains of up to 42% over purely language-based inference, demonstrating substantial benefits from appropriate modality selection.
AI.conclusion: Modality choice significantly affects LLM reasoning on spatial, multi-hop problems. Explicitly grounding such tasks into structured, geometry-aware representations can greatly improve accuracy, and a metric-based switching mechanism is a promising first step toward principled, automatic modality selection in LLM-based systems.

title: CLExEval: A Human-in-the-Loop Framework for Qualitative Evaluation of LLM Clinical Reasoning
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve strong results on many medical benchmarks, but their clinical reasoning remains difficult to evaluate reliably. A central risk is an evaluation illusion: fluent and well-structured explanations can appear clinically convincing even when the final diagnosis is incorrect. We introduce CLExEval, a human-in-the-loop framework for evaluating LLM clinical reasoning under progressive information masking. CLExEval combines 5,600 expert-physician annotations with 200 clinical reasoning traces derived from 40 rare diagnostic cases. Our analysis identifies three recurring failure patterns: (i) verbosity bias, where GPT-4o-mini's diagnostic accuracy drops from 95.0% to 32.5% under information scarcity; (ii) a hidden knowledge paradox, where a specialist model reaches 92.5% maximum diagnostic potential but fails to retrieve that knowledge reliably in verbose contexts; and (iii) a 68.6% reasoning-to-output mismatch, where correct diagnoses appear in reasoning traces but are not reflected in final answers. We further evaluate the LLM-as-a-Judge paradigm on a human-verified failure set (n = 142). GPT-4o-mini approved 47.9% of clinically incorrect outputs, while HuatuoGPT-o1 approved all validly scored failures and showed a positive self-preference bias. These results suggest that standalone automated clinical evaluations can substantially overestimate clinical reliability without expert-grounded validation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CLExEval, a human-in-the-loop framework to more reliably evaluate clinical reasoning of LLMs by masking information and using expert annotations, revealing that current models appear convincing but often fail under information scarcity and exhibit major reasoning-output mismatches.
AI.motivation: Existing medical benchmarks focus on final answers and are vulnerable to an “evaluation illusion,” where LLMs produce fluent, plausible clinical explanations that mask incorrect diagnoses. This makes it hard to know how clinically reliable LLMs really are, especially in challenging, rare diagnostic cases. The authors want a more trustworthy way to probe how models reason clinically, how they use available information, and whether automated evaluation methods (LLM-as-a-judge) are themselves reliable.
AI.method: They build CLExEval, which (1) uses 40 rare diagnostic cases with 200 LLM-generated reasoning traces, (2) progressively masks clinical information to simulate data scarcity, and (3) collects 5,600 annotations from expert physicians on these traces. They then analyze failure patterns in GPT-4o-mini and a specialist medical model, including how performance changes with verbosity and information masking, how much of the model’s latent knowledge is actually retrieved, and how often correct reasoning fails to propagate to final answers. Finally, they test LLM-as-a-judge by having models (GPT-4o-mini and HuatuoGPT-o1) evaluate a curated set of human-verified failures and quantify approval rates and biases.
AI.result: They identify three main failure modes: (i) verbosity bias – GPT-4o-mini’s diagnostic accuracy sharply degrades from 95.0% to 32.5% when information is scarce and the model is verbose; (ii) a hidden knowledge paradox – a specialist model shows high maximum diagnostic potential (92.5%) based on its reasoning traces but fails to consistently retrieve this knowledge in realistic, verbose settings; and (iii) reasoning-to-output mismatch – in 68.6% of cases, correct diagnoses appear somewhere in the reasoning but are not selected as the final answer. In LLM-as-a-judge experiments on 142 validated failure cases, GPT-4o-mini incorrectly approves 47.9% of clinically wrong outputs, while HuatuoGPT-o1 approves all properly scored failures and exhibits a self-preference bias when judging outputs.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs can look clinically competent while being unreliable, especially under information scarcity and in rare, complex cases. Many failures come not from total lack of knowledge, but from poor retrieval and unstable mapping from reasoning to final answers. Additionally, using LLMs to automatically judge clinical outputs can substantially overestimate model quality and can be biased. Therefore, trustworthy clinical evaluation requires expert-grounded, human-in-the-loop frameworks like CLExEval rather than relying solely on automated LLM-based benchmarks.

title: Benchmarking Large Language Models on Floating-Point Error Classification
summary: This paper investigates the capability of Large Language Models (LLMs) to detect and classify floating-point errors statically in software code. We introduce InterFLOPBench, a benchmark of 90 C kernels with 1 130 test samples designed to evaluate LLMs across six categories of floating-point error: cancellation, comparison, division by zero, overflow, underflow and NaN, compared across 14 LLMs. The evaluation framework treats floating-point error detection as a multi-label classification problem and employs the F1-score metric to measure performance. Results demonstrate that latest models (Qwen 3 32b, Gemini 2.5 Flash, Phi 4 Reasoning, DeepSeek R1T2, and gpt-oss 20b and 120b) achieve a performance greater than 0.88 overall F1-score. Performance varies between error categories, between explicit operations such as division by zero (Average F1-score: 0.8479) and more subtle numerical phenomena such as underflow (Average F1-score: 0.6059) and cancellation (Average F1-score: 0.6164).

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well modern Large Language Models can statically detect and classify different kinds of floating-point errors in C code using a new benchmark, InterFLOPBench.
AI.motivation: Floating-point errors are pervasive in numerical software and can lead to subtle bugs, but traditional program analysis tools can be complex to use and may miss certain classes of numerical issues. With LLMs increasingly used for code understanding and generation, it is important to know whether they can reliably identify and categorize floating-point issues directly from source code and how their performance compares across error types and models.
AI.method: The authors construct InterFLOPBench, a benchmark of 90 C kernels with 1,130 labeled test samples that exhibit six categories of floating-point errors: cancellation, comparison, division by zero, overflow, underflow, and NaN. They cast error detection as a multi-label classification task where LLMs, prompted on the code, must predict which error categories are present. They systematically evaluate 14 different LLMs using F1-score as the primary metric, analyzing overall performance and per-category performance, and comparing newer against older or smaller models.
AI.result: Several recent, larger LLMs (Qwen 3 32B, Gemini 2.5 Flash, Phi 4 Reasoning, DeepSeek R1T2, and gpt-oss 20B/120B) achieve high overall performance with F1-scores above 0.88 on the benchmark. However, accuracy is not uniform across error types: explicit and syntactically obvious issues like division by zero are detected relatively well (average F1 ≈ 0.85), whereas more subtle numerical problems such as underflow and cancellation are significantly harder for the models (average F1 ≈ 0.61 for each).
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that state-of-the-art LLMs already possess strong capabilities in statically detecting many floating-point errors in C code, especially those corresponding to explicit operations. Nonetheless, there remain notable weaknesses for more nuanced numerical phenomena like underflow and cancellation, highlighting that LLM-based analysis is promising but not yet a full replacement for specialized numerical analysis tools; further work is needed to improve sensitivity to subtle floating-point behaviors and to better understand model limitations across error categories.

title: Tone-Conditioned Curriculum Learning for Low-Resource Bantu Speech Recognition
summary: Southern Bantu languages are spoken by over 80 million people, yet current foundation ASR models still produce zero-shot WER above 100%, which limits practical use in education and public services. We addressed this gap with a tone conditioned curriculum framework for 6 Southern Bantu languages that combined hybrid difficulty scoring, gated adapters driven by tonal statistics and staged curriculum training. We trained on a community corpus and tested transfer to NCHLT to measure robustness beyond matched evaluation. Results revealed clear interactions between architecture and language, with W2V-BERT outperforming Whisper on Nguni languages by 3 to 4 WER points whilst Whisper performed better on Sotho-Tswana languages. W2V-BERT with tone conditioning reached 28.41% average WER across datasets and 23.79% on Xitsonga transfer. No single model suited all 6 languages, so deployment should pair model selection per language with validation across corpora.

AI.tldr: They develop and evaluate a tone-aware training framework to improve automatic speech recognition for six Southern Bantu languages, achieving substantially lower word error rates and showing that different architectures suit different language subgroups.
AI.motivation: Southern Bantu languages are spoken by tens of millions of people, but state-of-the-art foundation ASR models perform very poorly in zero-shot settings (WER > 100%), which makes them unusable for real-world applications like education and public services. There is a need for methods that both exploit tonal information and generalize across corpora for multiple related low-resource languages.
AI.method: They propose a tone-conditioned curriculum learning framework for six Southern Bantu languages, combining: (1) a hybrid difficulty scoring scheme to order training examples, (2) gated adapter modules whose behavior is driven by tonal statistics of the languages, and (3) staged curriculum training. They train models on a community-collected corpus and then evaluate cross-corpus robustness by transferring to the NCHLT dataset. They compare two architectures, W2V-BERT and Whisper, and analyze their performance by language subgroup (Nguni vs. Sotho-Tswana) and with/without tone conditioning.
AI.result: The experiments show strong interactions between architecture choice and language family: W2V-BERT outperforms Whisper by 3–4 absolute WER points on Nguni languages, whereas Whisper is better on Sotho-Tswana languages. With tone conditioning, W2V-BERT reaches an average WER of 28.41% across datasets, and achieves 23.79% WER on Xitsonga in cross-corpus transfer from the community dataset to NCHLT. These results indicate substantial improvements over prior zero-shot foundation models and demonstrate successful cross-corpus generalization.
AI.conclusion: Tone-aware curriculum training with gated adapters can significantly improve ASR for Southern Bantu languages, but architecture effectiveness is language-dependent. No single model works best for all six languages, so practical deployment should select and tune architectures per language and validate them across multiple corpora to ensure robustness and generalization.

title: HistoriQA-ThirdRepublic: Multi-Hop Question Answering Corpus for Historical Research, Parliamentary Debates from the French Third Republic (1870-1940)
summary: We present HistoriQA-ThirdRepublic: a French-language dataset of multi-hop historical questions derived from parliamentary debates and newspapers of the French Third Republic. Designed in collaboration with a historian, the corpus captures complex reasoning patterns typical of historical inquiry, including cross-source synthesis, temporal reasoning, and the integration of sparse evidence. The dataset is made of 1782 questions and emphasizes multi-hop connections across heterogeneous historical documents, providing a resource for evaluating retrieval-augmented and large language model systems in domain-specific contexts. We describe the methodology for constructing the corpus, including the selection and alignment of sources, question validation, and metadata integration. While the dataset focuses on French historical documents, our methodology can be readily adapted to other languages and national corpora. Finally, we demonstrate how the corpus can support realistic evaluation scenarios for multi-hop question answering, bridging the gap between NLP benchmarks and the needs of historical scholarship.

AI.tldr: HistoriQA-ThirdRepublic is a French multi-hop historical QA dataset built from Third Republic parliamentary debates and newspapers to evaluate retrieval-augmented and LLM systems on realistic historical reasoning.
AI.motivation: Existing QA benchmarks poorly reflect the complex, multi-source, temporally grounded reasoning practiced in real historical research, especially for non-English corpora. There is a need for domain-specific, historically grounded datasets that better match historians’ workflows and allow rigorous evaluation of NLP systems in this setting.
AI.method: The authors collaborated with a historian to design a corpus of 1782 French-language multi-hop questions based on heterogeneous primary sources from the French Third Republic (parliamentary debates and newspapers). They developed a methodology for selecting and aligning sources, formulating questions requiring cross-document and temporal reasoning, validating those questions, and attaching rich metadata. Emphasis was put on multi-hop connections and sparse evidence integration, enabling realistic retrieval-augmented QA scenarios.
AI.result: The outcome is HistoriQA-ThirdRepublic, a curated dataset of 1782 historically grounded, multi-hop QA instances over aligned French Third Republic sources, with associated metadata and validation. The dataset demonstrates diverse reasoning types (cross-source synthesis, temporal reasoning, sparse evidence use) and is suitable for evaluating retrieval and LLM-based systems in a historically realistic setting.
AI.conclusion: HistoriQA-ThirdRepublic provides a focused resource for testing multi-hop QA in French historical domain, significantly closer to real historians’ needs than generic benchmarks. The construction methodology is generalizable to other languages and national histories, and the corpus enables more realistic evaluation of retrieval-augmented and LLM systems for historical scholarship, helping to bridge the gap between current NLP benchmarks and practical research workflows.

title: Moral Safety in LLMs: Exposing Performative Compliance with Puzzled Cues
summary: As large language models take on morally consequential roles in healthcare, legal, and hiring contexts, we need to examine whether their ethical behaviors are genuine or superficial. We show that current fairness evaluations substantially overestimate moral safety. Models appear fair when demographic identity is stated as an explicit label, yet become measurably less fair when the same identity must be inferred. We term this failure \emph{performative compliance}, where a model is fair when the presentation resembles a fairness evaluation and less fair as that cue weakens. We introduce a cue-variation methodology that holds the moral dilemma and the demographic identity fixed and varies only how that identity is conveyed. Hiding the explicit label raises harmful decisions by $+4.4$~pp and changes model safety rankings, and the shift persists when models correctly infer the demographic, ruling out attribution error. We propose the \textbf{Cue Visibility Gap}, a model-agnostic robustness metric that can be added to any existing fairness benchmark to separate genuine from performative moral safety. Fairness evaluations that omit cue variation measure surface compliance, not moral robustness, and should not ground deployment decisions in high-stakes settings.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that current fairness tests for large language models overestimate how morally safe they are because models act fair only when fairness cues are obvious; the authors propose a new robustness metric that checks whether fairness holds when those cues are obscured.
AI.motivation: Large language models are increasingly used in high‑stakes, morally sensitive domains such as healthcare, law, and hiring. Existing fairness benchmarks usually present demographic information in very explicit ways (e.g., labeled race or gender fields). The authors suspect that this setup might cue models to behave more ethically than they would in naturalistic settings where demographic identity is implicit or must be inferred, leading to an inflated sense of moral safety.
AI.method: They design a cue‑variation methodology in which the underlying moral dilemma and the demographic identity of the person involved are held constant, but the way that demographic identity is conveyed is systematically varied—from explicit labels to more implicit or hidden cues. They then measure how the models’ decisions change as the visibility of the demographic label changes, and they compare fairness metrics and model safety rankings across cue conditions, including cases where the model still correctly infers the demographic identity.
AI.result: When explicit demographic labels are hidden or made less salient, models make more harmful decisions by about +4.4 percentage points, and model safety rankings shift, indicating that fairness performance is not stable across cue conditions. Importantly, this performance drop persists even when the model correctly infers the demographic group, ruling out attribution error as the main cause and supporting the notion of context‑dependent, superficial fairness behavior.
AI.conclusion: The authors define this phenomenon as “performative compliance”: models behave fairly when the input looks like a fairness test but become less fair as those cues weaken. They introduce the Cue Visibility Gap as a model‑agnostic metric that can be layered onto existing fairness benchmarks to distinguish robust moral safety from surface‑level compliance. They argue that fairness evaluations that do not incorporate cue variation risk overstating safety and therefore should not be used alone as the basis for deployment decisions in high‑stakes domains.

title: CryoACE: An Atom-centric Framework for Accurate and Automated Model Building in Cryo-EM
summary: Protein automodeling from cryo-EM density maps faces unique challenges in enforcing physicochemical validity and managing conformational heterogeneity. Current solvers are often limited to static predictions or require computationally intensive heuristic searches. We present CryoACE, an end-to-end framework that reconstructs precise atomic graphs for both homogeneous and heterogeneous structures. Our method features two key innovations: an atom-centric reconstruction paradigm, where density features are sampled directly at atomic coordinates and iteratively recycled to refine structures, replacing expensive voxel convolutions for efficient multimodal fusion; and a training-free guidance mechanism that leverages predicted local resolution priors to resolve dynamic ambiguity. Validated on a newly constructed high-quality dataset, CryoACE significantly outperforms existing baselines on static benchmarks and, for the first time, unveils atomic-level dynamic conformations on complex real-world datasets like EMPIAR-10345 without relying on pre-built static structures.

AI.tldr: CryoACE is an end-to-end framework that builds atom-level protein models directly from cryo-EM density maps, handling both static and heterogeneous structures more efficiently and accurately than existing methods.
AI.motivation: Automodeling proteins from cryo-EM maps must ensure physicochemical validity and deal with conformational heterogeneity, but current methods are often static or depend on costly heuristic searches. There is a need for an approach that can accurately recover atomic structures, including multiple conformations, in a computationally efficient and principled way.
AI.method: CryoACE introduces (1) an atom-centric reconstruction paradigm where features are sampled directly from the density at atomic coordinates and iteratively recycled to refine the model, avoiding heavy voxel-wise convolutions and enabling efficient multimodal feature fusion; and (2) a training-free guidance mechanism that uses predicted local resolution priors from the map to disambiguate dynamic and heterogeneous regions without retraining the model. The framework reconstructs protein structures as atomic graphs for both homogeneous and heterogeneous cases and is trained and evaluated on a curated, high-quality dataset.
AI.result: On static structure benchmarks, CryoACE substantially surpasses existing baseline methods in reconstruction accuracy. On heterogeneous datasets, such as EMPIAR-10345, it is able for the first time to reveal atomic-level dynamic conformations directly from cryo-EM data without depending on pre-existing static structural models.
AI.conclusion: CryoACE provides an efficient, accurate, and general end-to-end solution for atomic-level protein modeling from cryo-EM density maps, overcoming limitations of previous static or heuristic approaches and enabling direct recovery of dynamic conformational ensembles from experimental data.

title: Overview of the TalentCLEF 2026: Skill and Job Title Intelligence for Human Capital Management
summary: This paper presents an overview of the second edition of the TalentCLEF challenge, organized as a Lab at the Conference and Labs of the Evaluation Forum (CLEF) 2026. TalentCLEF is an initiative aimed at advancing Natural Language Processing research in Human Capital Management. The second edition of the challenge consisted of two tasks: Task A, contextualized job-person matching, focuses on identifying and ranking the most suitable candidates represented by their resumes for a given job vacancy in English and Spanish. Task B, job-skill matching with skill type classification, addresses retrieving the most relevant skills for a given job title in English and distinguishing between core and contextual skills. TalentCLEF attracted 113 registered teams and received more than 400 submissions in the two tasks, reflecting the growing interest of the research community in shared evaluation benchmarks for Human Capital Management. This paper describes the motivation and organization of the challenge, summarizes the datasets and evaluation settings, and reports the main results obtained by the participating teams.

AI.tldr: Overview of the second TalentCLEF 2026 shared task on NLP for Human Capital Management, describing tasks, datasets, evaluation setups, and participating systems’ main results.
AI.motivation: Advance NLP research applied to Human Capital Management by providing shared benchmarks and standardized evaluation for job-person and job-skill matching. There is growing industrial and academic interest in automating recruitment and skill extraction, but a lack of open, multilingual, and comparable benchmarks limits progress and comparison of methods.
AI.method: Organize a shared task (TalentCLEF 2026) with two subtasks: (A) contextualized job-person matching, ranking candidate resumes for a job vacancy in English and Spanish; (B) job-skill matching, retrieving relevant skills for a job title in English and classifying them as core or contextual. Provide datasets, clear evaluation metrics and protocols, collect submissions from participants, and analyze their performance and approaches.
AI.result: TalentCLEF 2026 attracted 113 registered teams and over 400 submissions across the two tasks, indicating strong community engagement. The paper summarizes dataset properties, experimental setups, and the best-performing systems’ results for both tasks, comparing alternative approaches to job-person and job-skill matching.
AI.conclusion: The second edition of TalentCLEF successfully strengthened the benchmark landscape for NLP in Human Capital Management, confirming substantial research interest in contextualized job matching and skill modeling. The shared tasks, datasets, and reported results provide a foundation for future work and iterative improvements in methods for recruitment and skill extraction, and motivate continued editions of the challenge.

title: Optimization Algorithms for Joint OFDM Waveform Design and RIS Configuration in 6G Networks: From Convex Relaxation to Foundation Models
summary: Joint OFDM-RIS optimization for 6G is a mixed-integer nonlinear programming (MINLP) problem covering sum-rate maximization, energy efficiency, max-min fairness, and peak-to-average power ratio (PAPR)-constrained objectives. Seventy-eight joint OFDM-RIS optimization works published between 2021 and 2026 are surveyed. No standardized benchmark exists, and cross-paper comparisons remain infeasible. This survey classifies these works into four paradigms: (I) model-based convex relaxation, (II) heuristic and metaheuristic search, (III) deep reinforcement and unsupervised learning, and (IV) emerging methods including foundation models (FM), diffusion-based generative AI, and quantum optimization. A literature synthesis of self-reported benchmarks shows that ML-based methods (Paradigm~III) report 95-99\% of model-based spectral efficiency at 10^2-10^4 x faster per-inference runtime (method-pair dependent; literature values are self-reported and exclude ML pre-training cost). A companion tutorial benchmark at N=16, N=64, and N=128 reveals a critical scaling property: GPU-based neural network inference (DDQN, PPO, graph neural network (GNN), unsupervised DL) is N-invariant, with identical runtime at N=16 and N=128, while iterative solvers (AO+SCA, PSO) scale polynomially. Energy efficiency (P2) and PAPR-constrained (P4) benchmarks are deferred to future work with standardized power models and waveform generators. Six open challenges emerge from the synthesis: the cross-paradigm benchmark deficit, real-world hardware-constrained deployment, joint waveform-RIS optimization for doubly-dispersive channels, multi-objective PAPR trade-offs, LLM safety in live network control, and diminishing returns of standalone heuristics. We specify requirements for a standardized benchmark. This study serves as a roadmap for researchers and practitioners working on joint OFDM-RIS optimization in 6G networks.

AI.tldr: Survey of 78 joint OFDM–RIS optimization works for 6G, classifying methods into four paradigms, comparing their reported performance/runtime, revealing scaling properties via a tutorial benchmark, and outlining open challenges and benchmark requirements.
AI.motivation: Joint optimization of OFDM waveforms and RIS configurations in 6G is formulated as a challenging MINLP spanning multiple objectives (sum-rate, energy efficiency, fairness, PAPR). The rapid growth of disparate solution methods and lack of standardized benchmarks make it hard to compare approaches or guide future research, motivating a structured survey and preliminary benchmark study.
AI.method: Systematic literature survey of 78 papers (2021–2026) on joint OFDM–RIS optimization; classification of methods into four paradigms: (I) model-based convex relaxation, (II) heuristic/metaheuristic search, (III) deep RL and unsupervised learning, and (IV) emerging methods (foundation models, diffusion-based generative AI, quantum optimization). Synthesis of self-reported benchmarks across papers, plus a companion tutorial benchmark evaluating scaling for selected model-based (AO+SCA), heuristic (PSO), and ML (DDQN, PPO, GNN, unsupervised DL) methods at RIS sizes N=16, 64, 128.
AI.result: From literature synthesis, ML-based methods (Paradigm III) typically achieve 95–99% of the spectral efficiency of model-based methods while being 10^2–10^4 times faster per inference (ignoring training cost). The tutorial benchmark shows that GPU-based neural inference is effectively N-invariant in runtime between N=16 and N=128, whereas iterative solvers like AO+SCA and PSO scale polynomially with N. Benchmarks for energy efficiency and PAPR-constrained objectives are identified as incomplete and left for future standardized evaluations.
AI.conclusion: The field lacks standardized, cross-paradigm benchmarks, making objective comparison of joint OFDM–RIS optimization methods difficult. ML-based approaches appear highly promising for fast inference with near-optimal performance, particularly as system dimensions grow, but comprehensive evaluation across objectives and realistic hardware constraints is missing. The paper distills six key open challenges—spanning benchmarking, hardware-aware deployment, joint waveform-RIS design, multi-objective PAPR trade-offs, safe use of large models in control, and the limited future value of standalone heuristics—and specifies requirements for a community-accepted benchmark, positioning the work as a roadmap for 6G OFDM–RIS research.

title: Cross-lingual Relation Extraction with Large Language Models: Zero-Shot, Few-Shot, and Fine-Tuned Evaluation on Romanian
summary: Relation extraction (RE) for low-resource languages is typically constrained by the lack of annotated corpora. We investigate the feasibility of cross-lingual RE for Romanian by combining automatic dataset translation with large language model (LLM) inference. We translate the SemEval-2010 Task 8 benchmark from English to Romanian using an LLM-based translation pipeline and evaluate Gemma 4 31B under zero-shot, few-shot, and QLoRA fine-tuned configurations, against four encoder baselines spanning 125M to 560M parameters: XLM- RoBERTa (base and large), Romanian BERT, and RoBERT- large. We assess two task formulations: relation classification with marked entities and end-to-end extraction.
