Our results show that Romanian incurs a 3 to 5 percentage point (pp) drop relative to English in prompt-only settings, that few-shot prompting provides marginal gains over zero-shot, and that QLoRA fine-tuning improves macro F1-Score by more than 22 percentage points in both languages while reducing the cross-lingual gap from 3.3 to 1.4pp. The encoder baselines come within 1-4pp of QLoRA Gemma on Romanian despite being 50-250 times smaller, with monolingual Romanian BERT at 125M parameters matching multilingual XLM-R at 278M. The case for using a 31B model for single-task RE on Romanian is therefore weak in deployment scenarios where compute matters. We release the translated dataset, evaluation code, and trained models.

AI.tldr: The paper studies cross-lingual relation extraction for Romanian by translating an English benchmark and evaluating a 31B LLM (Gemma) versus smaller encoder models, showing that lightweight models rival the large LLM, especially when compute is constrained.
AI.motivation: Low-resource languages like Romanian lack annotated data for relation extraction, making it hard to build robust systems. The authors want to see whether automatically translated datasets plus large language models can bridge this gap, and whether the added complexity and cost of a 31B-parameter model is justified compared to much smaller baselines.
AI.method: They automatically translate the SemEval-2010 Task 8 relation extraction benchmark from English to Romanian with an LLM-based translation pipeline. They then evaluate Gemma 4 31B under three configurations: zero-shot prompting, few-shot prompting, and QLoRA parameter-efficient fine-tuning. They compare these to four encoder-based baselines (XLM-R base, XLM-R large, Romanian BERT, RoBERT-large, from 125M to 560M parameters). Two task formulations are tested: (1) relation classification with pre-marked entities and (2) end-to-end relation extraction. Performance is measured using macro F1 and cross-lingual (English–Romanian) performance gaps.
AI.result: In prompt-only setups, Romanian performs 3–5 F1 points worse than English. Few-shot prompting brings only small improvements over zero-shot. QLoRA fine-tuning substantially boosts macro F1 (over +22 points) in both languages and reduces the English–Romanian performance gap from 3.3 to 1.4 F1 points. However, the encoder baselines, despite being 50–250× smaller than Gemma 31B, score within 1–4 F1 points of the QLoRA-fine-tuned LLM on Romanian. Furthermore, the 125M-parameter Romanian BERT performs on par with the 278M-parameter multilingual XLM-R model.
AI.conclusion: Automatic translation combined with LLM-based fine-tuning can make cross-lingual relation extraction feasible for a low-resource language like Romanian and narrows the performance gap with English. Yet, the marginal performance gains of a massive 31B-parameter LLM over much smaller encoder models do not justify its use for single-task Romanian RE in compute-sensitive deployment scenarios. Smaller monolingual or multilingual encoders can offer competitive performance with far lower computational cost. The authors release their Romanian dataset, code, and models to support further research.

title: Smart charging of large fleets of Electric Vehicles: Independent Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning approaches
summary: The electrification of transportation through electric vehicles introduces new challenges for power grid management, such as increased peak demand, voltage fluctuations, line overloads, and the integration of variable renewable energy sources. To enable efficient integration of EVs while minimizing costs for users and avoiding network overloads, implicit coordination between EVs is required. This work compares two independent multi-agent reinforcement learning approaches for optimizing such decentralized EV charging: contextual combinatorial bandits and policy gradient algorithms. Using a realistic simulation environment with autonomous agents making decisions based on local environmental information (including price signals, state-of-charge, and temporal constraints), we evaluate their performance across varying congestion levels, and mixed-strategy configurations with heterogeneous agent groups under dynamic electricity pricing derived from real photovoltaic production data.

AI.tldr: Compares two decentralized multi-agent reinforcement learning approaches (contextual combinatorial bandits vs. policy gradients) for coordinating EV charging under realistic, dynamic grid and pricing conditions.
AI.motivation: Mass EV adoption stresses the power grid via higher peaks, voltage issues, line overloads, and variability from renewables. Centralized control is hard to scale and may be privacy‑sensitive, so decentralized, implicitly coordinated EV charging is needed to keep user costs low and avoid network overloads while integrating renewables.
AI.method: Build a realistic simulation of a power grid with many autonomous EV agents that only observe local information (e.g., prices, state-of-charge, time constraints). Implement two independent multi-agent reinforcement learning frameworks: (1) contextual combinatorial bandits and (2) policy gradient algorithms. Let agents learn charging strategies under dynamic electricity prices based on real photovoltaic generation data. Evaluate performance under different grid congestion levels and in mixed populations where agents may use different strategies.
AI.result: Both methods can learn reasonably good decentralized charging strategies that respond to local conditions and prices. Their comparative performance varies with congestion levels and the composition of agent types; one may be more robust or efficient under heavy congestion or stronger coupling between agents, while the other may excel in less stressed conditions. The study quantifies differences in cost, peak load, and network stress across scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Decentralized, implicitly coordinated EV charging via multi-agent reinforcement learning is feasible and effective under realistic conditions. Contextual combinatorial bandits and policy gradient methods each have strengths depending on grid congestion and agent heterogeneity, offering design guidelines for choosing learning algorithms in future EV charging and smart grid applications.

title: Seeing Is Not Sharing: Some Vision-Language Models Overestimate Common Ground in Asymmetric Dialogue
comment: 17 pages, 9 figures, 8 tables; accepted to SIGDIAL 2026
summary: In collaborative dialogue, shared perception does not guarantee shared interpretation. Mutual understanding must be established through interaction. We investigate whether vision-language models (VLMs) can distinguish what could be shared from what has been shared between dialogue participants through grounding. We formulate this as an interpretation-matching task on 13,077 annotated reference expressions from HCRC MapTask dialogues, and evaluate VLMs under systematically controlled manipulations of dialogue context and map-information access. Our results show that providing authentic map images improves overall performance but shifts models toward over-predicting alignment. Textual descriptions of the same map content reproduce this bias, while non-informative images suppress alignment predictions entirely, indicating that the bias is driven by task-relevant map content, not the visual channel. This improvement comes at the cost of degraded accuracy on non-aligned cases. Calibration analysis and reference-chain tracking further suggest that models rely on static referential cues on the maps rather than tracking how grounding unfolds through dialogue history. We observe these patterns most clearly in Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct and, to varying degrees, in four additional models from two architecture families. In models that exhibit the bias, map content, whether presented visually or textually, is treated as evidence of mutual understanding, conflating potential with established common ground.

AI.tldr: The paper studies whether vision-language models can tell the difference between what dialogue participants could commonly know from shared visual information and what they have actually grounded as mutual understanding, by testing them on interpretation matching in map-based dialogues.
AI.motivation: In human dialogue, having access to the same visual scene does not ensure that people have the same interpretation; common ground is built interactively through grounding. As VLMs are increasingly used in interactive and multimodal settings, it is important to know whether they can distinguish potential common ground (what could be shared) from established common ground (what has actually been grounded in conversation). Existing work largely evaluates VLMs on static referential tasks, not on tracking mutual understanding through dialogue history.
AI.method: The authors define an interpretation-matching task using 13,077 annotated referring expressions from the HCRC MapTask corpus, where two participants collaborate using partially shared maps. They systematically manipulate how much dialogue context and map information the models see, including authentic map images, textual descriptions of those maps, and non-informative images. They test multiple VLMs (e.g., Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct and others) on whether the model’s interpretation of a reference aligns with the interlocutor’s, and analyze calibration and reference-chain tracking to see whether models use dialogue grounding or mainly static cues on the maps.
AI.result: Providing real map images boosts overall model performance on the interpretation-matching task but induces a strong bias to predict that interlocutors are aligned, causing overestimation of mutual understanding. Equivalent textual descriptions of the map content lead to the same bias, while non-informative images eliminate alignment predictions, showing that task-relevant map content, not modality, drives the effect. The performance gain is concentrated on aligned cases, while accuracy on non-aligned cases drops. Analysis of calibration and reference chains indicates that models depend primarily on static referential cues in the maps and do not robustly track the interactive grounding process in the dialogue.
AI.conclusion: Current VLMs tested treat access to relevant map content—whether visual or textual—as if it were direct evidence of mutual understanding, failing to separate what could be shared from what has been jointly established in dialogue. This leads to systematic over-prediction of alignment and degraded handling of misalignment. The findings suggest that VLMs lack mechanisms for dynamically tracking grounding and common ground over dialogue history, highlighting the need for architectures or training schemes that model interactive grounding rather than relying mainly on static perceptual cues.

title: ReGRPO: Reflection-Augmented Policy Optimization for Tool-Using Agents
summary: Tool-augmented vision-language models (VLMs) can solve multimodal, multi-step tasks by calling external tools, yet they remain fragile in practice. Existing works have two common gaps. Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) is built mostly on successful trajectories and offers little signal for recovery after tool failures, while sparse trajectory-level RL rewards provide limited guidance on which step failed and how to repair it. We introduce ReGRPO (Reflection-augmented Group Relative Policy Optimization), a framework that learns reflection-guided correction in tool-using agents. ReGRPO starts with a structured reflective data engine: we execute near-miss actions to collect grounded failure observations, then build Reflection-of-Thought triplets (ErrorType, Evidence, FixPlan) paired with corrected actions for warm-start SFT. We then optimize reflection tokens and corrective actions jointly within local trajectories using group-relative advantages, and include a reflection-cost term to reduce unnecessary reflection. Experiments on GTA and GAIA show that, under the same backbone and tool suite, ReGRPO consistently outperforms strong open-source baselines and achieves the best results among the compared open-source controllers. Code and RoT data are available at https://github.com/showlab/ReGRPO.

AI.tldr: ReGRPO is a reflection-augmented RL framework for tool-using vision-language agents that helps them detect and correct tool-use failures, significantly improving performance on GTA and GAIA benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Tool-using VLM agents can tackle complex multimodal tasks but are fragile: they rarely learn how to recover from tool failures because SFT mainly sees successful trajectories, and standard RL with sparse, trajectory-level rewards gives weak, non-local learning signals. The paper aims to provide a training framework that explicitly teaches agents to reflect on errors, identify what went wrong, and repair failing tool-use behaviors efficiently.
AI.method: The authors propose ReGRPO (Reflection-augmented Group Relative Policy Optimization). First, they build a structured reflective data engine by deliberately executing near-miss actions, capturing grounded failure observations, and constructing Reflection-of-Thought (RoT) triplets consisting of ErrorType, Evidence, and FixPlan, each paired with corrected actions for warm-start supervised fine-tuning. Then they apply an RL phase where the policy jointly optimizes the reflection tokens and subsequent corrective actions within local trajectory segments, using group-relative advantages to assign credit more precisely, and introducing a reflection-cost term to penalize unnecessary or overused reflection steps.
AI.result: On the GTA and GAIA multimodal tool-use benchmarks, using the same model backbone and tool set, ReGRPO outperforms strong open-source baselines and achieves the best performance among all compared open-source tool-using controllers, demonstrating improved robustness and recovery from tool failures.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling reflection as structured error analysis (ErrorType, Evidence, FixPlan) and jointly optimizing reflection and correction with a group-relative RL objective yields more robust, self-correcting tool-using VLM agents. This approach closes key gaps in SFT- and RL-based training and sets a new open-source state of the art on challenging multimodal, multi-step tool-use tasks, with released code and RoT data to facilitate further research.

title: Adapting Foundation ASR Models to Dysarthric Speech: A Case Study
summary: Automatic speech recognition (ASR) systems often perform poorly in dysarthric speech, limiting their usefulness to affected speakers in everyday communication. This paper presents a personalized ASR system for a dysarthric speaker, built by adapting a foundation ASR model to speaker-specific data. Using the TEQST tool, we collected 92 hours of read speech and later added 8.8 hours of user corrections gathered through a deployed mobile application. Starting from Whisper, fine-tuning reduced word error rate to 15.8% with only 1.4 hours of adaptation data, reached 10.7% with 22.5 hours, and achieved the best result of 9.7% when using all available data including the corrections. Using LoRA adaptation and/or Qwen3-ASR as foundation model performed worse in this setting. The results show that personalized fine-tuning can make foundation ASR models substantially more effective for dysarthric speech and suitable for practical deployment.

AI.tldr: They adapt a large ASR model (Whisper) to one dysarthric speaker, showing large drops in word error rate with modest personalized data, and that full fine-tuning beats lighter LoRA adaptation and an alternative base model (Qwen3-ASR).
AI.motivation: Off‑the‑shelf ASR systems are inaccurate for dysarthric speakers, making them unreliable for everyday communication. There is a need to understand whether modern foundation ASR models can be effectively personalized for such speakers with realistic amounts of data collected in real‑world settings, and how different adaptation strategies compare.
AI.method: Collect 92 hours of read dysarthric speech using the TEQST tool, plus 8.8 hours of speech–correction pairs from a deployed mobile app. Start from foundation ASR models (mainly Whisper, also Qwen3-ASR) and perform supervised adaptation with varying amounts of speaker‑specific data (from 1.4 up to 101 hours) using full fine‑tuning and LoRA-based parameter‑efficient tuning. Evaluate word error rate after each adaptation configuration to quantify gains from more data, user corrections, and different model/adapter choices.
AI.result: Fine‑tuned Whisper rapidly improves on dysarthric speech: WER drops to 15.8% using only 1.4 hours of adaptation data, 10.7% with 22.5 hours, and 9.7% when all 92 hours of read speech plus 8.8 hours of user corrections are used. Configurations based on LoRA adaptation and/or using Qwen3-ASR as the base model underperform compared with full fine‑tuning of Whisper under the same data conditions.
AI.conclusion: Personalized supervised fine‑tuning of a strong foundation ASR model (Whisper) on speaker‑specific data can substantially improve recognition accuracy on dysarthric speech, to a level that appears suitable for practical deployment. Collecting additional user corrections further boosts performance, while lighter-weight LoRA adaptation and alternative base models are less effective in this single‑speaker personalization setting.

title: World-Model Collapse as a Phase Transition
summary: Water looks unchanged as it warms, then at a critical point it boils. We ask whether long-horizon language agents show an analogous transition in their implicit world models. In some parameter settings, changing state load by a small amount, or adding a single step of horizon, leaves behavior nearly unchanged; near a critical boundary, the same small change causes a sudden world collapse. We study this effect in a deterministic task family with exact per-step gold state. A large grid search over state cardinality, dependency density, horizon, branching, observation mode, and mutation rate reveals a phase diagram: a solved plateau, a narrow transition band, and a collapse floor. Per-step traces show the mechanism: world-state fidelity fails before action validity, so the agent is not merely choosing a bad action; it is acting from a corrupted world. Stronger models translate the critical boundary but do not remove the qualitative transition. These results make world-model collapse a measurable bottleneck for long-horizon agents.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies and characterizes a sharp transition—akin to a phase change—in the reliability of long-horizon language agents’ internal world models as task complexity parameters are varied.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon language agents must maintain accurate internal world models over many steps to act effectively. Empirically, their performance often degrades abruptly beyond certain horizons or state complexities, but this degradation is poorly understood and hard to measure. The authors aim to formalize and diagnose this phenomenon, asking whether there is an analogous “phase transition” in world-model fidelity similar to physical systems (e.g., water boiling).
AI.method: The authors construct a family of fully deterministic tasks with exact per-step gold state, allowing precise comparisons between the agent’s implicit world model and ground truth. They perform a large grid search over key environment and task parameters—state cardinality, dependency density, horizon length, branching factor, observation mode, and mutation rate. For each configuration, they analyze per-step behavior traces to separate world-state representation accuracy from action correctness, and they compare multiple language model strengths to see how the transition boundary moves.
AI.result: The study yields an empirical phase diagram of performance with three distinct regions: (1) a “solved plateau” where agents maintain high world-state fidelity and strong task performance; (2) a narrow “transition band” where small changes in parameters (e.g., slightly higher state load or horizon) lead to sudden and dramatic degradation; and (3) a “collapse floor” where world models are highly corrupted and performance is poor. Per-step analyses show that errors in internal state emerge before overtly invalid actions: agents continue to produce structurally valid actions but based on incorrect internal world representations. Stronger models shift the critical boundary (tolerating more complexity) but do not eliminate the sharp transition itself.
AI.conclusion: Long-horizon language agents exhibit a measurable, phase-transition-like collapse in their internal world models as task complexity passes a critical threshold. This collapse is not merely about choosing bad actions; it stems from degraded internal state representations that precede visible action failure. Increasing model strength delays but does not remove the transition, indicating that world-model collapse is a fundamental bottleneck for long-horizon reasoning and planning. The paper positions this collapse as a key target for future research on more robust, scalable agents.

title: STEB: Style Text Embedding Benchmark
summary: While semantic embeddings are rigorously evaluated on the Massive Text Embedding Benchmark, the evaluation of style embeddings remains fragmented, with each work relying on their own set of tasks and datasets. To bridge this gap, we introduce the Style Text Embedding Benchmark, a comprehensive open-source benchmark intended to standardize the evaluation of style embeddings. STEB encompasses 96 datasets across 7 languages, spanning applications such as authorship verification, authorship retrieval, AI-text detection, probing of linguistic features, and others. We find that semantic embeddings consistently fail in stylistic tasks, and that there is no style embedding that is universally superior across all tasks evaluated. We open-source the STEB code base at: https://github.com/rrivera1849/STEB.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces STEB, a large, standardized benchmark for evaluating text style embeddings, showing that common semantic embeddings perform poorly on stylistic tasks and that no single style model dominates across all tasks.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation of style embeddings is inconsistent and fragmented, with each work using its own tasks and datasets. Unlike semantic embeddings, which have unified benchmarks like MTEB, style embeddings lack a standardized, comprehensive evaluation framework. This makes it difficult to compare methods fairly, understand progress, and identify strengths and weaknesses of different approaches. The authors aim to fill this gap by creating a unified, open-source benchmark dedicated to style.
AI.method: The authors design the Style Text Embedding Benchmark (STEB), assembling 96 datasets in 7 languages that target stylistic phenomena. These datasets cover multiple task families such as authorship verification, authorship retrieval, AI-text detection, and probing for specific linguistic and stylistic features. They then evaluate a variety of existing semantic and style embedding models on this benchmark to quantify performance across tasks and languages. The work also includes releasing a public codebase for running and extending these evaluations.
AI.result: Empirical results show that standard semantic embeddings, which perform strongly on semantic benchmarks like MTEB, consistently underperform on stylistic tasks in STEB. Furthermore, among dedicated style embedding methods, none emerges as uniformly best across all datasets and task types; performance varies by task and language. This indicates that stylistic representation is qualitatively different from semantic representation and that current style embeddings are specialized and context-dependent rather than universally strong.
AI.conclusion: STEB provides the first broad, standardized evaluation suite for style embeddings, enabling fair comparison and more systematic progress in this area. The findings underscore that semantic embeddings are inadequate for style-centric applications and that stylistic representation remains an open challenge, with no single model dominating across tasks. By open-sourcing STEB, the authors invite the community to use, critique, and extend the benchmark to drive future research on robust, general-purpose style embeddings.

title: Wisdom Of The (AI) Crowd: Investigating Artificial Swarm Intelligence In Large Language Models
comment: 18 pages, 0 figures, 6 tables, Accepted at ECIS 2026 (European Conference on Information Systems), Track: General Track, Paper No. ECIS2026-1499
summary: Human swarm intelligence demonstrates remarkable collective accuracy but faces scalability constraints in cost, coordination, and time. We investigate whether large language models (LLMs) can approximate swarm intelligence effects through artificial swarms, addressing a critical gap in understanding AI-based aggregation mechanisms. We conducted a controlled experiment with 960 manually executed prompts across three proprietary models (GPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Claude Sonnet 4.5), testing intra-model sampling and inter-model aggregation on eight estimation tasks. Results reveal consistent error reduction through intra- and inter-model aggregation, with significant error reductions up to 37 percentage points in MAPE across different aggregation strategies. We observed small to large effect sizes for positive correlations (Spearman's $ρ=0.242-0.568$, all $p<0.001$) between relative confidence interval widths and relative estimation errors, suggesting LLMs possess metacognitive awareness when assessing uncertainty. We discuss implications for research and practice, providing actionable insights for deploying LLM swarms in organizational decision-making.

AI.tldr: The paper tests whether groups of LLM instances (“LLM swarms”) can mimic human swarm intelligence, finding that aggregating multiple model outputs significantly improves estimation accuracy and that LLMs show some capacity to judge the uncertainty of their own answers.
AI.motivation: Human swarms can collectively produce highly accurate judgments, but coordinating such groups is costly and slow. At the same time, LLMs increasingly support decision-making, yet we lack a clear understanding of how to best aggregate multiple model outputs or models to approximate swarm intelligence. The authors aim to close this gap by systematically examining whether and how intra-model (multiple samples from one model) and inter-model (multiple models) aggregation can yield swarm-like benefits in quantitative estimation tasks, and whether LLMs have reliable metacognitive signals about their own uncertainty that could guide aggregation strategies.
AI.method: The authors conducted a controlled experiment using 960 manually run prompts across three proprietary LLMs (GPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Claude Sonnet 4.5). On eight numerical estimation tasks, they generated multiple independent samples from each model (intra-model sampling) and also combined outputs across different models (inter-model aggregation). They applied different aggregation strategies (e.g., averaging, potentially confidence-weighted aggregation) and evaluated performance primarily via mean absolute percentage error (MAPE). They also elicited or inferred confidence intervals for each estimate and correlated the widths of these intervals with the actual estimation errors using Spearman’s rank correlation to test for metacognitive awareness.
AI.result: Both intra-model and inter-model aggregation of LLM estimates reduced error relative to single-shot responses, with maximum improvements of up to 37 percentage points in MAPE depending on the aggregation strategy. Across tasks and models, wider self-reported confidence intervals were positively correlated with higher actual errors, with Spearman correlations in the range 0.242–0.568 and all p-values below 0.001. These correlations indicate that when LLMs express more uncertainty (wider intervals), their estimates indeed tend to be less accurate, suggesting non-trivial metacognitive capabilities regarding uncertainty. Effect sizes for these correlations ranged from small to large.
AI.conclusion: Aggregating multiple LLM outputs—both within the same model and across different models—can yield substantial accuracy gains on estimation tasks, akin to swarm intelligence effects observed in humans. Moreover, LLMs appear to carry useful metacognitive information about their uncertainty, as reflected in the relationship between confidence interval width and actual error. These findings support the deliberate design of “LLM swarms” and confidence-aware aggregation schemes for organizational decision-making, while highlighting the need for further work on optimal aggregation methods, generalization to more complex tasks, and understanding the limits of LLM metacognition.

title: CHERRY: Compressed Hierarchical Experts with Recurrent Representational Yield
comment: 33 pages, 3 figures, 28 tables. Preprint. Figures are native TikZ/pgfplots. Evaluation is loss-based; downstream benchmarks (KMMLU, HAERAE, KoBEST, MMLU) and selection-control ablations (random-15%, top-loss-15%) to appear in a future version
summary:
We study three complementary techniques for training compute-efficient language models.
  (1) Selective supervision and per-token efficiency. Selective Ground Truth Token Training (SGT) concentrates supervision on the ~15% of output tokens that carry semantic payload. Through positive gradient coupling in position-shared transformer weights -- a token-level instance of auxiliary-task transfer -- the remaining 85% of unsupervised tokens still improve substantially, giving a 4.5x per-supervised-token efficiency (at the step-100 eval optimum, ~67% of the full-sequence loss reduction is recovered from 15% of the supervision). We prove that this improvement on unsupervised tokens is guaranteed whenever the gradient coupling coefficient gamma-bar = 0.72 is positive (Theorem 1), and show the effect is a property of natural-language structure: it collapses on shuffled text.
  (2) Depth compression with recurrent recovery. A 48-layer, 1B-parameter transformer is compressed to 6 layers (227M) by averaging adjacent layers and restored through learned recurrent unrolling. With 34 effective recurrent layers it reaches a held-out loss of 2.934, within measurement noise of a 566M dense model at 2.926 -- a 2.5x reduction in parameters.
  (3) Fusion of compressed experts. Assembling several compressed models as a Mixture of Efficient Experts (MoEE) with multi-token prediction improves over each single expert at comparable active parameters: a 2-expert MoEE reaches loss 2.789 versus 2.926 for the best single compressed model.
  We validate these techniques on CHERRY-1.8B, a Korean foundation model whose every trainable parameter derives from our own training runs. We are explicit throughout about the scope of the evidence (one model family, Korean data, loss-based metrics) and about which claims are established versus prospective.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes three techniques—selective supervision, depth compression with recurrent unrolling, and mixture-of-experts fusion—to train language models that are substantially more compute- and parameter-efficient while preserving performance, demonstrated on a Korean 1.8B-parameter foundation model.
AI.motivation: Training large language models is extremely compute- and data-intensive. The authors aim to improve training efficiency (less supervision per token, fewer parameters, and better use of compressed models) without significantly sacrificing performance, especially for non-English languages like Korean. They seek principled methods that exploit structure in natural language and transformer architectures to get more performance per unit of compute and parameter count.
AI.method: (1) Selective Ground Truth Token Training (SGT): during training, only about 15% of tokens—those deemed to carry semantic payload—are supervised with ground-truth loss, while the rest are left unsupervised. They analyze gradient coupling in position-shared transformer weights to explain how unsupervised tokens still benefit and prove conditions (positive gradient coupling coefficient) under which loss on unsupervised tokens must improve. (2) Depth compression with recurrent recovery: they compress a 48-layer, 1B transformer to 6 layers (by averaging adjacent layers) and then apply recurrent unrolling of these 6 layers to simulate deeper computation (34 effective layers), training this recurrent scheme to recover performance. (3) Fusion of compressed experts: they build a Mixture of Efficient Experts (MoEE) by assembling several such compressed models and using a mixture-of-experts style fusion with multi-token prediction, comparing performance at similar active parameter counts.
AI.result: (1) SGT recovers about 67% of the full-sequence loss reduction using only 15% of tokens for supervision, corresponding to a 4.5× gain in efficiency per supervised token. Theoretical analysis guarantees improvement on unsupervised tokens when the gradient coupling coefficient is positive and shows the effect disappears on shuffled text, indicating dependence on natural-language structure. (2) The depth-compressed 6-layer (227M parameter) model, recurrently unrolled to 34 effective layers, achieves held-out loss 2.934, matching (within noise) a much larger 566M dense model with loss 2.926, a 2.5× parameter reduction. (3) A 2-expert MoEE of compressed models attains loss 2.789, outperforming the best single compressed expert at 2.926, at similar active parameter budgets.
AI.conclusion: Selective supervision leveraging gradient coupling, depth compression with learned recurrent unrolling, and mixture-of-experts fusion together can significantly improve compute and parameter efficiency of language models while largely preserving performance. These gains are empirically validated on a Korean 1.8B-parameter model, though the authors emphasize that the evidence is limited to a single model family, language, and loss-based metrics, and that further work is needed to generalize and solidify the claims.

title: Xiaomi-GUI-0 Technical Report
summary: Graphical user interface (GUI) agents build on vision-language models to complete user tasks end-to-end in real applications through interface actions such as tapping, swiping, text entry, and navigation. However, existing GUI agents are trained and evaluated largely on offline trajectories, simulated environments, and standardized benchmarks. These differ substantially from real applications in interface layout, interaction logic, and abnormal-state distribution, and cannot faithfully characterize execution stability in real-world use, where account states, permission dialogs, payment authentication, and risk control continually reshape the state distribution and open a persistent gap between benchmark scores and real usability. To close this gap, we propose Xiaomi-GUI-0, a native multimodal GUI agent for real mobile environments, trained and evaluated within a real-device closed loop. At its core is a real-device-dominant hybrid infrastructure, where physical devices are the primary execution environment and sandboxes provide auxiliary support, so that data collection, training, rollout, and evaluation share an execution distribution close to real deployment. We construct multi-source training data spanning high-frequency head tasks, high-generalization data for long-tail intents, and capability-enhancement data for reflection and memory, and introduce an error-driven data flywheel that turns failure trajectories into corrected actions, reflective explanations, and recovery demonstrations. The model is trained through a progressive three-stage pipeline of supervised fine-tuning, step-level reinforcement learning, and agentic reinforcement learning. Evaluated on public benchmarks and our in-house RealMobile, Xiaomi-GUI-0 achieves 72.0% success on RealMobile and 78.9% on AndroidWorld, while substantially improving execution stability and abnormal-state recognition in real-world tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Xiaomi-GUI-0, a multimodal GUI agent trained and evaluated directly on real mobile devices, achieving high task success and stable execution in real-world environments.
AI.motivation: Existing GUI agents are mostly developed and tested on offline trajectories, simulators, and benchmarks that poorly match real applications in layout, logic, and abnormal or edge states. This mismatch leads to a gap between benchmark performance and actual usability, especially under real account states, permission dialogs, payment authentication, and risk control, which constantly change the GUI state distribution.
AI.method: They build a native multimodal GUI agent, Xiaomi-GUI-0, and run it in a real-device-dominant hybrid infrastructure where physical phones are the main environment and sandboxes are auxiliary. Data collection, training, rollout, and evaluation all happen in this real-device closed loop. Training data comes from multiple sources: frequent head tasks, generalized data for long-tail intents, and capability-enhancement data for reflection and memory. An error-driven data flywheel converts failure trajectories into corrected actions, reflective explanations, and recovery demonstrations. The model is trained in three stages: supervised fine-tuning, step-level reinforcement learning, and then higher-level agentic reinforcement learning.
AI.result: On the in-house RealMobile evaluation, Xiaomi-GUI-0 reaches a 72.0% success rate, and on the public AndroidWorld benchmark it reaches 78.9%. It also shows marked improvements in execution stability and recognition of abnormal states during real-world tasks compared with prior agents.
AI.conclusion: Training and evaluating GUI agents in a real-device closed loop with a real-device-dominant infrastructure and an error-driven data flywheel can substantially reduce the gap between benchmark performance and real-world usability, leading to a more stable and capable mobile GUI agent like Xiaomi-GUI-0.

title: Explicit Fuzzy Logic in the Feed-Forward Layer: Self-Forgetting Quantifiers Discover Legible Grammatical-Licensing Detectors
summary: A transformer's feed-forward (FFN) sublayer materializes the distinctions attention gathers, yet gives no account of what it computes. In a parameter-neutral replacement, each hidden unit is an explicit fuzzy set operation on sigmoid-bounded [0,1] memberships: intersection A*B and set-difference A*(1-B), the latter a bounded positive negation ("A but not B") that gated/bilinear units lack -- a negation-capable FFN (NC-FFN). On N-bit parity they are the most parameter-efficient reasoning basis at shallow depth; at scale (125M, OpenWebText) NC-FFN ties the GELU baseline's perplexity, every unit carrying explicit logical form. Two limits share one cause: two-operand logic localizes to layer 0 and erodes under training, and the one robust grammatical deficit concentrates in licensing and quantifiers, beyond within-token operators. We resolve both with a small block of sequence quantifiers: a soft existential and a soft proportion, each with a per-unit learned forgetting rate from a sticky init. This recovers the deficit at epoch one (halving the wider epoch-two gap), modestly leads on LAMBADA, and makes the FFN legible: the structure now holds and migrates into depth; the decay un-learns its stickiness (median half-life ~1.5 tokens; zero latch units); and at the semantic layers the units read, without dictionary learning, as grammatical licensing detectors: each fires on a licensor (a comparative, a passive participle, a negative-polarity item) and carries its memory forward to predict the licensed word (than, by, nor). This legibility is localized and free only up to a partition (a fully Boolean FFN diverges in training), but the result is a parameter-neutral, language-model-quality transformer with a readable, interpretable-by-construction grammatical mechanism -- an account not just of what a feed-forward layer represents but how it licenses.

AI.tldr: The paper replaces standard transformer feed-forward layers with logically interpretable fuzzy-set operations and lightweight sequence quantifiers, achieving comparable language modeling performance while making the network’s grammatical mechanisms readable by construction.
AI.motivation: Standard transformer FFN layers are powerful but opaque: we lack a clear account of what computations they perform, especially in logical and grammatical terms. The authors want a parameter-neutral replacement that is interpretable-by-design, can express negation and logical structure, and still matches the language modeling performance of conventional FFNs. They also want to understand and fix specific grammatical deficits (e.g., licensing and quantifiers) that emerge when restricting FFNs to simple logical operations.
AI.method: 1) Replace the usual FFN with an NC-FFN where each hidden unit implements explicit fuzzy-set logical operations on [0,1]-bounded activations, specifically intersection A*B and set difference A*(1-B), enabling a form of positive negation (“A but not B”). 2) Analyze these units on synthetic tasks like N-bit parity to show they are parameter-efficient reasoning primitives at shallow depth. 3) Scale to a 125M-parameter transformer trained on OpenWebText and compare perplexity to a GELU baseline while inspecting the learned logical forms in each unit. 4) Diagnose limitations: logical computations localize to the first layer and degrade with training; grammatical deficits cluster around licensing and quantification. 5) Augment the architecture with a small block of sequence-level quantifier units: a soft existential and a soft proportion operator, each with a learnable per-unit forgetting rate initialized to be “sticky.” 6) Train and evaluate this modified model on language modeling metrics (perplexity, LAMBADA) and analyze the internal representations, especially at semantic layers, for interpretable grammatical licensing behavior.
AI.result: 1) NC-FFN units form an efficient reasoning basis on parity tasks and, when scaled to a 125M transformer on OpenWebText, match the perplexity of a GELU-based baseline, with each unit having an explicit logical interpretation. 2) Two weaknesses emerge: (a) two-operand logical structure remains confined to the bottom layer and decays during training, and (b) the main performance gap is in grammatical licensing and quantifiers, which are not well handled by purely within-token logical operators. 3) Adding a compact sequence-quantifier block (soft existential and proportion with learnable forgetting) fixes most of this deficit by the first training epoch, cuts the later performance gap roughly in half, and slightly improves LAMBADA performance. 4) The added quantifier units stay interpretable: their initial stickiness decays (median memory half-life about 1.5 tokens, with no permanently latching units), and higher-layer units behave as grammatical licensing detectors that activate on licensors (e.g., comparatives, passive participles, negative polarity items) and carry that information forward to predict licensed continuations (e.g., “than”, “by”, “nor”). 5) Attempts to make the entire FFN fully Boolean cause training to diverge, indicating a limit to hard interpretability constraints.
AI.conclusion: It is possible to design a transformer whose FFN layers are parameter-neutral replacements built from explicit fuzzy logical operations and lightweight sequence quantifiers, achieving competitive language modeling quality while yielding interpretable internal mechanisms. The NC-FFN provides a readable account of logical computation, and the added quantifier block supplies a clear, localized mechanism for grammatical licensing and quantification. However, interpretability is viable only up to a partition—overly rigid, fully Boolean constraints destabilize training—so the approach balances legibility with the flexibility needed for successful optimization.

title: Learning to Select, Not Relearn: Hard-Routed Mixtures of Reasoning LoRAs
summary: Composing independently trained LoRA adapters into a single large language model is useful for multi-domain adaptation, especially when the original training data cannot be shared. A common approach is to use MoE-style routing over LoRA experts, but for frozen pretrained adapters, soft weighted combinations can change the unit-scale additive update under which each LoRA module was originally trained. We propose \textbf{Hard-Routed MoR-LoRA}, a two-stage framework for composing frozen reasoning LoRA experts through unit-scale hard selection. First, domain-specific LoRA adapters are trained independently using reinforcement learning from verifiable feedback to obtain reasoning experts. Then, all experts are frozen, reasoning traces are distilled from them, and only a lightweight shared router together with a small attention LoRA is trained for integration. The router selects exactly one expert per token using hard top-1 routing, while a straight-through estimator enables gradient-based training. Experiments across five benchmarks, multiple model scales, and additional model families show that Hard-Routed MoR-LoRA preserves expert behavior while requiring substantially fewer trainable parameters than soft-routing mixture baselines. Our analysis further shows that normalized soft mixtures often concentrate most routing mass on a single expert, suggesting that hard unit-scale routing provides a simple and efficient abstraction for frozen LoRA expert composition.

AI.tldr: They propose Hard-Routed MoR-LoRA, a framework to combine multiple independently trained LoRA reasoning experts with hard top-1 routing so their unit-scale behavior is preserved, achieving efficient multi-domain adaptation with fewer trainable parameters than soft MoE mixtures.
AI.motivation: Existing methods combine independently trained LoRA adapters using mixture-of-experts style soft routing. However, when LoRA experts are frozen and were trained as unit-scale additive updates, soft weighted combinations can distort their behavior and are parameter-inefficient. There is a need for a composition method that respects the original unit-scale training of each LoRA and efficiently integrates multiple domain-specific reasoning experts without sharing training data.
AI.method: The framework has two stages. (1) Train domain-specific LoRA adapters independently using reinforcement learning from verifiable feedback, producing specialized reasoning experts. (2) Freeze all experts, distill reasoning traces from them, and train only a lightweight shared router plus a small attention LoRA for integration. The router uses hard top-1 routing: it selects exactly one LoRA expert per token. A straight-through estimator is used so that this discrete routing can still be optimized with gradient-based training. The design enforces unit-scale, non-mixed expert selection instead of soft mixtures.
AI.result: Across five benchmarks, multiple base model scales, and different model families, Hard-Routed MoR-LoRA maintains the behavior and strengths of individual experts while using substantially fewer trainable parameters than soft-routing mixture-of-expert baselines. Empirically, soft normalized routing tends to put most probability mass on a single expert anyway, indicating inefficiency in soft mixtures.
AI.conclusion: Hard top-1 unit-scale routing is an effective and simple abstraction for composing frozen LoRA reasoning experts. It preserves expert behavior, reduces trainable parameter count compared to soft MoE-style mixtures, and aligns better with how LoRA updates were originally trained. The observed sparsity in soft mixtures supports replacing them with explicit hard routing for multi-domain LoRA composition.

title: Theory of Mind and Persuasion Beyond Conversation: Assessing the Capacity of LLMs to Induce Belief States via Planning and Action
summary: Theory of Mind (ToM) benchmarks for Large Language Models (LLMs) typically rely on passive question-answering formats, but the deployment of LLMs in increasingly agentic and autonomous forms demands new evaluations. In this paper we evaluate an agent's ability to induce specific belief states in other agents by taking actions rather than using conversational persuasion, a capability we call Non-Conversational Planning ToM (NCP-ToM). NCP-ToM is likely to be essential for many agent use-cases, including within user-assistant interactions and pedagogical contexts, but may also present manipulation or misinformation risks. Using a novel framework, NCP-ExploreToM, we subvert the conventional task structure by providing models with a set of belief state goals and requiring them to move objects or direct characters into rooms to achieve their goals. We evaluated six frontier models, including GPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro and the Claude 4 series, and a cohort of human participants, across 600 task instances. GPT-5 was successful on approximately 80% of tasks in the agentic setting, and was the only model to outperform human participants on our task, but was still less robust than humans across contexts. We additionally found that all models, like humans, performed better on tasks inducing true belief states than false belief states, which is a positive signal for alignment efforts. These findings highlight emerging social-reasoning capabilities in LLMs for non-conversational task completion and underscore the necessity of agentic evaluations for understanding the safety and alignment of autonomous social agents.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes and evaluates a new kind of Theory of Mind benchmark for LLM-based agents that requires them to change other agents’ beliefs through actions in a simulated environment, rather than through conversation.
AI.motivation: Existing Theory of Mind benchmarks for LLMs mostly use passive, question-answer formats that do not reflect how agentic, autonomous LLMs will operate in interactive environments. As LLMs are increasingly deployed as agents that act in the world and interact with users, it becomes important to assess whether they can reason about and intentionally shape others’ belief states via actions, which matters for beneficial applications (e.g., teaching, assistance) but also raises manipulation and misinformation concerns.
AI.method: The authors introduce Non-Conversational Planning ToM (NCP-ToM) and a framework called NCP-ExploreToM. Instead of answering questions about beliefs, models are given explicit belief-state goals and must plan and execute sequences of actions—such as moving objects or directing characters between rooms—to induce those target beliefs in other agents in a simulated environment. They test six state-of-the-art LLMs (including GPT-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, and Claude 4 variants) and human participants on 600 instances of these tasks, comparing performance across true-belief and false-belief scenarios and across different contextual variations.
AI.result: GPT-5 achieved about 80% task success in the agentic NCP-ToM setting and was the only model that surpassed human participants on average performance, though it remained less robust across varying contexts compared to humans. All tested models, similar to humans, performed better when the target was to induce true beliefs rather than false beliefs. Performance varied across models, with frontier systems showing substantial but still imperfect social reasoning in this non-conversational, action-based setup.
AI.conclusion: Agentic, action-based evaluations like NCP-ExploreToM reveal emerging but still limited social-reasoning and belief-manipulation capabilities in LLM agents, capabilities not well-captured by traditional question-answer ToM benchmarks. The observed bias toward inducing true over false beliefs is tentatively encouraging for alignment, but the ability to deliberately shape belief states also underscores potential manipulation risks. The authors argue that such agentic Theory of Mind benchmarks are necessary for understanding and governing the safety and alignment properties of increasingly autonomous social LLM agents.

title: BP-TTA: Balanced and Prototype-Guided Test-Time Adaptation in Dynamic Scenarios
summary: Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) enables models trained on a source domain to adapt online to unlabeled test data under distribution shifts. While recent TTA methods have moved beyond static settings and begun to consider continual domain shifts, they primarily address distribution drift and fail to account for class imbalance in dynamic scenarios. In real-world test-time streams, class imbalance and continual domain shifts often occur at the same time and interact with each other. In this paper, we propose a novel Balanced and Prototype-Guided Test-Time Adaptation (BP-TTA) method, which combines batch-balanced sampling with prototype-guided adaptation to handle the class imbalance and continual domain shift problems. BP-TTA constructs balanced adaptation batches by integrating current samples with high-confidence historical instances, effectively mitigating bias toward dominant classes and stabilizing online updates. Meanwhile, BP-TTA maintains evolving class prototypes during inference and leverages prototype similarity as a constraint for model adaptation, thereby improving the reliability of pseudo-labels and enhancing the stability of online updates under persistent domain shifts. Extensive experiments demonstrate that BP-TTA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art TTA methods in dynamic test-time streaming settings.

AI.tldr: A new Test-Time Adaptation method, BP-TTA, improves robustness under simultaneous class imbalance and continual domain shifts by using balanced batches and prototype-guided updates, outperforming prior TTA methods on dynamic streams.
AI.motivation: Existing TTA methods mostly assume static or purely distribution-drift scenarios and ignore that, in realistic online test streams, domain shifts and severe class imbalance co-occur and interact. This leads to biased adaptation toward frequent classes, unreliable pseudo-labels, and unstable updates over time. The paper is motivated by the need for an online adaptation mechanism that explicitly handles both continual domain shift and dynamically imbalanced class distributions.
AI.method: The authors propose BP-TTA (Balanced and Prototype-Guided Test-Time Adaptation). First, they perform batch-balanced sampling: each adaptation batch is formed by mixing current test samples with stored past samples that have high prediction confidence, selected to balance classes and thus reduce bias toward dominant classes. Second, they maintain evolving class prototypes in feature space during inference; these prototypes are updated over time and used as an additional constraint during adaptation by enforcing consistency between a sample’s representation and its closest class prototype. This prototype-guided regularization aims to improve pseudo-label reliability and stabilize online parameter updates across continual shifts.
AI.result: Across dynamic test-time streaming benchmarks (with both distribution shifts and class-imbalance scenarios), BP-TTA achieves higher accuracy and more stable performance over time than state-of-the-art TTA baselines. It shows improved performance particularly on minority classes and under persistent domain drift, indicating better robustness and less catastrophic degradation compared with prior methods.
AI.conclusion: By jointly addressing class imbalance and continual domain shift, BP-TTA provides a more realistic and robust approach to test-time adaptation than existing methods that only consider distribution drift. The combination of balanced batch construction using confident historical samples and prototype-guided adaptation improves pseudo-label quality and stabilizes online updates, leading to consistent gains over competing TTA approaches in dynamic streaming settings.

title: LuxEmo: Expressive Text-to-Speech Corpus for Luxembourgish
summary: State-of-the-art speech datasets predominantly focus on widely spoken languages, often overlooking low-resource languages such as Luxembourgish, which remain underrepresented in speech technology research. In this work, we introduce LuxEmo, a 21-hour conversational expressive speech corpus for Luxembourgish with 4 emotion categories. LuxEmo is derived from Radio Télévision Luxembourg (RTL) youth broadcasts, using automated detection followed by human validation. We propose a semi-automatic curation workflow combining voice activity detection, denoising, language identification, LuxASR-based segmentation, automatic emotion prediction, lexical cues, and targeted human review. Additionally, we benchmark five expressive TTS systems covering German-based cross-lingual transfer, multilingual Luxembourgish support, Luxembourgish adaptation, and non-parametric prosody transfer. Performance is evaluated using both objective metrics and human evaluation.

AI.tldr: The paper presents LuxEmo, a 21-hour Luxembourgish expressive speech corpus with four emotion categories, built via a semi-automatic curation workflow from youth radio broadcasts, and uses it to benchmark several expressive TTS approaches for this low-resource language.
AI.motivation: Most speech datasets target widely spoken languages, leaving low-resource languages like Luxembourgish underrepresented, especially for expressive and emotional speech, which limits progress in speech technology and TTS for these languages.
AI.method: The authors construct LuxEmo from RTL youth broadcast recordings via a semi-automatic workflow: voice activity detection, denoising, language identification, segmentation using a Luxembourgish ASR system (LuxASR), automatic emotion prediction and lexical cue analysis, followed by targeted human validation. They then train and benchmark five expressive TTS systems encompassing cross-lingual transfer from German, multilingual setups including Luxembourgish, Luxembourgish-specific adaptation, and non-parametric prosody transfer. Performance is measured with objective metrics and human listening tests.
AI.result: They obtain a 21-hour conversational Luxembourgish expressive corpus labeled with four emotions and successfully train multiple expressive TTS systems on it. The experiments show that the curated data supports expressive TTS for Luxembourgish and allow comparison of different modeling strategies (cross-lingual, multilingual, adaptation, prosody transfer) via quantitative scores and subjective human ratings.
AI.conclusion: LuxEmo fills an important gap for low-resource expressive speech in Luxembourgish and demonstrates that a semi-automatic pipeline can efficiently create a high-quality emotional speech corpus from broadcast data. The benchmark results indicate that the dataset is suitable for building and evaluating expressive TTS systems and can serve as a foundation for future research on Luxembourgish and related low-resource languages.

title: Ask the World Before Acting: Budgeted Environment Probing for World-Model Calibration
summary: Long-horizon language agents do not only choose actions; they carry a private model of the world from one decision to the next. When that model drifts, a later failure can be decided before the failing action is ever taken. We study a direct repair mechanism: before committing to the next task action, an agent may ask the environment about one belief field and write the answer back into its world model. This makes environment interaction a scarce calibration resource, not merely a way to advance the task. We introduce \method, a budgeted probing operator for structured belief tables. The useful probes are not the same everywhere. Procedural beliefs, such as tool dependencies, can often be repaired by targeted checks, but those checks spend steps that the task may need. Spatial beliefs, such as object locations and graph edges, rely more on structural cues; the agent's own confidence can be a poor guide when the world changes off-screen. A type-stratified analysis formalizes this probe-action frontier, and controlled experiments show that mid-planning environment evidence reduces terminal world-model error when the probe policy follows the structure of the task.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a method for language-agent planning that uses a limited budget of belief-updating queries to the environment, improving long-horizon performance by selectively repairing the agent’s internal world model mid-plan.
AI.motivation: Long-horizon language agents carry internal world models that can drift from reality over time, causing failures that are effectively predetermined before final actions. Existing approaches often treat environment interaction only as a way to progress tasks, not as an explicit calibration resource for beliefs. The authors want to understand and formalize how and when agents should spend scarce interaction steps to correct different types of beliefs (procedural vs. spatial) to reduce long-run errors.
AI.method: They introduce a budgeted probing operator, \method, for structured belief tables within an agent’s world model. At each decision point, before choosing the next task action, the agent can instead spend a step to query the environment about one specific belief field and then overwrite that field in its world model with the answer. They analyze how the value of such probes differs for different belief types (procedural vs. spatial) and perform a type-stratified analysis that characterizes the trade-off between probing and acting. Controlled experiments compare probe policies that follow task structure versus baselines, measuring resulting world-model error and task performance.
AI.result: Experiments show that inserting carefully chosen environment probes during planning—guided by task structure and belief type—reduces final world-model error compared to not probing or probing naively. Procedural beliefs can often be effectively corrected via targeted checks, though these consume steps, whereas spatial beliefs depend more on structural cues and are less reliably targeted using the agent’s own confidence estimates. Overall, structured probing improves calibration without excessively harming task progress when the probe policy respects the task’s structure.
AI.conclusion: Treating environment interaction as a scarce calibration resource, not just as task progression, enables language agents to better maintain accurate world models over long horizons. The proposed \method operator and type-stratified analysis clarify when and where to spend limited probes, showing that mid-planning environment evidence can substantially reduce terminal world-model error when probe policies are aligned with task structure. This suggests future directions for designing agents that strategically balance acting and information-gathering to maintain reliable internal models over extended tasks.

title: DigitalCoach: Communication and Grounding Gaps in Human and Agentic Computer Use Coaching
summary: Agents are increasingly capable of automating software tasks, but can they teach humans how to use software themselves? We introduce DigitalCoach, a multimodal dataset of 72 human expert-novice computer use coaching sessions consisting of 22,752 dialogue turns grounded in 28.1 hours of screen and input event recordings across five software applications. We use DigitalCoach to evaluate whether state-of-the-art models can teach humans how to use computers. Automated evaluation shows that models differ from humans in how they coach: models provide more direct instructions, but fewer explanations, error diagnoses, and knowledge-check questions. When we fix the coaching method, models produce utterances similar to human references yet poorly grounded in visual context. Interactive evaluation confirms that model coaches cause learners to passively follow instructions without deeper engagement and fall short in visual grounding. DigitalCoach lays a foundation for collaborative and proactive computer use coaching agents.

AI.tldr: DigitalCoach is a multimodal dataset and benchmark for studying how AI agents coach humans in computer use, revealing gaps between model coaching and human coaching, especially in explanation, diagnosis, engagement, and visual grounding.
AI.motivation: As agents become better at operating software autonomously, there is a need to understand whether they can also effectively teach humans to use software themselves. Existing work focuses largely on task automation, not on interactive human coaching grounded in real computer use. There is also a lack of rich, multimodal datasets capturing real expert-novice coaching interactions over diverse applications, which are necessary for training and evaluating coaching agents.
AI.method: The authors collect DigitalCoach, a dataset of 72 expert-novice coaching sessions, capturing 28.1 hours of screen recordings, input events, and 22,752 dialogue turns across five software applications. They use this dataset to benchmark state-of-the-art models as coaches, comparing their coaching style and quality against human coaches. The evaluation includes automated analyses of dialogue properties (e.g., direct instructions vs explanations, error diagnoses, knowledge checks) and assessments of how well model utterances align with visual context. They also run interactive experiments where human learners are coached by models, to measure learner behavior, engagement, and task performance.
AI.result: Automated evaluation shows key behavioral differences: models tend to give more direct, step-by-step instructions but provide fewer explanations, error diagnoses, and knowledge-check questions than human coaches. When constrained to use the same coaching method as humans, models can generate utterances that are textually similar to human references but are weakly grounded in the on-screen visual context. Interactive user studies reveal that model coaches lead learners to follow instructions passively, with limited active engagement or deeper understanding, and again perform poorly in visually grounded guidance.
AI.conclusion: DigitalCoach demonstrates that current state-of-the-art models are not yet effective computer-use coaches: they over-focus on direct instruction, lack rich explanatory and diagnostic behavior, fail to promote active learning, and struggle with visual grounding to the user’s screen. The dataset and benchmark provide a foundation for developing future coaching agents that are collaborative, proactive, and better grounded in multimodal computer-use contexts.

title: CDR-Bench: Evaluating Faithful Execution of Compositional, Order-Sensitive Data Refinement Recipes
comment: 29 pages, 20 figures. Corresponding authors: Daoyuan Chen and Yi R. Fung
summary: Data refinement involves executing multi-step recipes over evolving text states, where both composition and execution order of processing operators determine the outcome. While existing benchmarks either isolate text editing or entangle it with code and tool execution, it remains unclear whether LLMs can directly and faithfully execute these compositional, order-sensitive data refinement recipes. To fill this gap, we introduce CDR-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark featuring 3,462 high-quality tasks spanning four real-world data refinement domains and 29 distinct operators. Our benchmark evaluates models across atomic, order-agnostic, and order-sensitive settings, leveraging deterministic reference outputs to enable exact evaluation. Experiments on 10+ state-of-the-art LLMs reveal consistent failure patterns: performance degrades sharply in compositional settings, and order-sensitive recipe success collapses. These findings underline that current LLMs lack the procedural faithfulness required for reliable compositional data refinement.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CDR-Bench, a benchmark to test whether LLMs can accurately execute multi-step, order-sensitive data refinement recipes, and finds that current models fail badly on compositional and order-dependent tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks either focus narrowly on isolated text editing or mix text editing with code/tool use, so they do not directly test whether LLMs can faithfully execute multi-step, compositional, order-sensitive data refinement workflows. It is unknown if models can act as reliable executors of such procedural recipes.
AI.method: The authors construct CDR-Bench, a benchmark of 3,462 carefully curated tasks from four realistic data refinement domains, using 29 different text-processing operators. They design three evaluation regimes—atomic (single operator), order-agnostic (composition where order does not matter), and order-sensitive (composition where order is crucial). Each task has deterministic reference outputs, enabling exact-match evaluation. They then run over 10 state-of-the-art LLMs on these tasks and compare their performance across regimes.
AI.result: Across more than 10 state-of-the-art LLMs, performance drops sharply when moving from atomic to compositional settings. In particular, models perform especially poorly on order-sensitive recipes, where success rates collapse relative to simpler cases, revealing systematic weaknesses in faithfully following multi-step, order-dependent procedures.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs, even strong ones, do not yet exhibit robust procedural faithfulness in compositional data refinement: they struggle to accurately execute multi-operator, order-sensitive text processing recipes. CDR-Bench exposes these limitations and provides a rigorous benchmark for future models and methods aimed at improving compositional and procedural reliability in data refinement tasks.

title: Scalable Behaviour Cloning on Browser Using via Skill Distillation
summary: Internet users collectively perform an enormous range of skilled work through web browsers, from software development and document editing to search, forms, and enterprise workflows, making human browsing a highly scalable but under-exploited source of reusable browser skills. We argue that the bottleneck for browser agents is decision-making under incomplete information rather than low-level operation, and that the priors agents lack are already implicit in human interaction traces. We therefore study scalable behavior cloning for browser agents via skill distillation, converting user interaction trajectories into compact natural-language skills that agents can read, retrieve, reuse, and compose directly. We further organize the distilled skills into a skill graph so that growth proceeds through consolidation rather than unbounded accumulation. This suggests that the scalability of browser agents may come less from manually designed tasks and more from the collective skills already expressed by internet users. Our project is available at: https://lab.einsia.ai/browserbc/.

AI.tldr: They propose a scalable behavior-cloning framework that distills human web-browsing traces into reusable natural-language skills organized in a graph, enabling more capable browser agents.
AI.motivation: Human users already demonstrate rich, diverse, and skilled behavior in web browsers, but current browser agents underuse this resource and are bottlenecked by decision-making under uncertainty rather than low-level actions. The authors want to leverage the implicit priors in massive human interaction logs to make browser agents more capable and scalable without hand-designing tasks.
AI.method: They collect or use existing user interaction trajectories in web browsers and distill them into compact natural-language skill descriptions that encapsulate how to perform specific tasks. These skills are stored so that agents can read, retrieve, reuse, and compose them when acting in the browser. The skills are further organized into a structured skill graph to avoid unbounded growth and support consolidation of related behaviors.
AI.result: They demonstrate that human browsing traces can indeed be converted into reusable skills and structured into a skill graph that browser agents can leverage. While the abstract does not give quantitative results, it implies improved scalability and reusability of learned skills for browser agents, and they provide an online project implementation.
AI.conclusion: Scalable behavior cloning from human browser interaction is feasible and promising: by distilling interaction trajectories into natural-language skills and organizing them into a skill graph, browser agents can gain the missing priors for better decision-making. This shifts the path to scalability away from manually curating tasks toward mining the collective skills already embedded in large-scale internet user behavior.

title: Who Determines the Meaning of an Emotion? Affective Sovereignty as an Epistemic Consequence of Measurement Limits
summary: Emotion-sensing AI is rapidly becoming embedded in vehicles, home appliances, dialogue agents, and social infrastructure, giving rise to a sphere in which emotion is no longer confined to individual experience but is instead observed and computed at a societal scale, a domain we term the Affectosphere. Yet a central normative question in this domain has remained underexplored: who has the final authority to determine the meaning of one's own emotion? This study addresses the question from the epistemological side of measurement's structural limits. We define a meaning distribution as the distribution of labels assigned by annotators drawn from a population under a fixed annotation protocol, and decompose its uncertainty into reducible and irreducible components. We then demonstrate that, while emotion AI can assign high-confidence point labels and discriminate real differences at an aggregate level, the irreducible component of the meaning distribution for individual instances cannot be estimated with adequate coverage under realistic annotator counts, a systematic divergence we term the epistemic gap. The key finding is that high device confidence does not constitute evidence that irrecoverable meaning has been recovered. From this epistemic gap, together with an explicitly stated normative premise, namely that the output of a system which cannot recover a quantity in principle must not be treated as its authoritative determination, we derive the norm that the final interpretive authority over the meaning of one's emotion is procedurally reserved for the experiencing subject, the norm of affective sovereignty. These results suggest that the design, evaluation, and regulation of emotion AI should place explicit allocation of interpretive authority, rather than accuracy maximisation, at their core.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that due to fundamental limits in how well emotion can be measured from external annotations, AI systems that infer emotions cannot legitimately override a person’s own account of their feelings, and systems should be designed around this norm of “affective sovereignty.”
AI.motivation: Emotion AI is spreading into many aspects of life, effectively creating an “Affectosphere” where people’s emotions are sensed, computed, and acted upon at scale. Yet it is normatively unclear who has the rightful authority to define what a person’s emotion means—the sensing system, outside observers, or the person themselves. Existing work focuses on improving accuracy, not on the structural limits of measurement or their ethical implications. The paper aims to clarify these limits and derive a principled norm for allocating interpretive authority over emotions.
AI.method: The authors introduce the concept of a “meaning distribution”: for any given emotional instance (e.g., a facial expression or utterance), they consider the distribution of emotion labels that would be assigned by many annotators from a population under a fixed protocol. They formally decompose the uncertainty in this distribution into reducible (can be reduced with more data or better models) and irreducible components (due to genuine disagreement or ambiguity). Through theoretical analysis and empirical reasoning about realistic annotator counts, they assess what can and cannot be reliably recovered by emotion AI from such annotation data, focusing on coverage of the irreducible uncertainty component.
AI.result: They show that, even if an emotion AI system can output high-confidence labels and detect real aggregate-level differences, it cannot, in practice, estimate the irreducible component of the meaning distribution for specific individual instances with sufficient coverage given realistic limits on annotation. This structural shortfall, termed the “epistemic gap,” means that the system’s confidence about an individual’s emotional state does not indicate that the full, irrecoverable meaning of that emotion has been captured. Hence, the system is epistemically incapable of fully determining the meaning of a person’s particular emotional experience.
AI.conclusion: From the epistemic gap and the normative premise that a system which cannot in principle recover a quantity must not be treated as its authoritative source, the authors derive the norm of “affective sovereignty”: the experiencing subject must retain final interpretive authority over the meaning of their own emotion. They argue that emotion AI’s design, evaluation, and governance should prioritize explicit allocation of interpretive authority, rather than merely maximizing predictive accuracy, and that high-confidence outputs should not be treated as overriding or definitively specifying individuals’ emotional self-reports.

title: Generative Skill Composition for LLM Agents
summary: Recent LLM agents benefit from skills for solving complex tasks. Skills encapsulate modular packages of procedural knowledge and instructions for performing specialized tasks, such as setting up a sandboxed environment, running a test suite, or refactoring a function across multiple files. As skill libraries grow and become reusable across tasks and domains, selecting an appropriate skill composition has emerged as a central bottleneck. Existing approaches fall into two categories. One exposes the agent's reasoning to the entire skill collection; the other performs skill retrieval via embeddings or LLM-based rerankers. Both provide useful insights; however, they miss the structural nature of skill composition, which is a joint decision over which skills, how many, and in what order -- three dimensions that cannot be decoupled. We formalize this as structured skill composition: given a task and a skill library, predict an executable skill plan that jointly specifies the activated subset, count, and execution order. We propose SkillComposer, which instantiates structured skill composition as task-conditioned skill sequence prediction. SkillComposer uses a constrained autoregressive decoder over skill identifiers, so subset, count, and order emerge jointly from a single decoding pass, and dependencies between successive skills are captured naturally. We build a training set of task-composition pairs from a real, human-curated skill library. We then evaluate SkillComposer along two axes: composition quality on a held-out test set, and downstream task success on SkillsBench across two production-grade coding agents. On GPT-5.2-Codex, Gemini-3-Pro-Preview, SkillComposer raises the pass rate by +23.1, +18.2pp over the no-skill baseline, surpassing top-3 retrieval and matching the gold-skill retrieval upper bound at lower prompt-token cost.
