AI.tldr: The paper introduces SkillComposer, a method to automatically compose and order reusable skills for LLM agents, treating skill selection as structured sequence prediction rather than simple retrieval.
AI.motivation: LLM agents rely on reusable skills to handle complex, specialized tasks, but as skill libraries grow, choosing which skills to use, how many, and in what order becomes a bottleneck. Existing methods either expose the model to the full skill set or use retrieval, but they fail to model the inherently structured nature of composing multiple skills into an executable plan.
AI.method: The authors formalize structured skill composition as predicting a skill plan that jointly encodes the subset of skills, their count, and their execution order. They propose SkillComposer, which casts this as task-conditioned skill sequence prediction using a constrained autoregressive decoder over discrete skill identifiers. The decoder generates a sequence where subset, count, and order are jointly determined in a single pass, capturing dependencies between successive skills. They construct a dataset of (task, skill composition) pairs from a human-curated library to train the model.
AI.result: SkillComposer is evaluated on held-out composition prediction and on downstream task performance on SkillsBench with two production-grade coding agents (GPT-5.2-Codex and Gemini-3-Pro-Preview). It improves pass rates by +23.1 and +18.2 percentage points over no-skill baselines, outperforms top-3 retrieval methods, and matches the performance of an oracle gold-skill retrieval, while using fewer prompt tokens.
AI.conclusion: Modeling skill selection as structured sequence prediction via SkillComposer enables more effective and efficient use of large skill libraries in LLM agents. Jointly deciding which skills to activate, how many, and in what order yields better compositions than retrieval-based approaches, leading to substantial gains in task success at lower prompting cost.

title: CSTrader: A Testbed for Language-Grounded Trading in a Community-Driven Virtual Asset Market
summary: Niche asset markets, such as Counter-Strike 2 (CS2) weapon skins, are small, volatile, and heavily driven by community discussions and platform rules. These properties make them hard for traditional quantitative models, but provide an ideal testbed for studying how large language models (LLMs) turn unstructured text into trading actions. We present CSTrader, a multi-agent framework for language-grounded trading in the CS2 skin market. The system first integrates heterogeneous signals from various sources, then uses specialized agents for technical analysis, liquidity, events, and (reversed) sentiment, and finally applies risk control, transaction friction, and portfolio management agents to produce buy, sell, or hold decisions under realistic trading frictions. We build a live-like evaluation environment with real CS2 data from a highly volatile period and evaluate several recent LLM backbones. Across models, CSTrader consistently outperforms both a falling market index (-15.62%) and simple single-prompt LLM baselines, achieving up to a 7.58% cumulative return with controlled risk. Ablation studies show that liquidity, reversed sentiment, and transaction friction agents are crucial for turning noisy language signals into stable profits, suggesting that niche, language-driven markets are a useful benchmark for future language-to-action research. Code is available at: https://github.com/IatomicreactorI/CSGOTrading?tab=readme-ov-file#quick-start

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CSTrader, a multi-agent LLM-based trading system for the niche CS2 skin market, showing that structured, language-grounded agents can extract profitable trading signals from noisy community text under realistic frictions.
AI.motivation: Niche asset markets like CS2 skins are highly volatile, thinly traded, and heavily influenced by community discussions and platform rules, making them difficult for traditional quantitative trading models. At the same time, they offer a controlled environment to test how LLMs can convert unstructured language signals into concrete trading actions. The authors aim to build a realistic benchmark and framework to study language-to-action decision making in financial-like settings, especially where text dominates over structured fundamentals.
AI.method: They design CSTrader, a multi-agent framework where heterogeneous signals (community discussions, events, market data, platform rules) are first integrated. Then several specialized LLM-driven agents handle technical analysis, liquidity estimation, event analysis, and a novel reversed sentiment analysis. Additional agents model risk control, transaction frictions, and portfolio management. The system operates in a live-like backtest environment fed with real CS2 market data from a highly volatile period, and different LLM backbones are plugged in as the core reasoning engines. Ablation studies remove specific agents (e.g., liquidity, reversed sentiment, friction) to measure their impact on performance.
AI.result: In the simulated live environment, CSTrader beats both a declining market index (-15.62% over the test period) and simple single-prompt LLM baselines that lack the multi-agent structure and explicit frictions. Depending on the backbone model, CSTrader attains up to 7.58% cumulative return with controlled risk characteristics. Ablations show that removing liquidity modeling, reversed sentiment analysis, or transaction friction significantly degrades performance and stability, confirming their importance in reliably extracting value from noisy language signals.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that carefully structured, multi-agent LLM systems can translate unstructured community language into profitable trading decisions even in small, noisy, and friction-heavy markets. The importance of liquidity, reversed sentiment, and explicit friction modeling suggests that realistic market constraints and specialized language agents are key to stable performance. The authors propose niche, language-driven markets like CS2 skins as promising benchmarks for future research on language-to-action models, and release their codebase to facilitate such work.

title: When LLMs Read Tables Carelessly: Measuring and Reducing Data Referencing Errors
summary: While large language models (LLMs) perform well on table tasks, they still make data referencing errors (DREs), i.e., incorrectly citing or omitting table values, despite understanding the table structure. Beyond final-answer accuracy, DREs directly compromise the correctness and reliability of intermediate reasoning steps. Yet prior studies have only offered limited, small-scale analyses. In this work, we present the first systematic evaluation of tabular data referencing errors across different models and tasks. Our results show that DREs occur across all tested models (1.7B to 20B parameters). Furthermore, we demonstrate that incorporating data referencing as a critic significantly improves answer accuracy up to 12.0%, through critic-based filtering and rejection sampling. Finally, we trained a lightweight 4B-parameter critic model that achieves an average F1 score of 78.2% in detecting both in-distribution and out-of-distribution DREs, and effectively assists inference for larger models.

AI.tldr: The paper systematically studies how often and why large language models make tabular data referencing errors (mis-citing or omitting table values), and proposes a critic model that detects such errors and improves answer accuracy.
AI.motivation: Although LLMs can interpret tables and achieve good answer accuracy, they often mis-reference the underlying numbers (e.g., wrong row/column, missing values). These data referencing errors directly harm the faithfulness of intermediate reasoning, but prior work only analyzed them in a limited, small-scale way. There was no systematic benchmark across models or a dedicated mechanism to detect and mitigate such errors.
AI.method:
1) Define and taxonomize tabular data referencing errors (DREs) such as wrong value citations and omissions.
2) Build an evaluation framework and benchmarks covering multiple models (1.7B–20B parameters) and several table-related tasks to measure DRE rates.
3) Introduce a "data referencing critic" that checks model outputs for DREs and use it in two ways: (a) critic-based filtering of answers and (b) rejection sampling to select more faithful generations.
4) Train a dedicated, lightweight 4B-parameter critic model on DRE annotations and evaluate its ability to detect in-distribution and out-of-distribution DREs.
AI.result:
1) All evaluated models, regardless of size (1.7B–20B), exhibit non-trivial rates of data referencing errors when working with tables.
2) Adding a data-referencing critic during inference improves final answer accuracy by up to 12.0% through filtering and rejection sampling.
3) The 4B critic model attains an average F1 score of 78.2% in detecting DREs, including for out-of-distribution cases, and successfully supports larger models by flagging or filtering their erroneous references.
AI.conclusion: LLMs’ apparent competence on table tasks masks a systemic issue: they frequently mis-reference the underlying tabular data, undermining reasoning faithfulness. A dedicated data-referencing critic—especially a compact, specialized model—can effectively detect these errors and substantially improve answer accuracy for larger LLMs. Future systems should explicitly model and check data referencing, not just final-answer correctness, when using tables.

title: CLOUDADV: Decision-Aligned Instance Sizing with Zero-Shot Foundation Models under Drift
summary: Cloud virtual machines are often overprovisioned, creating avoidable cost and operational inefficiency. We present CLOUDADV, an interactive engineer-facing advisory system for cloud instance sizing under workload drift. The system combines zero-shot time-series forecasting with bounded recommendation generation across day-, week-, and month-scale planning horizons. For each query, CLOUDADV constructs a structured decision context from historical utilization, forecast summaries, current VM metadata, candidate instance options, pricing, and explicit sizing heuristics. A higher-capacity LLM is used offline to generate reference recommendations, while a smaller production model is evaluated on the same prompts to assess deployment-time alignment under latency and cost constraints. Evaluation prioritizes downstream recommendation quality using simulated Azure cost savings and ex-post exceedance, with rolling-origin forecast accuracy reported as a secondary diagnostic against classical and supervised baselines. In a case study of seven production VMs, the reference recommendations reduce simulated monthly cost from about \$1,503 to \$708, yielding \$795/month in savings (52.9%) under conservative heuristic constraints, while the highest observed exceedance rate among downgraded cases is 1.5%. Although Chronos-2 does not minimize every forecasting metric, it often induces recommendation patterns similar to those of a supervised per-VM baseline. These results suggest that zero-shot foundation models can support decision-aligned provisioning in non-stationary cloud environments while reducing the operational burden of repeated per-tenant retraining, revalidation, and redeployment.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CLOUDADV, a system that recommends cost-efficient cloud VM sizes under changing workloads using zero-shot time-series forecasting plus LLM-based recommendation generation, demonstrating large cost savings with minimal performance risk.
AI.motivation: Cloud VMs are commonly overprovisioned because engineers must manually pick instance types and sizes under uncertainty and workload drift, leading to unnecessary cloud spend and operational inefficiency. Existing sizing approaches often require per-tenant modeling, retraining, and maintenance, which is burdensome in non-stationary environments. The paper is motivated by the need for an interactive, engineer-facing tool that can give reliable, cost-aware sizing advice over multiple time horizons without heavy, repeated ML operations overhead.
AI.method: The authors build CLOUDADV, an advisory system that: (1) gathers a structured decision context for each VM from historical utilization, time-series forecasts, VM metadata, available instance options, pricing, and sizing heuristics; (2) uses zero-shot time-series forecasting (Chronos-2) to predict future utilization over day/week/month horizons; (3) uses a high-capacity LLM offline to generate reference instance-sizing recommendations given that context; (4) evaluates a smaller production LLM on the same prompts to measure how well low-latency, low-cost models align with the reference; and (5) evaluates performance mainly via downstream recommendation quality (simulated Azure cost savings and the fraction of time utilization exceeds capacity) with forecast accuracy as a secondary diagnostic versus classical and supervised baselines.
AI.result: In a case study on seven production Azure VMs, the reference recommendations using CLOUDADV reduce simulated monthly costs from about $1,503 to $708, saving around $795 per month (52.9% savings) while keeping the worst-case exceedance rate for downgraded VMs at only 1.5%, indicating low performance risk. Chronos-2, the zero-shot forecaster, does not always achieve the best pure forecasting metrics but yields recommendation behavior similar to a supervised per-VM baseline, supporting its practical suitability. The smaller production LLM’s outputs are compared to the larger reference model to gauge feasibility under deployment constraints.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that zero-shot time-series foundation models combined with LLM-based recommendation generation can effectively support decision-aligned cloud instance provisioning in non-stationary environments. CLOUDADV can deliver substantial cost savings with minimal exceedance risk while avoiding the operational burden of continual per-tenant model retraining and maintenance. This suggests that interactive LLM-powered advisors are a viable approach for scalable, cost-aware cloud capacity management across varying time horizons.

title: Reinforcement Learning with Metacognitive Feedback Elicits Faithful Uncertainty Expression in LLMs
summary: Metacognition is a critical component of intelligence that describes the ability to monitor and regulate one's own cognitive processes. Yet LLMs exhibit systemic deficiencies in key metacognitive faculties: they hallucinate with high confidence, fail to recognize knowledge boundaries, and misrepresent their internal uncertainty--undermining trustworthiness and reliability. Since monitoring task performance and adapting behavior accordingly are central to metacognition, we posit that models capable of accurately judging their own performance are better positioned to improve it. We operationalize this idea via two novel mechanisms: reinforcement learning with metacognitive feedback (RLMF), a paradigm to refine completion rankings during preference optimization based on the quality of a model's self-judgments of performance, and metacognitive data selection, which uses similar self-judgments to identify high-value training examples, outperforming naive active learning. We apply these innovations to the problem of faithful calibration (FC), a task that is itself fundamentally metacognitive: the goal is to align expressed with intrinsic uncertainty, difficult even for frontier LLMs. We adopt a two-stage, decoupled approach, first using these methods to calibrate the faithfulness of models' self-reported confidence scores, then mapping to natural, context-adaptable linguistic uncertainty via targeted output editing. Extensive experiments show RLMF achieves generalizable, state-of-the-art FC on diverse tasks while preserving accuracy. Further, RLMF surpasses standard RL by up to 63% while enhancing models' ability to assess and express their own capability limits. This positions RLMF as a promising paradigm to enhance LLM metacognition toward improved abilities and alignment, and suggests metacognitive performance as an effective RL signal to overcome limits of prior intrinsic feedback methods.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes methods to improve large language models’ metacognition—their ability to judge and communicate their own uncertainty—using reinforcement learning with metacognitive feedback and metacognitive data selection, achieving state-of-the-art faithful calibration while preserving task accuracy.
AI.motivation: LLMs often hallucinate with unjustified confidence, misjudge their own knowledge boundaries, and provide unreliable uncertainty estimates. These metacognitive failures harm trustworthiness and limit the usefulness of intrinsic feedback signals in RL-style training. The authors are motivated to develop a systematic way to help models better monitor and regulate their own performance, so that they can more accurately assess and communicate uncertainty and use these assessments to improve themselves.
AI.method: They introduce two mechanisms. (1) Reinforcement Learning with Metacognitive Feedback (RLMF): during preference optimization, the model not only generates answers but also self-judges its performance; the quality of these self-judgments is used to refine completion rankings and guide RL updates. (2) Metacognitive data selection: the model’s self-judgments are used to pick high-value training examples, outperforming naive active learning strategies. They apply these methods to faithful calibration via a two-stage pipeline: first, calibrating numeric confidence scores so they faithfully reflect intrinsic uncertainty; second, converting these calibrated scores into natural-language expressions of uncertainty through targeted output editing.
AI.result: Experiments across diverse tasks show that RLMF achieves state-of-the-art faithful calibration while maintaining base task accuracy. Compared to standard RL approaches, RLMF can improve performance by up to 63% on relevant metrics and leads to better self-assessment and expression of capability limits. Metacognitive data selection also surpasses simple active learning baselines in identifying beneficial training examples.
AI.conclusion: Using metacognitive performance as an explicit RL signal is a powerful way to improve LLMs’ uncertainty estimation and communication. RLMF and metacognitive data selection enhance models’ metacognitive abilities, yielding better calibrated confidence, improved recognition of capability limits, and stronger alignment without sacrificing accuracy. This suggests metacognitive feedback is a promising direction for overcoming limitations of previous intrinsic-feedback RL methods and for advancing trustworthy, self-aware language models.

title: One Reflection Is Not Enough: Self-Correcting Autonomous Research via Multi-Hypothesis Failure Attribution
summary: Autonomous research agents can now draft hypotheses, write code, run experiments, and produce papers, but they remain brittle when experiments fail. Under the prevailing paradigm, failure recovery is usually delegated to a single free-form reflection: a rich trajectory of metrics, logs, and design choices is compressed into one verbal critique, which often leads either to localized trial-and-error or to hard pivots that discard useful context. We propose SAGE, a Self-correcting, Autonomous, Grounded Experimenter, to tackle this failure-recovery bottleneck. Its core mechanism, Multi-Hypothesis Failure Attribution (MHFA), treats recovery as a structured causal diagnosis. By analyzing dynamic trajectory features, MHFA systematically generates multiple evidence-grounded explanations for a failure, independently evaluates their severity, and deterministically routes the verified root cause to the correct intervention level (hypothesis, experimental design, or implementation). To guarantee scientific honesty, SAGE further employs a grounded reporting mechanism that explicitly constrains drafted results to actual measured values, redacting hallucinated numbers. On a 12-topic, 5-domain benchmark, SAGE increases metrics-bearing outputs from 42% to 92% over a reflection baseline, improves artifact quality from 5.00 to 6.75/10, and blindly outscores AI-Scientist-v2 (52.0 vs. 48.2), with gains concentrated in code development and execution. While fully autonomous scientific writing and generating conference-ready papers remain notoriously difficult open problems for the entire field, SAGE successfully produces significantly more reliable and higher-quality scientific artifacts. Ultimately, by coupling structured recovery with explicit grounding constraints, SAGE significantly outperforms monolithic reflection paradigms, establishing a highly trustworthy foundation for future autonomous research.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SAGE, an autonomous research agent that recovers from failed experiments more effectively using structured causal diagnosis and grounded reporting, leading to more reliable and higher-quality scientific artifacts than baseline reflection-based systems.
AI.motivation: Existing autonomous research agents can conduct many parts of the research process but are brittle when experiments fail. Current failure-recovery methods typically rely on a single unstructured reflection step that compresses rich experimental trajectories into one verbal critique, often causing narrow trial-and-error or radical changes that discard useful information. There is a need for a more systematic, reliable way to diagnose and recover from failures while maintaining scientific honesty and reducing hallucinations in reported results.
AI.method: The authors propose SAGE (Self-correcting, Autonomous, Grounded Experimenter), centered on a mechanism called Multi-Hypothesis Failure Attribution (MHFA). MHFA analyzes dynamic trajectory features from an experiment (metrics, logs, design choices) to generate multiple, evidence-grounded explanations for why a failure occurred. It independently assesses the severity of each hypothesized cause and deterministically routes the confirmed root cause to the appropriate intervention level: hypothesis revision, experimental design change, or implementation fix. Additionally, SAGE uses a grounded reporting mechanism that constrains written results to measured values, explicitly removing or redacting hallucinated numerical claims. Performance is evaluated on a benchmark spanning 12 topics and 5 domains, and compared against reflection-based baselines and AI-Scientist-v2.
AI.result: On the benchmark, SAGE increases the fraction of outputs that include valid metrics from 42% with a reflection baseline to 92%. Artifact quality scores improve from 5.00 to 6.75 out of 10. In blind evaluation, SAGE outperforms AI-Scientist-v2 with an overall score of 52.0 versus 48.2, with particularly notable improvements in code development and execution capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Structured, multi-hypothesis failure diagnosis combined with explicit grounding of reported results substantially improves the reliability and quality of scientific artifacts generated by autonomous research agents. SAGE shows that moving beyond monolithic reflection-based recovery can yield more trustworthy and effective autonomous experimentation, providing a strong foundation for future systems targeting fully autonomous scientific research and paper writing, even though truly conference-ready, fully autonomous papers remain an open challenge.

title: Introspective Coupling: Self-Explanation Training Tracks Behavioral Change Despite Fixed Supervision
summary: When does training language models (LMs) to generate explanations of their predictions yield faithful introspection, rather than superficial imitation? We study LMs trained to explain which features of their inputs influenced their behavior, using models' counterfactual behavior on modified inputs as supervision. Surprisingly, we find that LMs trained on fixed counterfactual explanations derived from earlier checkpoints of themselves, or even from behaviorally similar models in different families, frequently produce explanations more faithful to their own current behaviors than to those of their training targets. This "introspective" coupling between LM explanations and behaviors occurs when training explanations remain sufficiently correlated with current behaviors over the course of training, even as behaviors themselves shift. We also show that introspective coupling tracks behavior shifts: when explanation training is provided concurrently with other post-training objectives, explanations track those shifts without requiring updated supervision. This phenomenon appears in multiple tasks, including sycophancy and refusal, and is robust to label noise. Overall, our results show that even fixed datasets of counterfactual explanations can provide scalable and generalizable post-training signal for introspection.

AI.tldr: The paper studies when language models trained to explain their own predictions actually reflect their true decision process rather than just mimic training labels. Using counterfactual behavior as supervision, they find that even with fixed, possibly noisy explanation datasets, models’ generated explanations tend to stay aligned with their *current* behavior and adapt as the model’s behavior changes.
AI.motivation: Explanation-generating LMs are often used for interpretability, but it is unclear whether their explanations are faithful to the models’ underlying decision process or merely imitate provided examples. This work aims to understand under what conditions training on explanation data—specifically counterfactual explanations—leads to genuine introspection that tracks how the model’s behavior actually changes over time.
AI.method: They train language models to generate explanations of which input features influenced their outputs, using counterfactual behavior (model outputs on modified inputs) as supervision. Crucially, they fix this explanation dataset, often generated from earlier checkpoints of the same model or from similar but distinct model families. They then study how the trained models’ explanations compare to both the training targets and the models’ *current* behaviors, measuring a notion of “introspective coupling”—how well explanations predict actual behavior under counterfactual changes. They also test scenarios where explanation training is combined with other post-training objectives to see whether explanations track induced behavior shifts without updating the explanation dataset.
AI.result: They find that models trained on fixed counterfactual explanations regularly produce explanations more aligned with their *current* behavior than with the specific explanation labels they were trained on. This introspective coupling arises when the initial explanation labels remain sufficiently correlated with the model’s evolving behavior across training, even though the behavior itself drifts. Moreover, when other post-training goals (e.g., encouraging or discouraging certain responses) change model behavior, the explanation behavior shifts accordingly, without needing new explanation labels. These results hold across multiple tasks such as sycophancy and refusal and are robust even when the explanation labels include noise.
AI.conclusion: Fixed datasets of counterfactual explanations—generated even from earlier checkpoints or related models—can still provide effective post-training signals that yield explanations tracking the model’s own current behavior. Under appropriate correlation conditions, explanation training induces a form of faithful introspection that generalizes across behavior shifts and is relatively robust to label noise, suggesting a scalable way to train models to introspect about which input features drive their decisions.

title: Surprise as a Signal for Plasticity and Metacognition
summary: We study a single idea across two settings: that a prediction-error signal, computed by a small predictor over the latent space of a frozen encoder, can serve both as a gate on plasticity and as a substrate for metacognition. In the first system, a non-parametric episodic memory writes a new concept only when this surprise is high, and a periodic offline replay phase consolidates recent traces into a slow linear readout. On a continual stream of 1000 ImageNet classes with a frozen DINOv2 or I-JEPA backbone, the consolidation phase recovers 17.7 points of retention on the oldest classes for DINOv2 and 51.3 points for I-JEPA (single-seed runs), and an ablation shows that replaying only a recent window is worse than no replay at all. In few-shot evaluation the same memory reaches 91.6% on 5-way 1-shot mini-ImageNet, above a task-specific baseline, while a harder 500-way regime exposes the true difficulty. In the second system, the same surprise signal, computed in a shared text-image space, modulates the behaviour of a vision-language model: it answers assertively when a concept is known, hedges when it is partially familiar, and refuses to identify the object and asks for an explanation when it is novel, learning the concept from a single user utterance. The external detector separates known from novel concepts at an AUROC of 0.966 (95% CI +/-0.024), far above the model's own verbalised confidence (0.618), while its token-level confidence sits below chance under greedy decoding; after a sleep phase that empties the fast store, the system recalls 99.2% of fifty taught facts from the consolidated store while a base model recovers none. We report both systems as proof-of-concept, with explicit limitations, and position the second against recent episodic-memory and personalised-VLM work.

AI.tldr: The paper explores a single prediction-error (surprise) signal over frozen latent representations as a mechanism both for controlling when new memories are written and for enabling metacognitive behavior in a vision-language model.
AI.motivation: Current systems struggle with continual learning (avoiding catastrophic forgetting) and with calibrated metacognition (knowing what they don’t know). The authors are motivated to find a simple, shared computational principle—prediction error over a frozen representation—that can address both efficient memory writing/consolidation and reliable novelty detection/metacognitive control in large models.
AI.method: They use a frozen encoder (DINOv2 or I-JEPA) and train a small predictor in its latent space. The prediction error (surprise) guides: (1) an episodic memory that stores new examples only when surprise is high, with a periodic offline replay phase that consolidates recent episodic traces into a slow linear readout; (2) a vision-language system where the same surprise signal in a shared text-image space modulates the model’s response style (assert, hedge, or refuse and ask for an explanation), and supports one-shot learning of novel concepts which are then consolidated. They evaluate on continual ImageNet-class streams, few-shot mini-ImageNet, and a personalised VLM-style setting with a surprise-based external novelty detector.
AI.result: In continual learning with 1000 ImageNet classes and frozen DINOv2/I-JEPA backbones, their consolidation phase significantly improves retention on the oldest classes: +17.7 points for DINOv2 and +51.3 points for I-JEPA, and they find that replaying only a recent window is counterproductive. The same memory system achieves 91.6% accuracy on 5-way 1-shot mini-ImageNet and exposes difficulty in a 500-way few-shot regime. In the VLM setting, the external surprise-based detector distinguishes known vs. novel concepts with AUROC 0.966 (±0.024), outperforming the model’s own stated confidence (0.618), while token-level confidence is below chance. After a sleep/consolidation phase that transfers from fast to slow memory, the system recalls 99.2% of 50 taught facts, whereas the base model recalls none.
AI.conclusion: A single prediction-error signal over frozen representations can effectively gate memory writing and support metacognitive control in large models. The approach yields improved continual-learning retention, competitive few-shot performance, and strong novelty detection plus reliable recall of user-taught concepts in a VLM. The authors present both systems as proofs of concept, note clear limitations, and relate the second system to emerging work on episodic memory and personalised vision-language models, suggesting prediction-error-driven memory as a promising design pattern.

title: Design and Implementation of Agentic Orchestrations and Orchestration of Agents
summary: Agentic Business Process Management has gained momentum recently. The prospect is that the autonomy of AI agents, i.e., predominantly LLM-based agents, can be balanced with a certain level of robustness, tractability, and traceability through a combination with process technology. In this paper, we provide a classification framework for agentic orchestration options along properties such as task specificity, traceability and tractability, autonomy and reactivity, and correctness assurance and present qualitative decision criteria for realizations of different scenarios. We also provide metrics for the quantitative assessment of realization properties and show them through different agentic implementations of a predictive light sensing scenario. Altogether, this work aims at providing properties, criteria, and metrics for the design and implementation of agentic orchestrations and orchestration of agents.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a framework to classify and evaluate different ways of orchestrating AI agents within business processes, providing qualitative criteria and quantitative metrics, and illustrating them on a predictive light-sensing scenario.
AI.motivation: Agentic Business Process Management is emerging as organizations want to use autonomous LLM-based agents within business processes while still preserving robustness, tractability, and traceability. There is currently a lack of systematic guidance on how to orchestrate such agents and how to assess different orchestration options. The paper addresses this gap by structuring the design space and providing evaluation tools.
AI.method: The authors develop a classification framework for agentic orchestration options along key properties: task specificity, traceability and tractability, autonomy and reactivity, and correctness assurance. They define qualitative decision criteria for choosing orchestration patterns in different scenarios and propose quantitative metrics for assessing realization properties. The approach is demonstrated using multiple agentic implementations of a predictive light-sensing scenario.
AI.result: The result is a structured taxonomy of orchestration options for agentic BPM, a set of qualitative decision criteria for selecting appropriate orchestration designs, and a collection of quantitative metrics to compare implementations. These are instantiated and exemplified via different implementations of a predictive light sensing use case, showing how the metrics distinguish between orchestration choices.
AI.conclusion: The work concludes that effective agentic BPM requires explicit consideration of properties such as autonomy, traceability, and correctness when orchestrating agents. The proposed framework, decision criteria, and metrics offer practitioners and researchers a basis for systematically designing, comparing, and improving agentic orchestration solutions in business processes.

title: A time-series classification framework for individual-level absenteeism prediction under severe class imbalance
summary: Staff absenteeism imposes substantial operational costs in high-demand work environments such as healthcare, emergency services, meat processing, construction, and courier and delivery services, where proactive workforce planning depends on reliable individual-level absence prediction. Existing regression and classification approaches share a structural limitation; they map features observed at time t to labels at the same time t, reproducing already-realised outcomes rather than predicting future events, and discard the sequential behavioural structure inherent in individual attendance histories. We propose a Time Series Classification (TSC) framework that separates historical attendance sequences from future absence labels, enabling genuinely proactive prediction. Due to the lack of public longitudinal attendance data, we construct a reproducible simulated dataset calibrated to the UCI dataset. We analyse Binary Focal Loss (BFL) and Geometric Mean (G-Mean) loss under severe class imbalance using only the imbalance ratio $ρ$. For BFL, the initial gradient ratio is $ρα/(1-α)$, implying the balanced weight $α= 1/(1+ρ) \approx 0.023$. Experiments show that performance is governed mainly by $α$, with BFL achieving specificity 0.813 and balanced accuracy 0.888, comparable to G-Mean. Unlike BFL, G-Mean adapts automatically without parameter calibration. Among three deep learning architectures evaluated, Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), and the hybrid LSTM-Fully Convolutional Network (LSTM-FCN), the LSTM-FCN delivers strong precision and specificity. Stable performance is obtained with batch sizes >= 64 and window sizes between 40-80 days, yielding balanced accuracy of approximately 80% on held-out test data.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a time series classification framework for proactively predicting individual staff absences using attendance histories, and evaluates loss functions and deep-learning architectures under severe class imbalance on a simulated dataset.
AI.motivation: Operational environments like healthcare and emergency services incur high costs from staff absenteeism, and proactive workforce planning requires accurate individual-level absence prediction. Existing methods treat each time point independently and predict already-realised absences instead of future ones, failing to exploit the sequential structure in attendance records. There is also a lack of suitable public longitudinal datasets and a need to handle severe class imbalance in absence prediction.
AI.method: The authors propose a time series classification framework that explicitly separates historical attendance sequences from future absence labels, enabling forward-looking prediction. They construct a reproducible simulated longitudinal attendance dataset calibrated to a UCI dataset due to the lack of public data. To address severe class imbalance, they analytically examine Binary Focal Loss (BFL) and G-Mean loss using only the imbalance ratio ρ, derive the gradient ratio and a theoretically balanced weight α = 1/(1+ρ), and experimentally study the effect of α. They then compare three deep learning architectures—LSTM, CNN, and LSTM-FCN—under different batch sizes and time-window lengths.
AI.result: For BFL, they show that the initial gradient ratio is ρα/(1−α), giving an optimal balanced weight α ≈ 0.023 for the studied imbalance ratio. Empirically, model performance is driven mainly by α, and with the chosen α, BFL achieves specificity 0.813 and balanced accuracy 0.888, closely matching G-Mean performance. G-Mean does not require manual parameter tuning and adapts to imbalance automatically. Among architectures, the hybrid LSTM-FCN yields the strongest precision and specificity. Robust results are obtained for batch sizes of at least 64 and temporal windows of 40–80 days, with balanced accuracy around 80% on held-out test data.
AI.conclusion: The proposed time series classification framework enables genuinely proactive prediction of staff absences by leveraging sequential attendance histories, overcoming the limitations of static regression/classification approaches. Properly configured Binary Focal Loss can handle severe class imbalance effectively, though G-Mean offers a simpler, parameter-free alternative. The LSTM-FCN architecture is particularly effective for this task, and performance is stable across reasonable choices of batch size and time window length, indicating practical viability for workforce planning applications.

title: Modality-Driven Search with Holistic Trace Judging for ARC-AGI-2
comment: 37 pages, 4 figures; source code available at https://github.com/beetree/ARC-AGI
summary:
Large language models can produce fluent, internally coherent reasoning traces for abstract reasoning tasks while still being confidently wrong - making selection among candidates, not just generation, the central challenge. I present a solver for ARC-AGI-2, a few-shot visual reasoning benchmark, built around two principles: (i) treating reasoning modalities as search operators, generating diverse candidates independently across text, image, and code channels, and (ii) context-preserving holistic judging, in which a judge model jointly compares all candidate reasoning traces within a single long-context prompt. Unlike self-consistency or majority voting, this approach reliably recovers correct minority hypotheses on tasks where the modal answer is wrong.
  On the ARC Prize semi-private evaluation set, the solver achieves 72.9 percent at USD 38.99 per task - the highest score on the verified leaderboard at the time of writing, exceeding the best standalone frontier models, GPT-5.2 Pro at 54.2 percent and Gemini 3 Pro at 54.0 percent, by +18.7 percentage points. On the public evaluation set, it achieves 76.1 percent at USD 19.69 per task. I release the full source code and document extensive negative results, including the finding that prescriptive prompting templates and iterative refinement systematically reduce hypothesis diversity and degrade performance.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a multi-modal, judge-based solver for the ARC-AGI-2 benchmark that treats different reasoning channels as search operators and uses a holistic long-context judge to pick among diverse candidate solutions, significantly outperforming frontier LLMs.
AI.motivation: Large language models can generate fluent but confidently wrong reasoning traces, especially on abstract reasoning tasks like ARC-AGI-2. Existing aggregation methods such as self-consistency or majority voting often fail when the majority answer is wrong, so there is a need for a method that both encourages hypothesis diversity and reliably selects the correct minority hypothesis.
AI.method: The author builds a solver around two key principles. First, reasoning modalities (text, image, code) are treated as distinct search operators, each independently generating diverse candidate solutions. Second, a dedicated judge model performs context-preserving holistic judging: all candidate reasoning traces and outputs are placed into a single long-context prompt so the judge can compare them jointly and select the best hypothesis. The method is evaluated on ARC Prize semi-private and public evaluation sets, and various design choices such as prescriptive prompting and iterative refinement are systematically tested and ablated.
AI.result: On the ARC Prize semi-private evaluation set, the solver reaches 72.9% accuracy at a cost of $38.99 per task, topping the verified leaderboard at the time and surpassing strong baseline models (GPT-5.2 Pro at 54.2% and Gemini 3 Pro at 54.0%) by 18.7 percentage points. On the public evaluation set, it achieves 76.1% accuracy at $19.69 per task. Negative results show that prescriptive prompting templates and iterative refinement decrease hypothesis diversity and harm performance.
AI.conclusion: By framing reasoning as a search over heterogeneous modalities and using a holistic long-context judge instead of naive voting schemes, the proposed solver can reliably identify correct minority hypotheses and achieve state-of-the-art performance on ARC-AGI-2. The findings highlight the importance of diversity in candidate reasoning and caution against overconstraining model behavior with rigid prompting or iterative refinement. The author releases full source code and detailed negative results to support reproducibility and further research.

title: ACE: Pluggable Adaptive Context Elasticizer across Agents
summary: The increasing complexity of agentic tasks has led to rapidly growing trajectory lengths, which poses significant challenges for large language model (LLM) based agents with fixed context windows. Existing context management techniques, such as truncation and summarization, suffer from inherent inflexibility and irreversibility: once information is discarded or compressed, it cannot be recovered even when it becomes critically relevant in later decision steps. To address these limitations, we propose the Adaptive Context Elasticizer (ACE), a plug-and-play module that elastically orchestrates historical step information into the agent's context at each decision step. ACE maintains a lossless message maintenance layer that stores both raw messages and compressed abstractions for each historical step, while a context orchestration layer adaptively assigns each step an elastic type as raw, abstract, or drop, at every decision step based on the current task state. This reversible design ensures that the main LLM always receives a compact yet information-rich context. We adapt ACE to four diverse agent frameworks, including ReAct, DeepAgent, WebThinker, and MiroFlow, without training or architectural modifications. Experiments show that ACE consistently outperforms truncation and summarization baselines, and brings consistent performance gains across all four agent frameworks.

AI.tldr: They propose ACE, a plug-and-play module that adaptively manages long interaction histories for LLM-based agents, keeping context compact but fully recoverable, and it improves performance over truncation and summarization in multiple agent frameworks.
AI.motivation: As agentic tasks for LLMs get longer and more complex, trajectory lengths exceed fixed context windows. Standard solutions like truncating old messages or summarizing them are irreversible: once details are cut or compressed away, they cannot be restored even if later become crucial, which harms decision quality. The authors aim to design a context management mechanism that is both compact and reversible, so that important past information can be reintroduced when needed without retraining the underlying agents.
AI.method: They introduce Adaptive Context Elasticizer (ACE), a plug-and-play module for LLM-based agents. ACE has a lossless message maintenance layer that stores for each historical step both the raw message and its compressed abstraction. On top of this, a context orchestration layer adaptively assigns an elastic type—raw, abstract, or drop—to each historical step at every decision point, conditioned on the current task state. This lets the agent receive a context that fits within the window but can switch between detailed and abstracted history as needed, and previously dropped steps can later be reintroduced in raw form using the stored data. ACE is integrated into four existing agent frameworks (ReAct, DeepAgent, WebThinker, and MiroFlow) without any additional training or architecture changes.
AI.result: In experiments across four agent frameworks, ACE consistently outperforms baseline context management strategies based on truncation and summarization. It provides improved task performance and more robust behavior as trajectory lengths grow, with performance gains observed in all tested agent architectures.
AI.conclusion: A reversible, elastic context management module like ACE can effectively handle long trajectories in LLM-based agents by balancing compactness and information richness. By maintaining lossless historical records plus abstractions and dynamically selecting the appropriate representation per step, ACE overcomes the inflexibility of truncation and summarization, yields better performance across diverse agent frameworks, and can be deployed in a plug-and-play fashion without retraining.

title: Which Tokens Matter? Adaptive Token Selection for RLVR with the Relative Surprisal Index
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a powerful tool for propelling Large Language Models (LLMs) beyond imitation-based training towards more robust reasoning capabilities. Among existing approaches, RL with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a pivotal paradigm for advancing LLM reasoning. Despite its empirical success, recent studies have offered different insights. One line of inquiry advocates prioritizing high-entropy token positions during training, while another perspective cautions against allowing low-probability tokens to dominate gradient updates. Notably, although high-entropy tokens are usually correlated with low probability, both paradigms empirically yield substantial performance gains. In this work, we argue that evaluating sampled-token probability or entropy in isolation is insufficient to capture the policy optimization dynamics. To resolve this tension, we introduce the Relative Surprisal Index (RSI), a principled, information-theoretic metric that naturally couples the token's entropy with the probability of the selected token. We show that, under mild conditions, RSI is related to the local ratio between the first-order variations of the logit-gradient norm and predictive entropy under a selected-logit perturbation. Building on RSI, we propose RSI Selection (RSI-S), an entropy-adaptive token filtering method that retains tokens within a stable RSI interval. RSI-S successfully reconciles previous contradictory paradigms and filters out both redundant low-surprisal tokens and unstable high-surprisal tail tokens. Empirical evaluations show that RSI-S achieves higher avg@32 accuracy across different model scales (Qwen2.5-1.5B, 3B, and 7B) on AIME and AMC benchmarks: RSI-S improves avg@32 accuracy by 2--3 percentage points over GRPO. Overall, RSI offers a promising perspective for RLVR improvement.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Relative Surprisal Index (RSI) and an RSI-based token selection method (RSI-S) to improve reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) for large language models, reconciling conflicting strategies around focusing on high-entropy vs low-probability tokens.
AI.motivation: Existing RLVR methods for LLM reasoning show that (1) prioritizing high-entropy token positions and (2) avoiding low-probability tokens both empirically help, despite appearing contradictory because high entropy often coincides with low probability. Evaluating token probability or entropy alone cannot fully explain policy optimization behavior, motivating a more principled metric that captures their joint effect on learning dynamics.
AI.method: They define Relative Surprisal Index (RSI), an information-theoretic metric that couples a token's entropy with the probability of the sampled token. Analytically, under mild assumptions, they relate RSI to the local ratio between first-order changes in the logit-gradient norm and in predictive entropy when perturbing the selected logit. Using RSI, they design RSI Selection (RSI-S), a token filtering rule that keeps only tokens whose RSI lies within a stable interval, thereby discarding both low-surprisal (redundant) and extreme high-surprisal (unstable) tokens during RLVR training.
AI.result: On reasoning benchmarks AIME and AMC, using RLVR with RSI-S yields higher avg@32 accuracy than GRPO across Qwen2.5 models of sizes 1.5B, 3B, and 7B, with gains of about 2–3 percentage points. This demonstrates that RSI-guided token selection improves sample efficiency and final reasoning performance compared to a strong baseline.
AI.conclusion: Relative Surprisal Index provides a theoretically grounded view of how entropy and token probability jointly influence RL optimization for LLMs. The proposed RSI-S selection rule unifies previously conflicting training heuristics by adaptively retaining only tokens in a stable RSI band, leading to measurable performance gains in RLVR for mathematical reasoning tasks and suggesting RSI as a promising direction for further RLVR algorithm design.

title: Scientific Explanations in Health Sciences: Causality, Trust, and Epistemic Adequacy
summary: Medical Artificial Intelligence (AI) is widely expected to transform clinical practice, yet the decision-making processes of many Machine Learning (ML) models remain opaque. Explainability has been advanced as a partial remedy to clarify why AI generates predictions, particularly in high-stakes contexts. Despite ongoing efforts, debates on what constitutes an adequate medical explanation remain unsettled. Yet, explanation has long been a central topic of inquiry in the philosophy of science and medicine. The insights developed in these fields, however, have been largely overlooked in contemporary explainable AI (XAI) research, leaving its foundational assumptions insufficiently examined. To address this gap, this paper develops a critical review at the intersection of philosophy of science and XAI. It examines prevailing accounts of what counts as an explanation in the health sciences and assesses their adequacy for informing XAI in medicine, arguing that they provide necessary conditions for a philosophically grounded approach to explainability in this domain. Building on this foundational philosophical literature, the discussion identifies three central axes of analysis: the role of causality in medical reasoning, the epistemic and relational dimensions of medical trust, and the criteria of explanatory adequacy as shaped by the pragmatic needs of diverse stakeholders. By integrating philosophical analysis with current developments in medical AI, the paper outlines principles for designing XAI systems that offer explanations that are not only epistemically robust but also aligned with the epistemic and practical requirements of clinical decision-making, shaping ongoing debates in medical XAI toward underexplored conceptual foundations.

AI.tldr: The paper critically reviews how philosophical theories of explanation can inform explainable AI (XAI) in medicine, proposing principles for clinically useful, trustworthy, and causally grounded explanations from medical AI systems.
AI.motivation: Medical AI is becoming central in clinical practice, but many machine learning models are black boxes. Existing XAI methods lack a clear, philosophically grounded notion of what counts as a good medical explanation, despite a rich literature in philosophy of science and medicine on explanation and causality. This gap risks producing explanations that are technically sophisticated but conceptually inadequate for real-world clinical decision-making and trust.
AI.method: The paper conducts a conceptual and critical review at the intersection of philosophy of science, philosophy of medicine, and current XAI work in healthcare. It surveys major accounts of explanation in the health sciences, analyzes how they handle causality, trust, and stakeholder needs, and evaluates their suitability as foundations for medical XAI. It then synthesizes these insights into analytic axes and design principles for XAI systems.
AI.result: The review identifies three key axes for structuring medical XAI: (1) explanations should appropriately engage with causal reasoning used in medicine; (2) they must account for both epistemic (knowledge-based) and relational (interpersonal) aspects of medical trust; and (3) explanatory adequacy is pragmatic and stakeholder-dependent, varying across clinicians, patients, and regulators. These axes reveal limitations in current XAI approaches that neglect philosophical insights on explanation and medical reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Philosophical theories of explanation provide necessary conditions for a robust, conceptually sound framework for medical XAI. By incorporating causal reasoning, nuanced models of trust, and pragmatic, stakeholder-sensitive standards of explanatory adequacy, designers can build XAI systems whose outputs are not only technically correct but also meaningful and actionable in clinical contexts. The paper argues that future debates and designs in medical XAI should be grounded in these underused philosophical foundations.

title: Think in English, Answer in Korean: Efficient Adaptation of Multilingual Tool-Using Agents
summary: We present LuckyStar 111B, a 111B-parameter hybrid reasoning model developed through a collaboration between Cohere and LG CNS for Korean-English enterprise agents under practical memory and serving constraints. The model trains from Cohere's fully post-trained Command A model rather than a new pretraining run, and uses preamble conditioning to switch between concise non-reasoning behavior and longer tool-oriented reasoning. We study four choices for scaling tool-using agents efficiently: multilingual supervised fine-tuning, reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards for multi-step tool-use tasks, language-consistency rewards for Korean user-facing responses, and 4-bit quantization for single-GPU serving. The adapted model improves mathematical reasoning, function calling, and agentic natural-language-to-SQL (NL2SQL) performance while preserving general Korean and English instruction-following quality. These results provide a practical recipe and failure-mode analysis for adapting post-trained multilingual models to verifiable agentic workflows under memory-constrained deployment.

AI.tldr: LuckyStar 111B is a 111-billion-parameter Korean-English hybrid reasoning model adapted from a post-trained base model (Command A), designed for enterprise agents with tight memory and serving constraints, using conditioning, fine-tuning, RL with verifiable rewards, language-consistency rewards, and 4-bit quantization to improve tool-use, math, and NL2SQL while preserving general instruction following.
AI.motivation: Enterprise deployments often require powerful bilingual (Korean-English) reasoning and tool-using agents, but face strict memory and serving constraints; training a new large model from scratch is costly, and naively adapting existing models can harm language quality or tool-using reliability, so the authors aim to provide a practical, efficient recipe for upgrading a post-trained multilingual model into a verifiable, tool-oriented agent suitable for constrained environments.
AI.method: Starting from Cohere’s fully post-trained Command A model instead of running new pretraining, the authors build LuckyStar 111B by: (1) using preamble conditioning to switch between concise non-reasoning behavior and extended, tool-oriented reasoning; (2) performing multilingual supervised fine-tuning focused on tool use; (3) applying reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards on multi-step tool-use tasks to shape reliable agent behavior; (4) adding language-consistency rewards to ensure high-quality Korean user-facing responses; and (5) applying 4-bit quantization for single-GPU serving under tight memory limits, while evaluating on math, function calling, and NL2SQL benchmarks.
AI.result: The adapted LuckyStar 111B model shows improved mathematical reasoning, function-calling accuracy, and agentic natural-language-to-SQL performance, while maintaining strong general instruction-following abilities in both Korean and English; it can be served efficiently with 4-bit quantization on a single GPU, and the authors document practical trade-offs and failure modes observed during this adaptation process.
AI.conclusion: LuckyStar 111B demonstrates that a large, post-trained multilingual model can be practically adapted into a high-quality, verifiable, tool-using enterprise agent under tight memory constraints by combining conditional reasoning modes, multilingual SFT, RL with verifiable and language-consistency rewards, and low-bit quantization; the work offers a concrete adaptation recipe and failure-mode analysis for similar deployments of multilingual agentic systems.

title: FARS: A Fully Automated Research System Deployed at Scale
summary: Recent automated research systems show that language-model agents can generate hypotheses, run experiments, and write complete manuscripts, but most evidence still comes from selected examples, human-framed topics, or a few pre-defined research tasks. We present FARS (Fully Automated Research System), a fully automated AI-for-AI research system designed to operate across research topics at scale. FARS autonomously generates and advances projects through ideation, planning, experimentation, and writing, using stage-specific agents coordinated through a shared workspace that records proposals, code, logs, results, and manuscripts. In its first public deployment, FARS produced 166 complete research papers spanning 67 fine-grained AI/ML topics while preserving intermediate artifacts as an auditable corpus rather than a curated set of successes. We evaluate this corpus with 282 structured reviews from volunteer reviewers covering 140 papers, including overall ratings, sub-scores, integrity checks, and LLM-use disclosure. The reviews indicate that FARS can produce review-worthy and occasionally strong AI/ML research artifacts in a large-scale public deployment, while also exposing recurring failure modes in narrow experimental scope, methodological limitations, and integrity issues.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FARS, a fully automated AI research pipeline that autonomously generates, executes, and writes up AI/ML research at scale, and demonstrates its capabilities and failure modes via a large public deployment and human review study.
AI.motivation: Existing demonstrations of language-model-based research agents are limited to cherry-picked examples, constrained topics, or a narrow set of pre-defined tasks, leaving unclear whether such systems can conduct substantive, scalable research across diverse topics and with transparent, auditable artifacts. There is a need for a more systematic, large-scale, and less curated evaluation of end-to-end automated research systems.
AI.method: The authors design FARS, a multi-agent, stage-specific automated research system where different LLM-based agents handle ideation, planning, experimentation, and manuscript writing, all coordinated through a shared workspace that stores proposals, code, logs, experimental outputs, and drafts. They publicly deploy FARS to autonomously generate and complete research projects across many AI/ML topics, resulting in 166 full papers. They then collect 282 structured human reviews from volunteer reviewers on 140 of these papers, including quantitative ratings, sub-scores, integrity checks, and disclosure of LLM use, to assess the quality and limitations of the produced research.
AI.result: FARS autonomously produced 166 complete AI/ML research papers spanning 67 fine-grained topics, each with associated intermediate artifacts. Human reviewers provided 282 structured reviews for 140 papers, assessing overall quality, methodological soundness, integrity, and LLM use. The reviews show that a non-trivial fraction of the papers are "review-worthy," with some evaluated as strong research contributions, while also identifying common weaknesses such as limited experimental scope, methodological flaws, and occasional integrity issues.
AI.conclusion: Fully automated, LLM-driven research systems like FARS can generate research outputs that are often suitable for peer review and sometimes of strong quality, at scale and across diverse AI/ML topics, when structured as coordinated stage-specific agents with an auditable workspace. However, the system also exhibits recurrent failure modes—especially in experimental breadth, methodology, and integrity—highlighting that while autonomous AI research pipelines are promising, significant challenges remain before they can reliably match robust human research practices.

title: Arena-T2I Hard: Benchmarking and Improving Faithfulness with Dependency-Aware Checklist
summary: Faithfulness -- how precisely a generated image aligns with its prompt -- is increasingly central to the real-world utility of text-to-image (T2I) models. Existing faithfulness benchmarks, however, rely on simple atomic instructions, on which top-tier systems already achieve near-perfect scores. As T2I models enter creative workflows, users issue multi-faceted requests combining intricate spatial relationships, stylistic constraints, and complex text rendering. In this setting, a single binary VLM-judge score no longer captures which specific constraints the model fails to satisfy. We introduce Arena-T2I Hard, a 310-prompt stress benchmark drawn from real arena T2I logs, with approximately 30 decomposed yes/no constraints per prompt spanning six categories, including text rendering. The strongest closed-source system we evaluate reaches 0.855 with a 33~pp performance gap across 11 systems, demonstrating substantial discriminative power. Moreover, high public-arena rankings fail to predict faithfulness, confirming that holistic Bradley-Terry (BT) preference scores prioritize aesthetics over fine-grained prompt adherence. We propose a dependency-aware checklist reward that decomposes each prompt into a DAG of yes/no questions and zeroes descendants of failed parents, turning faithfulness into a per-constraint training signal. Combined with a BT aesthetic reward via group-decoupled normalization (GDPO), which standardizes each reward within its rollout group so neither collapses, the recipe attains a strictly better faithfulness-aesthetics trade-off on SD3.5-Medium and FLUX.1-dev under MMRB2 pairwise comparisons than every single-reward, naive weighted-sum, or 4-reward BT-ensemble baseline.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Arena-T2I Hard, a challenging benchmark and training framework to evaluate and improve how faithfully text-to-image models follow complex, real-world prompts while balancing aesthetics.
AI.motivation: Existing text-to-image faithfulness benchmarks are too simple, using atomic instructions where leading models already score near-perfectly. In real creative workflows, user prompts are complex, combining spatial relations, style, and text rendering, and current evaluation methods (single binary scores, holistic preference rankings) cannot diagnose which specific constraints fail or distinguish faithfulness from aesthetics. The authors aim to create a more discriminative benchmark and a better training signal that separately yet jointly optimizes faithfulness and aesthetics.
AI.method: 1) Construct Arena-T2I Hard, a stress-test benchmark of 310 real prompts from arena logs, each decomposed into roughly 30 yes/no constraints across six categories including text rendering. 2) Evaluate 11 T2I systems with these decomposed constraints to quantify per-constraint faithfulness performance. 3) Propose a dependency-aware checklist reward: represent each prompt as a DAG of yes/no questions where descendants of failed parent constraints are zeroed, yielding a structured, per-constraint faithfulness signal. 4) Combine this faithfulness reward with an aesthetic Bradley-Terry (BT) preference reward using group-decoupled normalization (GDPO), which normalizes rewards within rollout groups so neither signal dominates. 5) Train or fine-tune models such as SD3.5-Medium and FLUX.1-dev under this joint reward and compare them against single-reward, naive weighted-sum, and 4-reward BT-ensemble baselines using MMRB2 pairwise comparisons.
AI.result: Arena-T2I Hard reveals a wide performance spread among top systems: the best closed-source model scores 0.855 with a 33 percentage point gap across 11 systems, demonstrating strong discriminatory power. Public arena rankings based on holistic BT scores do not correlate with faithfulness on this benchmark, confirming that existing rankings emphasize aesthetics over prompt adherence. Models trained with the proposed dependency-aware checklist reward plus GDPO-combined BT aesthetics reward achieve a strictly better faithfulness–aesthetics trade-off on SD3.5-Medium and FLUX.1-dev in MMRB2 pairwise comparisons than any baseline using a single reward, naive weighted sums of rewards, or a 4-reward BT ensemble.
AI.conclusion: Complex, decomposed evaluation is necessary to meaningfully assess faithfulness of modern T2I systems. Arena-T2I Hard provides such a benchmark, exposing substantial differences in models’ ability to satisfy multi-faceted prompts and showing that popular arena rankings over-index on aesthetics. The dependency-aware checklist reward turns decomposed constraints into an effective training signal, and when combined with an aesthetic BT reward via GDPO, yields models with strictly improved faithfulness–aesthetics trade-offs over standard reward-combination schemes. This establishes a practical recipe for building T2I models that better follow detailed prompts without sacrificing visual appeal.

title: A Self-Evolving Agentic System for Automated Generation and Execution of Biological Protocols
summary: Autonomous wet-lab experimentation requires more than plausible protocol text: biological intent, quantitative procedures, device constraints and experimental feedback must remain aligned from protocol and SOP design to code and physical execution. We developed ProtoPilot, a self-evolving multi-agent system, together with an expert-grounded benchmark and evaluation framework for testing this conversion as an experimental automation problem. The framework spans 294 synthetic-biology and molecular-biology tasks derived from 98 gold-standard protocols, wet-lab expert rubrics, device-level validity gates and real experimental tests. ProtoPilot incorporates layer-wise verifiability, multi-agent orchestration and a runtime-updated skill library to generate protocols, expand SOPs, synthesize SDK-compliant code and revise workflows from wet-lab feedback. It achieved a Top@3 expert-preference rate of 90.2%, an overall protocol-to-code gate pass rate of 89.5% and an Opentrons pass rate of 88.24%, compared with 32.35% for OpenTrons-AI. Wet-lab validation produced interpretable readouts, Sanger-confirmed products and feedback-corrected PCA-assembled DNA targets, establishing a verifiable route to autonomous experimentation. Together, these results show that the evaluation framework captures execution-relevant requirements for autonomous wet-lab automation, and that ProtoPilot can meet them by converting protocol and code generation into validated execution and feedback-guided revision.

AI.tldr: ProtoPilot is a multi-agent system and benchmark for reliably turning biology protocols into executable, verifiable robotic experiments with feedback-driven improvement.
AI.motivation: Existing autonomous wet-lab systems often stop at generating plausible textual protocols, without ensuring that biological goals, quantitative steps, device constraints, and execution feedback are consistently aligned from human-readable SOPs down to robotic code. There is also a lack of rigorous, expert-grounded benchmarks that test this full pipeline as an integrated automation problem.
AI.method: The authors build ProtoPilot, a self-evolving multi-agent system that handles protocol generation, SOP expansion, SDK-compliant code synthesis, and workflow revision from experimental feedback. They design an evaluation framework with 294 tasks derived from 98 expert, gold-standard protocols, add expert rubrics, device-level validity gates, and real experimental tests. ProtoPilot uses layer-wise verifiability, multi-agent orchestration, and a runtime-updated skill library to ensure each stage can be checked and improved.
AI.result: On the benchmark, ProtoPilot reached a 90.2% Top@3 expert-preference rate, an 89.5% pass rate on protocol-to-code validation gates, and an 88.24% Opentrons execution pass rate, far surpassing an existing baseline (OpenTrons-AI at 32.35%). In real wet-lab runs, ProtoPilot generated interpretable outputs, correct Sanger-validated products, and successfully feedback-corrected PCA-assembled DNA constructs.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that their benchmark framework successfully encodes the critical, execution-relevant requirements for autonomous wet-lab automation, and that ProtoPilot can satisfy these requirements by integrating protocol and code generation with verifiable execution and iterative, feedback-guided refinement, thereby providing a practical route toward fully autonomous biological experimentation.

title: Evo-PI: Aligning Medical Reasoning via Evolving Principle-Guided Supervision
summary: Despite recent progress, the reasoning capabilities of large multimodal language models (MLLMs) remain fundamentally constrained by static supervision, where fixed prompts, rules, or reward models provide non-adaptive guidance throughout training. Such static signals are often sufficient to enforce output formats, but fail to shape the underlying reasoning process, leading to brittle generalization and performance saturation in complex decision-making tasks. We propose Evo-PI, a principle-centric learning framework that treats reasoning principles as explicit, language-based supervision signals that can be generated, evaluated, and iteratively evolved. Instead of relying on fixed rewards, Evo-PI enables a co-evolutionary loop in which principles guide model reasoning, while model behaviors in turn refine the principles that supervise them. This dynamic alignment mechanism allows supervision to progressively adapt to the model's reasoning deficiencies. We instantiate Evo-PI in medical visual question answering as a high-stakes testbed requiring structured visual-textual reasoning. Across eight benchmarks and multiple model backbones, Evo-PI consistently improves reasoning accuracy, achieving gains of up to 24.6%. Our results suggest that evolving principle-guided supervision offers a scalable and general paradigm for training expert-aligned reasoning in MLLMs. Code is available at https://github.com/zhengxianda/Evo_PI.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Evo-PI, a framework that uses evolving, language-based reasoning principles as dynamic supervision to improve multimodal reasoning, demonstrated on medical visual question answering with large performance gains.
AI.motivation: Large multimodal language models are limited by static supervision signals (fixed prompts, rules, or reward models), which can enforce output formats but do not adequately shape the internal reasoning process, causing brittle generalization and saturation on complex decision-making tasks. There is a need for a supervision paradigm that can adapt to a model’s evolving reasoning strengths and weaknesses, especially in high-stakes domains like medical VQA.
AI.method: Evo-PI treats reasoning principles as explicit, language-based supervision. It sets up a co-evolutionary loop where (1) principles guide the model’s reasoning during training, and (2) the model’s observed behaviors are used to evaluate and refine these principles. Rather than a fixed reward model, this dynamic, principle-centric alignment process iteratively updates the supervision signals so they adapt to the model’s deficiencies. The framework is instantiated in the medical visual question answering setting with multiple MLLM backbones and evaluated on eight benchmarks.
AI.result: When applied to medical visual question answering, Evo-PI consistently improves reasoning accuracy over baselines across eight benchmarks and several model backbones, with performance gains reported up to 24.6%.
AI.conclusion: Evo-PI shows that iteratively evolving, principle-based supervision can more effectively shape the reasoning behavior of large multimodal language models than static signals. The approach appears scalable and general, providing a promising paradigm for training expert-aligned, robust reasoning in complex, high-stakes tasks. Code is publicly released for further exploration and reproduction.

title: RAISE: LLM-based Automated Heuristic Design with Robust Adversary Instance Search
summary: Automated Heuristic Design (AHD) with Large Language Models (LLMs) has shown remarkable progress in discovering high-quality heuristics. However, existing LLM-based AHD methods optimize heuristics for a fixed training instance set and may fail catastrophically when deployed under real-world distributional shifts. We propose Robust Adversary Instance Search (RAISE), a framework that integrates constrained worst-case instance search within a principled neighborhood of the training distribution into the LLM-based evolutionary search loop. RAISE treats robust AHD as a constrained adversarial instance search problem: the outer loop evolves heuristics via LLM operators, while an LLM-free inner loop efficiently identifies hard instances within an epsilon-ball around the training instance set using a basis distribution parameterization with boundary projection. Comprehensive experiments on Online Bin Packing (OBP), Online Job Shop Scheduling (OJSP), and Online Vehicle Routing (OVRP) across five distribution families demonstrate that existing LLM-based AHD methods degrade by up to 19 times under distribution shift, while RAISE consistently maintains strong performance across all tested distributions and problem scales

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RAISE, a framework that makes LLM-based automated heuristic design robust to distribution shifts by adversarially searching for hard problem instances near the training data and evolving heuristics against them.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based automated heuristic design methods produce heuristics that work well on a fixed training set but can fail badly when real-world instances differ from that training distribution. There is a need for a principled way to make such heuristics robust to realistic distribution shifts rather than just overfitting to a static instance set.
AI.method: RAISE formulates robust heuristic design as a constrained adversarial instance search problem. In an outer evolutionary loop, an LLM generates and evolves heuristic algorithms. In an inner loop, a non-LLM procedure searches for worst-case (hard) instances within an epsilon-neighborhood of the original training distribution. This inner search uses a basis distribution parameterization plus boundary projection to keep candidate instances within the allowed distributional ball. The heuristics are evaluated against these adversarial instances, guiding the evolution toward robust performance.
AI.result: On three online combinatorial optimization problems—Online Bin Packing, Online Job Shop Scheduling, and Online Vehicle Routing—across five different distribution families, the authors empirically show that standard LLM-based automated heuristic design can suffer performance degradation of up to 19× under distribution shift. In contrast, RAISE-trained heuristics maintain strong performance across all tested distribution shifts and problem sizes, indicating significantly improved robustness.
AI.conclusion: Integrating constrained adversarial instance search into the LLM-based evolutionary design loop yields heuristics that generalize much better under distribution shift. RAISE provides a principled robust AHD framework that outperforms existing LLM-based methods in both stability and worst-case performance across diverse online optimization tasks and distributions.

title: Large Databases Need Small, Open-Weight Language Models
summary: Language model systems built around proprietary APIs often operate on a token-based cost model.
