This becomes prohibitively expensive in the context of large databases, where LM-enhanced relational operators can incur costs exceeding $10,000 for a single set of experiments, hindering thorough research and practical deployment. In this paper, we demonstrate that quantized, open-weight models running locally on just 16GB of VRAM can match or exceed the accuracy of closed-source counterparts at lower latency and a fraction of the price, challenging the prevailing assumption that closed-source LM APIs are necessary for effective LM-database integration. We present and analyze the key system optimizations required to efficiently deploy these open-weight models within an LM-DB system. By integrating these local models into the BlendSQL v0.1.0 framework, we demonstrate a 390x reduction in overall costs and 3.8x reduction in latency compared to a proprietary LM API. We make our code available at https://github.com/CapitalOne-Research/play-by-the-type-rules/tree/main/sembench.

AI.tldr: Open-weight, locally hosted quantized language models can power LM-enhanced database systems with similar or better accuracy than proprietary API models, while cutting costs by up to 390x and latency by 3.8x.
AI.motivation: Token-based pricing of proprietary LM APIs makes LM-enhanced relational operators extremely costly on large databases, sometimes surpassing $10,000 for a single experimental run. This high cost obstructs comprehensive research, experimentation, and real-world deployment of LM-integrated database systems. The community commonly assumes that only large closed-source APIs are strong enough for such tasks, so there is a need to question this assumption and explore cheaper, open-weight alternatives that can run on commodity hardware.
AI.method: The authors evaluate quantized, open-weight language models deployed locally with about 16GB of VRAM inside an LM-database (LM-DB) system. They implement and benchmark key systems optimizations that make these models efficient in practice, and integrate them into the BlendSQL v0.1.0 framework. They then compare accuracy, latency, and cost of this local deployment against a baseline proprietary LM API across LM-enhanced relational operators and workloads.
AI.result: Quantized open-weight models running locally can match or outperform closed-source API models in accuracy for LM-database tasks. With the proposed system optimizations and BlendSQL integration, the local models achieve a 390x reduction in overall monetary cost and a 3.8x reduction in latency relative to a proprietary LM API, while retaining or improving result quality.
AI.conclusion: Closed-source, token-priced LM APIs are not strictly necessary for effective LM-database integration. Properly optimized, quantized open-weight models on modest hardware can deliver comparable or better accuracy while dramatically reducing both cost and latency. This opens the door to more scalable, affordable, and reproducible LM-DB research and deployment, and the released codebase provides a practical reference implementation.

title: Creating Intelligence: A Computational Foundation for AGI
summary: This work introduces a new computational theory of mind grounded in set theory and hyperdimensional computing. Whereas traditional neural networks rely on continuous weights and matrix multiplication, this framework works with sparse binary data. It represents information as discrete sets, directly modeling biological neural population codes. I demonstrate that associative memory emerges naturally from network topologies featuring a combinatorially expanded hidden layer. Learning is driven by topological plasticity rather than scalar weight adjustments. This architecture unifies auto-associative and hetero-associative learning under a single core algorithm: information retrieval via subset pattern matching and exact nearest-neighbor search. Operating with constant-time complexity, these mechanisms bridge perceptual data (sparse distributed representations) and symbols (sparse holographic representations) without continuous bottlenecks. Mapping this framework to neuroanatomy, I propose that both the cerebellum and the neocortex implement variants of this algorithm, making subset pattern matching the fundamental engine of cognition. Because it relies on discrete logic rather than matrix arithmetic, this algorithm translates directly into in-memory hardware. This opens a new route toward synthetic intelligence with human-level energy efficiency.

AI.tldr: Proposes a set-theoretic, sparse-binary, hyperdimensional computing framework as a new computational theory of mind and engine of cognition.
AI.motivation: Conventional neural networks use continuous weights and matrix multiplications, which are energetically expensive, biologically implausible in some respects, and form a continuous bottleneck between perceptual distributed codes and symbolic representations. The paper seeks a model that is more directly aligned with biological population codes, amenable to efficient hardware, and that unifies different forms of associative memory under a single principle.
AI.method: Define a computational architecture based on sparse binary sets and hyperdimensional representations instead of continuous vectors. Construct networks with combinatorially expanded hidden layers where connectivity/topology—not scalar weights—encodes learned associations. Use subset pattern matching and exact nearest-neighbor search as the core retrieval operations, showing how these realize both auto- and hetero-associative memory. Analyze algorithmic complexity (constant-time operations) and map the core operations to hypothesized implementations in cerebellum and neocortex, then consider realizations in in-memory digital hardware.
AI.result: Shows that associative memory behavior emerges from the proposed sparse, topologically plastic networks, with both auto-associative and hetero-associative retrieval realized by the same subset-matching mechanism. Demonstrates that the architecture can bridge perceptual sparse distributed codes with symbolic, holographic-like representations without relying on continuous-valued computations, and that the core operations can be implemented with constant-time complexity and mapped conceptually onto known cortical and cerebellar structures.
AI.conclusion: Subset pattern matching over sparse binary sets, implemented via topological plasticity in combinatorially expanded layers, can serve as a unifying algorithmic principle for cognition and associative memory. This discrete, set-theoretic framework is plausibly instantiated in neocortex and cerebellum, eliminates the need for continuous-weight matrix arithmetic, and is naturally suited to in-memory hardware, potentially enabling synthetic intelligence systems with energy efficiency approaching that of the human brain.

title: Adaptive Cluster-First Route-Second Decomposition for Industrial-Scale Vehicle Routing
summary: Large-scale capacitated vehicle routing problems (CVRPs) are commonly addressed using cluster-first route-second (CFRS) approaches that split a routing instance into smaller, computationally tractable subproblems. Existing splitting methods typically rely on fixed partitioning rules, predefined optimization objectives, or learned policies, which may perform inconsistently across instances exhibiting different spatial, demand, and operational characteristics. In this work, we propose an adaptive CFRS system that formulates a decomposition procedure as an iterative decision-making process. Motivated by the recent success of large language models (LLMs) in reasoning and tool selection, the system employs an LLM as a high-level decision maker that analyzes the evolving decomposition state and selectively applies further clustering, balancing, and refinement operators. The proposed algorithm jointly partitions customers and vehicles, enabling capacity-aware clustering while adapting partitioning decisions to the characteristics of each problem. We evaluate the approach on synthetic and benchmark-derived CVRP instances containing up to 500,000 customers. Experimental results demonstrate competitive performance on benchmark-scale instances while exhibiting improved scalability and robust routing quality on substantially larger problems. These results highlight the potential of adaptive, LLM-guided decision support as a practical approach for industrial-scale vehicle routing and large-scale logistics planning.

AI.tldr: An adaptive, LLM-guided cluster-first route-second system for large-scale capacitated vehicle routing jointly partitions customers and vehicles using iterative decisions, achieving competitive quality and better scalability up to 500k customers.
AI.motivation: Traditional CFRS methods for large-scale CVRPs depend on rigid partitioning rules, fixed objectives, or learned policies that may not generalize well across instances with differing spatial layouts, demands, and operational constraints. There is a need for more flexible, instance-adaptive decomposition strategies that can maintain routing quality while scaling to extremely large problems encountered in industrial logistics.
AI.method: Formulate the decomposition (clustering and splitting) of a CVRP instance as an iterative decision-making process. Use a large language model as a high-level controller that observes the current decomposition state and chooses among a set of operators—such as further clustering, balancing, and refinement steps—to adaptively improve the partitioning. The algorithm jointly partitions customers and vehicles to achieve capacity-aware clusters and dynamically adjusts partitioning actions to each problem’s characteristics.
AI.result: On synthetic and benchmark-derived CVRP instances with up to 500,000 customers, the proposed method attains competitive solution quality on standard benchmark scales and demonstrates improved scalability and robust routing performance on much larger instances, where traditional methods struggle.
AI.conclusion: An LLM-guided, adaptive CFRS framework can effectively handle very large CVRPs by intelligently orchestrating clustering and refinement operators. This suggests that LLM-based decision support is a promising and practical approach for industrial-scale vehicle routing and large-scale logistics planning, offering both robustness and scalability beyond conventional fixed-policy decomposition methods.

title: An Agentic AI Framework to Accelerate Scientific Discovery in Plant Phenotyping
summary: High-throughput plant phenotyping now generates image derived datasets far faster than scientists can analyze them. At Oak Ridge National Laboratory's Advanced Plant Phenotyping Laboratory (APPL), automated stations image hundreds of plants daily across multiple remote sensing modalities; yet, trait extraction and interpretation remain manual, expert-bound, and strictly post-hoc, making analysis, not acquisition, the binding constraint on discovery. We present an end-to-end agentic AI framework that turns the facility from a data factory into an interactive autonomous, discovery platform, where scientists partner with AI agents to accelerate time to insight. A conversational Co-Scientist Agent translates a scientist's natural-language question into a structured analysis plan, and a headless Compute Agent dispatches Vision Transformer segmentation and trait extraction on the Frontier exascale supercomputer. The two agents run in separate security and resource domains and communicate over a secure, token-authenticated streaming channel, a design that accounts for the federation, data-movement, and provenance realities cloud-native agentic frameworks ignore, ensuring end-to-end provenance is captured for every interaction. The framework turns a days- to weeks-long analysis process into an interactive loop where agents reason over results, recommend next analyses, and respond to follow-up questions in seconds.

AI.tldr: An AI-agent framework converts a high-throughput plant phenotyping facility into an interactive, autonomous discovery platform, enabling rapid, conversational analysis of large imaging datasets with full provenance.
AI.motivation: High-throughput plant phenotyping systems produce massive, multi-modal imaging datasets much faster than scientists can manually extract traits and derive insights from them. At APPL, imaging is automated and scaled, but analysis is manual, expert-dependent, slow (days to weeks), and strictly post-hoc, making data analysis—not data acquisition—the main bottleneck to scientific discovery. There is a need for a system that lets scientists interactively query data, automate complex analysis workflows, and close the loop between data collection and hypothesis-driven discovery while respecting security, resource, and provenance constraints.
AI.method: The paper proposes an end-to-end agentic AI framework composed of two main agents: (1) a conversational Co-Scientist Agent that takes a scientist’s natural-language questions and converts them into structured analysis plans, and (2) a headless Compute Agent that runs large-scale image analysis, including Vision Transformer-based segmentation and trait extraction, on the Frontier exascale supercomputer. The agents operate in distinct security and resource domains but communicate via a secure, token-authenticated streaming channel designed to handle federated environments, data movement, and provenance. Every interaction and computational step is captured to maintain end-to-end provenance.
AI.result: Using this framework, analyses that previously took days to weeks can be executed in an interactive loop, with turnaround times on the order of seconds for planning, dispatching, and reasoning over results. The agents can iterate on findings, propose subsequent analyses, and answer follow-up questions quickly, effectively transforming the plant phenotyping facility’s role from a passive data producer into an active, AI-augmented discovery environment.
AI.conclusion: The agentic AI framework demonstrates that integrating conversational scientific assistants with large-scale compute and robust, provenance-aware infrastructure can remove analysis as the main bottleneck in high-throughput phenotyping. By enabling rapid, interactive, and secure end-to-end analysis, the system turns a traditional data factory into an autonomous discovery platform where human scientists and AI agents collaborate to accelerate time to insight.

title: Harnessing Textual Refusal Directions for Multimodal Safety
summary: To improve safety in Large Language Models (LLMs) we can either perform post-training alignment or exploit refusal directions in the activation space. Both strategies are less feasible in Multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) as they require unsafe multimodal data, harder to collect than their unimodal counterpart. In this work, we relax this constraint and investigate whether textual refusal directions, extracted directly from the LLM backbone, generalize across modalities (i.e., image, video). Preliminary findings confirm this ability, though effectiveness is conditioned by layer selection, steering strength, and cross-modal alignment, with the latter causing safe multimodal inputs to be spuriously steered toward refusal. Building on this, we introduce Modality-Agnostic Refusal Steering (MARS), a light-weight training-free approach that injects multimodal safety without the need for multimodal safety data. MARS corrects modality misalignment via activation re-centering, adaptively scales steering strength within a geometrically defined trust region, and selects the optimal intervention layer, operating at the first generated token. Evaluated on five SOTA MLLMs across safety, utility, and video jailbreak benchmarks, MARS achieves consistent safety gains while preserving utility. These results reveal that safety-relevant structure is shared across modalities and that textual refusal directions are a powerful and underexplored foundation for multimodal alignment.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that safety behaviors learned in text-only LLMs ("refusal directions" in activation space) can be reused to improve safety in multimodal LLMs without extra multimodal safety data, and proposes a method called MARS to do so effectively.
AI.motivation: Multimodal LLMs need to be safe across text, images, and video, but collecting labeled unsafe multimodal data for alignment is expensive and difficult. Existing approaches like post-training alignment or activation steering usually assume text-only data. The authors want to know whether safety-related internal representations learned from text (refusal directions) can transfer to multimodal settings and be used to improve safety without needing new multimodal safety datasets.
AI.method: 1) Extract textual "refusal directions" from the LLM backbone in activation space. 2) Empirically test if these directions generalize when the model processes multimodal inputs (image, video). 3) Identify factors that affect effectiveness: which model layer is steered, how strong the steering is, and cross-modal alignment issues that may cause overshooting and unnecessary refusals. 4) Propose Modality-Agnostic Refusal Steering (MARS), a training-free technique that: (a) re-centers activations to correct modality misalignment, (b) adaptively scales steering strength within a trust region defined in activation space, and (c) automatically chooses the best intervention layer, steering at the first generated token. 5) Evaluate MARS on five state-of-the-art multimodal LLMs over safety benchmarks, utility benchmarks, and video jailbreak tests.
AI.result: They find that textual refusal directions do generalize to multimodal inputs but are sensitive to how and where steering is applied; naive steering can cause safe multimodal inputs to be incorrectly refused. With MARS, they obtain consistent improvements in safety metrics on five SOTA MLLMs, including robustness against video jailbreak attacks, while maintaining the models' helpfulness/utility on benign tasks.
AI.conclusion: Safety-relevant internal structure in LLMs is shared across modalities, meaning that refusal behaviors learned from text can be directly leveraged for multimodal safety. MARS operationalizes this insight as a simple, training-free activation-steering method that injects multimodal safety without requiring multimodal safety data, improving safety and robustness while preserving utility.

title: TreeAgent: A Generalizable Multi-Agent Framework for Automated Bias Labeling in Forestry via Compiled Expert Rules and Vision-Language Models
summary: Human-labeled data are widely used as reference annotations in ML, despite known variability across annotators in many expert-driven domains. In addition, expert annotation is slow, inconsistent, and remains a major bottleneck for scaling tasks like tree height bias classification in forestry remote sensing. We propose a multi-agent system (MAS) that orchestrates expert decision trees with Vision-Language Models (VLMs), treating the decision tree as a structural prior while VLMs perform localized semantic perception at individual nodes, with multi-agent voting to mitigate VLM stochasticity. We formalize a Decoupled Declarative Decision (D3) Framework that enables zero-modification generalization across diverse expert-defined decision structures. On a tree bias classification testbed, our framework outperforms supervised ML baselines and reduces the amount of expert labeling effort required. These results suggest that agentic orchestration of VLMs with expert priors can reproduce expert-defined labeling procedures at substantially lower annotation cost while maintaining interpretability.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a framework that combines expert decision trees with vision-language models (VLMs) to automate expert-style labeling with less human effort.
AI.motivation: Human expert labeling is slow, inconsistent, and expensive, especially in domains like forestry remote sensing where tasks such as tree height bias classification require specialized knowledge. Despite variability, these labels are used as ground truth in ML. The authors want a way to reproduce expert labeling procedures more efficiently, at scale, and with interpretability preserved.
AI.method: They design a multi-agent system (MAS) that coordinates multiple VLM agents according to an expert-defined decision tree. The decision tree serves as a structural prior specifying the decision logic, while the VLMs perform localized semantic perception at each node. Multiple VLM agents vote to reduce stochasticity. They formalize this as a Decoupled Declarative Decision (D3) Framework that can be applied without modifying different expert decision structures (zero-modification generalization).
AI.result: On a tree height bias classification benchmark in forestry remote sensing, their D3 framework achieves better performance than supervised ML baselines and simultaneously lowers the amount of expert labeling required.
AI.conclusion: Orchestrating VLM agents with expert decision tree priors can effectively mimic expert labeling workflows, maintaining interpretability while significantly reducing annotation costs and improving performance over standard supervised approaches.

title: Self-Study Reconsidered: The Hidden Fragility of Learning from Self-Generated QA
summary: Language models are increasingly taught from synthetic question--answer (QA) supervision: a model generates questions about a document, answers them from the same text, and the resulting pairs are used to fine-tune, distill, or compress knowledge into another model. We show that this generation step is not neutral preprocessing. It is an implicit policy that both selects which evidence becomes training signal and decides how that evidence is answered, and it is fragile at both stages. When choosing what to ask, generators do not scan a document uniformly. Coverage saturates early and concentrates on salient spans, diverse prompts converge on the same regions, and what looks question-worthy is driven by local presentation. As a result, salient artifacts such as poorly cleaned markup can hijack question generation across model families and scales. When answering, the model that produces the supervision tends to obey instruction-like passages embedded in the text. This compliance depends on the intent and surface form of the passage rather than its strictness, and is worst under task conflict, where larger models comply more often. These failure modes arise from choices made during QA generation, so they can be reduced without changing the training loop. Tying each question to a fixed target reduces biased selection, and filtering instruction-like spans before answering lowers mean injection compliance from $88\%$ to $13\%$ in our evaluation while retaining nearly all clean text.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes failure modes in synthetic question–answer (QA) supervision for language models and shows that question generation is a biased, fragile policy that can be improved with simple fixes.
AI.motivation: Synthetic QA supervision is widely used to train and compress language models, but is often treated as neutral preprocessing. The authors suspect that the question-generation step itself shapes what information becomes supervision and may introduce systematic biases or vulnerabilities (e.g., to artifacts or prompt injections). Understanding these effects is crucial for reliable data generation and downstream model behavior.
AI.method: The authors empirically study how QA generators choose what to ask about and how they answer, across models, prompts, and documents. They measure document coverage, concentration on salient spans, convergence across diverse prompts, and sensitivity to local presentation and artifacts like markup. For answering, they insert instruction-like passages into texts and measure how often the generator obeys them under varying intent, surface form, and task conflict, including across model scales. They then test mitigation strategies such as tying questions to fixed targets and filtering instruction-like spans before answering.
AI.result: They find that generators do not uniformly explore documents: coverage saturates quickly, questions cluster on salient spans, and varied prompts still converge on the same regions. Superficial presentation—especially artifacts like poorly cleaned markup—can dominate what is asked about, affecting multiple model families and sizes. In answering, generators frequently comply with instruction-like text embedded in documents, with compliance driven more by intent and surface form than by strictness, and it increases under task conflict and with larger models. Proposed mitigations reduce these issues: binding questions to fixed targets improves selection fairness, and filtering instruction-like content before answering reduces injection compliance from 88% to 13% while preserving most clean text.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic QA generation is not a neutral preprocessing step but an implicit and fragile policy that shapes supervision. However, its main failure modes—biased question selection and susceptibility to injected instructions—can be substantially mitigated without altering the training loop, via better question-target tying and simple filtering of instruction-like spans.

title: PolicyGuard: From Organizational Policies to Neuro-SymbolicCompliance Review Engines
summary: Policy-grounded document review requires determining whether a target document complies with organization-specific policies, guidelines, or playbooks. While large language models can assist with policy interpretation and document analysis, end-to-end prompting leaves the applied policy logic implicit, making compliance decisions difficult to inspect, update, and test. We present PolicyGuard, a neuro-symbolic framework for policy-grounded document compliance review. PolicyGuard converts organizational policy guidance into an executable review engine consisting of typed relational logic rules and atom-level extraction questions. During review, LLMs answer these local questions using retrieved document evidence, and a symbolic evaluator applies the formal rules to detect non-compliance. We instantiate and evaluate PolicyGuard on company-specific NDA compliance review, where contract clauses must be checked against organization-specific negotiation policies. By separating policy formalization, local document interpretation, and symbolic compliance evaluation, PolicyGuard makes document review more explicit, maintainable, and systematically testable.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PolicyGuard, a neuro-symbolic framework that turns organizational policies into executable logic rules and question-answering steps so LLMs can perform transparent, testable document compliance review, demonstrated on NDA contract checking.
AI.motivation: End-to-end LLM prompting for policy compliance leaves the underlying policy logic implicit and opaque, making it hard to inspect, update, and test how decisions are made for organization-specific document reviews. Organizations need a way to operationalize detailed internal policies in a form that is transparent, maintainable, and systematically verifiable while still leveraging LLMs for complex language understanding.
AI.method: The authors design PolicyGuard, which first formalizes organizational policy guidance as typed relational logic rules and fine-grained extraction questions. During review, LLMs are used only to answer these localized questions based on retrieved evidence from the document. A symbolic reasoning engine then applies the formal rules to the LLM-derived facts to infer compliance or non-compliance. The system is instantiated on company-specific NDA review, where individual contract clauses are mapped to negotiation policies and checked via the rule engine.
AI.result: When applied to NDA compliance review, PolicyGuard can systematically detect non-compliant clauses by combining LLM-based local interpretations with symbolic rule evaluation, yielding more interpretable and controllable decisions compared to monolithic end-to-end prompting. The framework demonstrates that organizational policies can be operationalized as explicit logical structures that drive the review process.
AI.conclusion: PolicyGuard shows that separating policy formalization, local document interpretation, and symbolic compliance evaluation enables more explicit, maintainable, and testable policy-grounded document review. The neuro-symbolic approach allows organizations to update and audit their policy logic independently of the LLM, improving transparency and robustness in compliance checking tasks like NDA review.

title: AxDafny: Agentic Verified Code Generation in Dafny
summary: We study agentic code generation in Dafny, where a model must generate both executable code and the proof artifacts for verification. We present AxDafny, a verifier-guided repair framework that iteratively generates implementations, invariants, assertions, and termination arguments. We also introduce LiveCodeBench-Pro-Dafny (LCB-Pro-Dafny), a benchmark of 250 competition-style programming problems translated into Dafny with formal specifications and a verifier-based evaluation harness. On LCB-Pro-Dafny, AxDafny substantially improves verification success over baseline GPT-5.5 performance. On DafnyBench, AxDafny achieves 92.7\% verification success, outperforming the strongest previously reported proof-hint baseline by 6.5 percentage points. Lastly, we show that verification success and runtime test performance measure different aspects of generated code.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AxDafny, an automatic, verifier-guided system that generates and repairs Dafny programs along with their proofs, and evaluates it on a new benchmark, showing large gains in verification success over strong baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing code-generation models struggle in formal verification settings like Dafny, where code must be both correct and accompanied by proofs. There is a need for systems and benchmarks that focus on agentic, verifier-in-the-loop generation of implementations and proof artifacts, and for understanding how verification success relates to traditional test-based evaluation.
AI.method: They design AxDafny, a framework that interacts iteratively with the Dafny verifier to generate and repair not only implementations but also invariants, assertions, and termination arguments. They also build LiveCodeBench-Pro-Dafny (LCB-Pro-Dafny), a suite of 250 competition-style problems translated into Dafny, with specifications and an automatic verifier-based harness for evaluation. AxDafny’s performance is compared against a strong GPT-5.5 baseline and prior proof-hint approaches on LCB-Pro-Dafny and DafnyBench.
AI.result: On the new LCB-Pro-Dafny benchmark, AxDafny significantly increases the fraction of problems whose solutions fully verify compared to GPT-5.5. On the existing DafnyBench benchmark, AxDafny attains 92.7% verification success, which is a 6.5 percentage point improvement over the best previous proof-hint baseline. They also empirically demonstrate that passing formal verification and passing runtime tests capture distinct facets of code quality and are not interchangeable metrics.
AI.conclusion: Verifier-guided, agentic code generation that jointly produces implementations and proof artifacts can substantially improve formal verification success in Dafny compared to strong language-model baselines and earlier proof-hint methods. The new LCB-Pro-Dafny benchmark facilitates systematic evaluation of such systems, and the observed divergence between verification success and test performance highlights the need to consider both when assessing generated code.
